responseSet.title: Epistemology: Chaired by María Isabel Siquier
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Epistemology

Chaired by María Isabel Siquier

Theodore Jacobs

Theodore Jacobs

170 East 77th Street (1G)
New York
NY 10021

Theodore Jacobs  

Ms Siquier opened the panel by noting the importance of the topic to be discussed. Today, she said, there is a growing interest in questions of epistemology and in interdisciplinary studies so that the present panel is quite relevant to current concerns in our field.

Dr Patricia Kitcher then presented her paper, ‘A defence of Freud's metapsychology’. Her effort in this paper, she stated, was to save at least some of Freud from the Freudians, and to defend metapsychology against analysts who view it as irrelevant and unnecessary. In addition, she sought to defend metapsychology ‘against epistemologists who regard it as unsound, as unempirical’.

Emphasising the fact that for Freud, metapsychology was a research ideal and that he believed that a comprehensive understanding of mental phenomena required describing a psychological process in ‘its dynamic, topographical and economic aspects’ (Freud, 1915, p. 181), Dr Kitcher divided her defence into three sections, corresponding to the three aspects of metapsychology.

Freud's basic aim in creating psychoanalysis, Kitcher pointed out, was to explain behaviour produced by unconscious motivation. He extended the range of ordinary psychological explanation, she added, ‘by extending the range of desires and beliefs that could rationalise the actions they caused’. In addition, he altered psychological explanation by proposing that the factors that make actions intelligible were themselves produced or transformed by previously unrecognised forces that acted upon them. The introduction of these forces allowed Freud to relate psychoanalytic explanation to ordinary psychological explanation because these forces explained how such odd beliefs and desires were produced from the more familiar stock of human ideas.

In Kitcher's view, ‘Freud intended the dynamic part of psychoanalysis to include descriptions of the unobserved, proximate cause of behaviour—ideas including beliefs and desires—and the forces that act on them’. Thus, she said, the dynamic approach is the least theoretical and most clinically relevant part of metapsychology. ‘It can be dismissed’, she added, ‘only at the price of giving up any attempt to explain the causes and cures of neurotic illness in terms of unconscious ideas and the forces that operate on them’.

responseSet.title: Intricate Engagements: The Collaborative Basis of Therapeutic Change.  By Steven A. Frankel.: Northvale, NJ and London: Jason Aronson. 1995. Pp. 262
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Intricate Engagements: The Collaborative Basis of Therapeutic Change. By Steven A. Frankel.

Northvale, NJ and London: Jason Aronson. 1995. Pp. 262

Jean Roiphe

Jean Roiphe

40 East 83rd Street, New York, NY 10028

Jean Roiphe  

Frankel's goals are important and ambitious ones. He starts with a clinical dilemma: a patient whose situation he feels he ‘understood perfectly’ from the framework of his analytic theory, yet who is not helped by his interpretations. To come to grips with this dilemma and the questions it raises for him, Frankel decides that he must find a way to learn other theories and take them seriously, and find methods for evaluating what does and does not work and produce therapeutic change within psychoanalysis and psychoanalytic psychotherapy. He raises the familiar and troubling problem ‘of how few guidelines actually exist within the field for determining what works’ (p. xv). The book is presented as a description of the author's project to address this problem.

The methodology Frankel proposes for the project is a review of the major psychoanalytic theories in search of common psychological principles and guidelines for interventions, a review of the literature and research on child development with similar goals, and a systematic review of his own clinical data to discover what factors advance the clinical process and lead to therapeutic change. The author takes note of the formidable problems inherent in using analytic data in a way that allows us to test hypotheses and, in the preface, describes his own method in some detail. This consists of taking detailed process notes within the analytic hour, writing down everything he is aware of about the patient and himself, regardless of whether he thinks it significant at the time. (This, of course, cannot entirely circumvent the problem of selection, as we will filter out of our perceptions and awareness altogether much of what we truly do not think is significant.)

responseSet.title: ‘Heresy’
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

‘Heresy’

Mary O'Neil

Mary O'Neil

1515 rue Dr Penfield #1005
Montreal
Quebec
H3G 2R8

Mary O'Neil  

Dear Sir,

I feel impelled to comment on Nathan M. Kravis's review (Int. J. Psychoanal., 78: 196-199) of Heresy: Sándor Rado and the Psychoanalytic Movement by Paul Roazen and Bluma Swerdloff. Sándor Rado was indeed an important yet relatively neglected figure in the history of psychoanalysis. Potential readers might be dissuaded by this review from learning more about him and this would be unfortunate.

In the course of preparing a book about Ruth Easser, one of Sándor Rado's first Columbia graduates, I interviewed over thirty psychoanalysts who were Rado's colleagues and students. I came to know something about how those who knew or were influenced by him at Columbia regarded this man. Perhaps, as a result, my reading of this small piece of psychoanalytic history differs greatly from that of Nathan Kravis. First it seems important to be clear about what this book is and what it is not. Swerdloff in her introduction comments that ‘this … is not a biography of Rado, nor an exposition of his significant contributions to psychoanalytic theory and practice. It is also not a carefully researched tract’ (p. viii). Kravis appears to have over read this slim volume expecting, perhaps, an objective biography or a systematic exposition of Rado's theories. I read this book as a thoughtful and evenly balanced annotated oral history—the subjective, highly personal story of a psychoanalyst whose significant contributions have been relatively unrecognized and are now only beginning to be reappraised (e.g. C. Tomlinson ‘Sándor Rado and Adolf Meyer: a nodal point in American psychiatry and psychoanalysis’. Int. J. Psychoanal., 77: 963-982). It is this aspect that Kravis's review fails to convey and therefore can be misleading in its emphasis.

To object to ‘Heresy’ in the title as metaphorical or ironical and to the statement that ‘Rado has been “effectively written out of psychoanalytic history’” as a ‘bizarre act’ misrepresents two realities. There can be little doubt that, at least beginning in his later years at the New York Psychoanalytic, Rado was indeed heretical with regard to both the then orthodox psychoanalytic theory and organization and he has been all but written out of psychoanalytic history; this is simply a statement of fact. There is much more known and written about Rado's contemporaries (e.g. other ‘heretics’ Sándor Ferenczi and Otto Rank and the dissidents, Karen Horney and Clara Thompson). Prior to this book and Tomlinson's article relatively little about Rado was to be found in the psychoanalytic literature. Roazen and Swerdloff identify several factors that contributed to his ostracization. Kravis acknowledges these at the end of his review but, in my opinion, with the wrong slant. Like Glover and others he continues to blame Rado for his personality flaws—‘reckless insistence on his own brand of dogma’, and ‘partly in response to his own bilious outbursts’, implying that he deserved to be banished. ‘Heresy’ on the other hand, acknowledges these realities without blame and provides (albeit from Rado's point of view) useful insight into the interactions between Rado and his colleagues and enemies at this important era in psychoanalytic history.

responseSet.title: ‘Preschoolers Face Moral Dilemmas’
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

‘Preschoolers Face Moral Dilemmas’

Mercedes Valcarce

Mercedes Valcarce

Madrid
28223

Mercedes Valcarce  

Dear Sir,

The paper ‘Preschoolers face moral dilemmas …’ issued in the recent IJPA (Vol. 78, Part 5) has attracted my attention because it deals with a very well known subject studied in Cognitive Psychology from whose frame of reference most of my colleagues at the Madrid University are working.

In my opinion, to deal from the psychoanalytical point of view with a field so much studied by cognitive psychologists, truly original conclusions should be contributed; but this is not the case in that paper, in my opinion.

Since the first of Piaget's studies (1932) there have been many works on the subject by such psychologists (Colby, 1983; Damon, 1998; Kagan & Lamb, 1987).

It seems to me that the paper only repeats conclusions that are quite old and well known, whereas the really analytical items—such as the role of one's earliest experiences, leading to the resolution of dilemmas—are not approached.

As regards the differences that are found in the resolution of dilemmas, it seems to me very weak and quite evident, concluding that ‘the ability to acknowledge dilemmas and resolve them improved with age’ (of course!).

602

Response

Robert Emde MD

Robert Emde

Campus
Box C268-69
4200 East Ninth Avenue
Denver
Colorado 80262

Robert Emde  

Dear Sir,

It is good the letter-writer is reading empirical reports in The Journal and is interested in integrations with cognitive psychology. Two aspects of the cited report, however, are not appreciated and can be pointed out in this response. The first has to do with developmental significance and the second with psychoanalytic relevance.

The developmental significance of the work is that the capacity of the preschooler to struggle with and, in some instances, resolve moral dilemmas—as well as individual differences in this capacity—were not known to cognitive psychology. Indeed, I served as editor of the classic monograph cited by the letter-writer of Colby, Kohlberg, Gibbs & Leiderman (1983) which provided the longitudinal data on moral dilemmas that summarized the accumulated and operationalized thinking resulting from previous works of Piaget and Kohlberg himself. The earliest age assessed and reported in that monograph was ten years, an age that served as the beginning point for a twenty-year developmental study. (I also remember discussions of reviewers at that time who wondered if a child as young as seven years of age could deal in any way with moral dilemmas.) The report in The Journal uses a different method than the Colby et al. technique, namely, a play narrative method designed to elicit cognitive and affective information in much younger children. The report provides new information that includes the following: 1)

responseSet.title: The Talking Cures.  The Psychoanalyses and the Psychotherapies.  By Robert S. Wallerstein.: New Haven and London: Yale University Press.  1995.  Pp. 587 + xx.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Talking Cures. The Psychoanalyses and the Psychotherapies. By Robert S. Wallerstein.

New Haven and London: Yale University Press. 1995. Pp. 587 + xx.

Lucy Zabarenko

Lucy Zabarenko

1111 Gilchrest Dr., Pittsburgh, PA 15235-5224

Lucy Zabarenko  

Being asked to review a book by Robert Wallerstein is like a request to comment on altruism in the good fairy of the North or aggression in Attila the Hun. It evokes questions about the editor's motivations. Does he harbour a conscious or unconscious hostility for the reviewer? Has he searched for a colleague he hopes will speak the unvarnished truth? Is he looking for something sensational or just different?

The reason for these musings is, of course, that the author is as close as we come to an icon in contemporary psychoanalysis. An amalgam of Samuel Johnson, Ben Franklin and Ludwig von Kochel, Robert Wallerstein has been our discipline's dogged biographer, its consummate diplomatic emissary, a hard-working politician and tireless cataloguer of its scientific and professional balance sheets. In The Talking Cures he endows us with a saga of his ideational and conceptual journeys, an enterprise of prodigious scope. The volume is exhaustive enough to be an encyclopaedia, clearly organised enough to put most texts to shame and replete with such thorough summaries of currents of thought that most historians would be envious.

This much said and the impossible mission of a review accepted, readers are entitled to one which applies the criteria incumbent on all literary products: does the book do what the author had in mind? What might it be used for and how? Is it fun to read and under what circumstances? And how well does it shoulder its way through the usual crowd of practicalities such as: will it fit in a briefcase, is it readily parsed and does the level of discourse make it accessible to a wide range of professionals without boring analytic practitioners and teachers?

There's little doubt about the first of these criteria. After living with the book for five months, it's plain to me that Wallerstein has succeeded in summarising, recapitulating and coalescing developments in the talking cures since the turning points in his life in the early 1950s. His scholarly sovereignty in these areas is unquestionable and commendably thorough, exactly what readers have come to expect.

responseSet.title: Vyötä kupeesi kuin mies [Gird your loins like a man].  By Vesa Manninen.: Jyväskylä: Kirjayhtymä Oy.  1996.  Pp. 160
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Vyötä kupeesi kuin mies [Gird your loins like a man]. By Vesa Manninen.

Jyväskylä: Kirjayhtymä Oy. 1996. Pp. 160

Turo Reenkola

Turo Reenkola

Ritokalliontie 8-16, 00330 Helsinki 33

Turo Reenkola  

The central theme in the book is the male struggle to reach and maintain an integrated self identity, to be an I. According to Freud's well-known definition the ability to love and work are the most important characteristics of a healthy adult identity. Male destinies, as Manninen describes them, show the mutually dependent relationship between these two capacities. He maintains that self-identity is based on the feeling of self-determination. Following Freud he asserts that our ego is formed when we are trying to cope with those three independent realities: one's own body, external nature and the fact of other peoples' individual psychic existence. These powers are constantly threatening our identity, our feeling of self-determination.

Submitting to our imperfections on all these fronts is the only permanently progressive solution. It emancipates the individual to relative autonomy and individual responsibility and makes possible the recognition of one's own neediness. This is the basis on which the striving for good contact with reality can be built (p. 53).

Manninen focuses on the conditions that are necessary for the ego to give up the striving for omnipotence and to be satisfied with what people and nature give us, when their independent existence is acknowledged.

Copyright © Institute of Psycho-Analysis, London, 1998

[[RunningHead]]

La Tecnica nella Psicoanalisi Infantile [Technique in child analysis]. By A. Ferro.

Milan: Raffaello Cortina Editore. 1992. Pp. 221

Gigliola Spoto

Gigliola Spoto

101 Camberwell Grove, London SE5 8JH

Gigliola Spoto  

Although Ferro writes about problems of technique in child analysis and all the clinical material in the book comes from analysis of children or adolescents, he clearly doesn't consider child analysis to have a ‘particular specificity’, or as different in any substantive way from adult analysis, despite the use of different clinical settings and expressive modes: the mind of the analyst functions in the same way with both adults and children, and Ferro is particularly interested in the way in which the analyst's mind functions. Even if his ideas are solidly rooted in his rich clinical and impressive experience as a child analyst, his book aims to make a more general contribution both to psychoanalytical theory and technique, and to the understanding of the general nature and function of the relationship between analyst and patient in any kind of psychoanalytical encounter. This makes it a book aimed at all practitioners of psychoanalysis and not just to those of us working as child analysts.

Ferro begins by outlining his theoretical model.

responseSet.title: Conscious and Unconscious Processes: Psychodynamic, Cognitive, and Neurophysiological Convergences.  By Howard Shevrin, James A. Bond, Linda A. W. Brakel, Richard K. Hertel and William J. Williams.: New York: The Guilford Press. 1996. Pp. 302
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Conscious and Unconscious Processes: Psychodynamic, Cognitive, and Neurophysiological Convergences. By Howard Shevrin, James A. Bond, Linda A. W. Brakel, Richard K. Hertel and William J. Williams.

New York: The Guilford Press. 1996. Pp. 302

Susan Vaughan

Susan Vaughan

25 West 81st Street, #1C New York, NY 10024

Susan Vaughan  

Clinicians who criticise psychodynamic research efforts often cite lack of relevance to the analytic endeavour and the loss of clinical richness that research necessitates as the basis for their criticisms. Analysts engaged in conducting psychoanalytic research know the challenges of developing rigorous methodology and defining what constitutes acceptable data when studying the complex process of psychoanalysis. There can be no doubt that the research presented in this volume is relevant to psychoanalysis. It provides a model example of a methodologically rigorous project that nonetheless retains the richness of the clinical endeavour. Clinicians and researchers alike will be happy to see the clear example of intriguing and important psychoanalytic research reported in Conscious and Unconscious Processes: Psychodynamic, Cognitive and Neurophysiological Convergences by Howard Shevrin et al. This book summarises the theoretical underpinnings, research methods, and findings of Shevrin and his colleagues' groundbreaking and innovative work over nearly forty years as they have attempted to provide objective evidence for the role of unconscious conflict in psychopathology.

The essence of the work described is as follows: patients with phobias (and others with pathological grief reactions, though these patients are not discussed in the book) were extensively interviewed using unstructured, exploratory psychodynamic interviews, psychological tests and psychiatric interviews to clarify DSM diagnosis. Transcripts of these interviews and test results were reviewed by judges who, through a complex series of steps, arrived at a psychodynamic formulation about each subject's core unconscious conflicts as they related to the presenting symptoms. The judges used their formulations to generate and hone a list of words that they believed closely related to the conscious symptoms of each patient as well as a list that related to that patient's unconscious conflicts. The lists were constructed so that the conscious symptom words and the unconscious conflict words did not overlap. In addition, ‘ordinary pleasant’ words and ‘ordinary unpleasant’ words drawn from Osgood's research on connotive meanings were chosen as controls. The pathological and control words were written on cards and presented visually to the subjects, first subliminally (for a time period too brief to allow conscious detection of the stimulus) then supraliminally (for a time period which was long enough to permit conscious detection) using a tachistoscope. Event-related potentials (ERPs) were recorded from electrodes placed on the scalps of the subjects during each of these tachistoscopic presentations.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Mass Sexuality/Private Sexuality

Chaired by Valentin Barenblit, Barcelona

Joseph Reppen

Joseph Reppen

211 East 70th Street
New York
NY 10021

Joseph Reppen  

The moderator, Valentin Barenblit, introduced the panel before a large and receptive audience, noting that the papers present an important interaction between psychoanalysis and other disciplines that stress social and cultural phenomena.

The paper by Elizabeth Tabak de Bianchedi, Marcelo Bianchedi, Julia Braun, Maria L. Pelento & Janine Puget, ‘Mega-offer: extension or restriction of sexuality’, was the first presentation. They noted that there have been changes in what is generally thought of as public or private sexuality and that today we face problems that relate to the inadequacy of our theories for understanding new clinical situations. Sexual intercourse has become public and one can observe sexual practices, which were done clandestinely in the past. Much can be ‘consumed’ sexually today.

The authors call these new sexual opportunities ‘mega-offer’, a term inspired by Amati Sas's use of the term ‘mega-deaths’. Sexual mega-offer now characterises our ‘liberal’ society, resulting in choices made on the basis of consumerism and the wish to belong rather than free choice. When the mega-offer intrudes into privacy it produces alienation.

The primal scene also takes on a different meaning as a result of cultural and social forces. The primal scene has different transformations. In the case of the Wolf Man what is highlighted is the sadistic and anal components, while in the oedipal period there is an emphasis on phallic/castration. More recently the primal scene has changed, diluting its violent character.

There is greater complexity in our understanding of the primal scene in post-Freudian theorists. Melanie Klein's concept of unconscious phantasy and object-representations that exist from the beginning of life emphasise a more dramatic aspect of the primal scene, emphasising feelings rather than aggression. Donald Meltzer included more characters in the primal scene, not only two as Freud conjectured or three as Klein thought, but five or six characters interacting. Berenstein and Puget make the primal scene even more complex in that they include the social context, something that neither Freud, Klein, nor Meltzer had in mind.

responseSet.title: Film Reviews Guest Editorial
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Film Reviews Guest Editorial

David Werman

David Werman

1503 Michaux Rd.
Chapel Hill
NC 27514

David Werman  

Dear Sir,

In his guest editorial announcing the inauguration of film reviews in the International Journal of Psycho-Analysis, Glen Gabbard (1997, 78: 429-434) outlines several ‘psychoanalytic approaches’ to the interpretation of film. Absent from these approaches is the experiential world of the spectator. This omission is hardly astonishing because it is unusual in psychoanalytic studies of art for the writer to describe and analyze his/her emotions; instead, the film, or other work of art, seems to be regarded as a Rosetta stone to be deciphered. To be sure, there are exceptions, but they are infrequent. At the end of his Editorial, Gabbard does state that ‘audiences do not attend films merely to be entertained. They queue up at the local multiplex to encounter long-forgotten but still powerful memories that stem from universal developmental experiences. By confronting them at a distance in a darkened cinema, they have the opportunity to master these anxieties vicariously and leave enriched and relieved’. This observation may come as a surprise to those who have braved that ‘local multiplex’ and have heard some members of the audience weeping while others laughed and still others slept soundly. Apart from the questionable universality of this view, it does not suggest that the critic's reaction to the film may be relevant to an understanding of it.

Jacob Arlow's review of Bergman's Wild Strawberries, appearing in the same issue of the Journal (Arlow, 1997. The end of time: a psychoanalytic perspective on Ingmar Bergman's Wild Strawberries. Int. J. Psychoanal., 78: 595-599) is most apposite in this regard. He sensitively and carefully describes the sequences of the film, offering interpretations of many of them. He interprets Dr Borg's dreams, explicates the possible meanings of the characters' names, identifies identification with the aggressor, the primal scene, oedipal and pre-oedipal conflicts, etc. Only at the end of his contribution does Arlow mention affects, and these he ascribes to Dr Borg, who ‘mixes nostalgia and regret’ over the life he might have had. Here again, we see the psychoanalyst observing the film at very long arm's length.

Franca Meotti

Franca Meotti

via Stefini 14, 20125 Milan

Franca Meotti  

The sub-title of this volume edited by Anna Nicolò Corigliano, ‘Essays on psychoanalysis and the couple’ (the main title means ‘treating the relationship’), indicates that its subject matter occupies an intermediate space defined in accordance with a theoretical position that has absorbed the teachings of Balint, Bion, Winnicott and, to some extent, Baranger & Baranger. Attention focuses on the concept of the relationship—between subject and object, between therapist and patient, between the individuals making up the couple, between the co-therapists, between the members of the family and—also—between psychoanalytic theory and its application to couple therapy.

(Translated by Philip Slotkin, MA, MITI)

responseSet.title: Psychoanalysis at the Political Border.  Essays in Honor of Rafael Moses.  Edited by Leo Rangell and Rena Moses-Hrushovski.: Madison, Connecticut: International Universities Press.  1996. Pp. 330.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalysis at the Political Border. Essays in Honor of Rafael Moses. Edited by Leo Rangell and Rena Moses-Hrushovski.

Madison, Connecticut: International Universities Press. 1996. Pp. 330.

Her Bert Gomberg

Her Bert Gomberg

8226 Douglas Avenue, Suite 430, Dallas, TX 75225

Her Bert Gomberg  

This book of essays, seventeen in all, is a fest-schrift in honour of Rafael Moses, the Israeli psychoanalyst, on his seventieth birthday. It is a diverse group of essays by distinguished contributors from Britain, Canada, Germany, Israel and the United States. The book, reflecting Moses's wide interests in individual and group psychology and how they extend into psychopolitical processes, is divided into four parts: firstly, two appreciative essays of the man and his work; secondly, essays concerning individual psychology; thirdly, essays concerning group and interpersonal psychological issues; and finally, essays emphasising psychoanalytic understanding of group political processes.

As the range of subject matter is wide, the theoretical approach is varied, and the attempts to bring psychoanalytic concepts to bear on a wide expanse of human experience, effort and action has varied results in the hands of these accomplished and serious contributors, no summary of the book as a whole is possible. Broadly speaking, it deals with issues of love and hate, how these imbue human experience, in particular interpersonal, group and political experience, and how psychoanalytic concepts may be brought to bear on such processes. The underlying hope of the book appears to be that increased understanding could lead to more effective and less destructive ways of dealing with group and political conflict.

A brief glance at the contents will indicate the diversity of the subject and approach of the essays. The articles in the section, ‘Within the individual’, include: Sandler & Sandler's useful discussion of the development of the psycho analytic conceptualisation of love, from energic concepts through object-relations theory, object- and self-representations, role-responsiveness, projective and introjective mechanisms and the capacity for empathy. This is followed by Harold Blum's article on aggression, violence and hatred and the ways in which these may be stimulated and transmitted. Yecheskiel Cohen delineates factors in development involving merger and fusion, separation, and achievement of self-identity that lead to the capacity for, or fear of, love. In contrast is Arnold Richards's highly theoretical but sophisticated discussion of the classical psychoanalytic theory of motivation and current controversies surrounding it, an interesting discussion and critical evaluation of several current competing paradigms.

responseSet.title: The Psychoanalytic Interpretation of Sexuality: Chaired by Esther Mateo, Caracas
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Interpretation of Sexuality

Chaired by Esther Mateo, Caracas

H. Perdigão

H. Perdigão

1424 Amelia Street
New Orleans
LA 70115-3618

H. Perdigão  

The Chairman introduced the panellists and commented that the theme of the panel was of central importance to psychoanalysis.

The first panellist, Joan Coderch, emphasised two points, which he illustrated with clinical material. First, the sexuality that the analyst should interpret is that which forms part of the mental structure of the patient, that manifests itself in the transference relationship and not that which expresses itself in the daily life of the patient. Second, that it is necessary to distinguish between (a) the sexuality that manifests itself within the framework of the transference, and (b) the sexuality that is at the service of a perverse erotisation of the transference.

He presented two clinical vignettes. In the first case, that of a homosexual man, Coderch discussed two of the patient's dreams to illustrate his point. The first dream expressed the fantasy that the analyst would change him into a person who would have available to him all the fertility, creativity and life, which he imagined existed inside the analyst. This was the image of the mother's inside, with which he hoped to make the feelings of nostalgia, abandonment and sadness that tormented him disappear. The second dream took place just before a weekend break, which was felt as a rejection. Facing this frustration the patient fell back on perverse sexuality with which he hoped to excite the analyst and awaken in him a desire for the patient.

Coderch emphasised that these two distinct aspects of sexuality must be interpreted in different ways. In the first dream, the love, the wish to be loved, have to be interpreted as such. In the second dream, the sexuality should be interpreted as being put in the service of eliminating his dependency on the analyst and the aggressive intent of provoking excitation in the analyst in order to disturb his mental functions. This would make the analyst more accessible and permit the patient to have him at his beck and call, a captive dominated by the patient's sexual attractiveness.

responseSet.title: Biology and Sexuality: Chaired by Alex Kacelnik, Oxford
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Biology and Sexuality

Chaired by Alex Kacelnik, Oxford

Jorge Olagaray

Jorge Olagaray

San Martin 1035, P15 Dptro. 72
5500 Mendoza
Argentina
jolagara@satlink.com

Jorge Olagaray  

This was one of the two interdisciplinary panels at the Congress. It included a discussant, Jacques Hochmann, the only psychoanalyst among the panellists, and was chaired by Alex Kacelnik, an ethologist and evolutionary biologist from Oxford University. The panel started with the biologists giving their views, later to be confronted from the psychoanalytic standpoint. With his presentation on ‘The problems’, the Chairman introduced the panel's scope—the philosophical and epistemological implications of the subject; life, death, reproduction and evolution; mind/body and, mainly, sex—with lively humour. He characterised the biologists as outsiders, with respect to psychoanalysis, and politely forewarned the audience that the arguments they were going to hear could be considered unconventional or provocative for analysts. He summarised the prejudices that affect the interaction between biologists and psychoanalysts. They mistrust psychoanalysis, as it is very difficult to test its hypotheses. And psychoanalysts underestimate the sophistication of the contemporary thinking on evolution and consider it to be ‘reductionist’.

Kacelnik then asked about the adequacy of Darwin's theory of natural selection for studying the design of the mind and sexuality. After a lively review and discussion of different opinions, he gave a possible explanation of people's reluctance to apply evolutionary theory to the human mind, in terms of the narcissistic injuries involved. He then approached sexuality from anisogamy, the asymmetry about the size and resources of each sex's gametes, with varied consequences. For instance, females evolve to be choosier about their partners, while males evolve to be more promiscuous. Animals or people do not ‘think’ of evolution, they just ‘like’ what they do, as a result of evolution.

He illustrated this with examples of the fact that not all animals just have straight reproductive sex, and showed links between sex, violence and death. Some conclusions, applicable to humans, could be that human infanticide is remarkably similar to that in lions and mice; female sexual desire is timed in relation to fertility, and social factors explain part of sexual behaviour, but the same is true in many animals, as the evolution of sexual pleasure takes over other forms of social behaviour.

Valencia: Promolibro. 1996. Pp. 329

Neri Daurella

Neri Daurella

Deu i Mata 152, 08029 Barcelona

Neri Daurella  

The book under review is a compendium of fifteen theoretical and clinical papers written by the distinguished Argentinian psychoanalyst León Grinberg during the almost twenty years since his emigration to Spain. The title reflects the author's interpersonal perspective: on the basis of an indepth study of the thought of Freud, Klein, Bion and others, coupled with many years of clinical experience, he has concluded that the activity of psychoanalysis is a dynamic, living interchange between two people.

(Translated by Philip Slotkin, MA, MITI.)

responseSet.title: Response
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Response

Jean-Michel Quinodoz

Jean-Michel Quinodoz

53 A chemin des Fourches
CH 1223 Cologny (Geneva)
February 1998

Jean-Michel Quinodoz  

Dear Sir,

Very many thanks to Dr von der Tann for his attentive reading and his comments. I do agree with him on certain points although I seem to have sometimes been taken too literally. For example, regarding the fractal objects, I share the same opinion that the notion of geometrical

1006

objects cannot be applied as such to the unconscious fantasy. I believe that the regular structures we observe as psychoanalysts in the unconscious fantasies at different scales, like the oedipal configuration, can be compared to the phenomena of ‘self similarity’ described in the complex systems. However this constitutes an analogous model of functioning, and not a direct transposition of the same way of functioning of one field to another.

I am grateful to Dr von der Tann for having raised the question of the methodological status of such parallels we are tempted to draw between certain aspects of the psychic functioning as observed in psychoanalysis and recent developments of deterministic chaos theory: is it about mathematical models? About metaphors? About analogous models? I also agree with Dr von der Tann's suggestion that the creative process, among others, needs further investigation and I hope he will enlighten us with some of his own research.

Dr Jean-Michel Quinodoz
53 A chemin des Fourches
CH 1223 Cologny (Geneva)
February 1998

responseSet.title: The Editorial Board for Latin America
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Editorial Board for Latin America

David Tuckett

David Tuckett

London, November 1998

David Tuckett  

This issue sees changes in the Latin American Editorial Board. Jorge Ahumada, who has served as Editor for Latin America first with Horacio Etchegoyen and then on his own, now makes way for Elias Mallet da Rocha Barros. Juan Francisco Jordán-Moore becomes Deputy Editor. Dr Ahumada would like to thank all those who have worked with him on the IJPA.

It is not really possible to overestimate the importance of the role Jorge Ahumada has played in the development of the Latin American Editorial Board. When, after his appointment as sole Editor following the recommendation of the Search Committee in 1993, I suggested to him that it was time that this Board became a fully functioning peer review board, just like the other Boards, with appointments solely on academic merit, he agreed readily. He put a great deal of effort into winning support for the changes and into making it a success. He has been tireless in his efforts to be constructive and creative so that many Latin American authors have cause to thank him for his editing skills. We have been able to publish a significant number of papers, which have earned international respect, and the Board itself has gone from strength to strength. It met for the first time in São Paulo in 1995 and went on to have further meetings on its own and with the other boards. Peer review issues were discussed and standards of evaluation considered with Jorge then taking on the daunting task of running a series of open workshops in Brazil, Uruguay and Chile, all of which have been reported as an unqualified success. His work was a major contribution to the development of the Journal's peer review policy that has recently been published (IJPA, 1998, 79: 431-448).

The task of an editor is not easy. If publication in the Journal is to mean something it requires trying to set standards and hence quite regularly having to issue bad news. It means making judgments and giving reasons while at the same time holding on to the degree of modesty, tact and open-mindedness that is essential to win the support of authors and potential authors in a situation where inevitably more people are disappointed than not. It means making firm decisions but being aware that it is all too easy to be rigid. Editors are never going to be infallible and most likely one will make mistakes. I believe Jorge Ahumada has been exemplary in his efforts and that he has given the Board the foundations necessary for it to publish in the International Journal the very best contributions from Latin America.

responseSet.title: Het Geval Freud
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Het Geval Freud

K. Eissler

K. Eissler

300 Central Park West
New York, NY 10024

K. Eissler  

Dear Sir,

In his response to Han Israëls' Letter to the Editor, ‘Het geval Freud’, Mark Solms reported Israëls' allegation that I suppressed a letter written in 1934 by Sigmund Freud to Professor Meller (Int. J. Psychoanal., 1998, 78: 817-818). Israëls is in error. Ernst Freud wanted to publish that letter but Meller's family objected.

Solms also reported Israëls' claim that I suppressed the letters that Freud wrote to his fianceé between 1882 and 1886 (Int. J. Psychoanal., 1997, 78: 818). This, too, is wrong inasmuch as I have for several years been endeavoring to have them published in a scholarly edition.

Sincerely
K. R. Eissler
300 Central Park West
New York, NY 10024

173

responseSet.title: Lone Star: Signs, Borders and Thresholds
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Lone Star: Signs, Borders and Thresholds

Harriet Kimble WRYE

Harriet Kimble

17711 Porto Marina Way
Pacific Palisades
CA 90272
harrietwrye@compuserve.com

Harriet Kimble  

In Lone Star, written, directed and edited by John Sayles (1995), hidden and evocative bits and pieces of characters' lives emerge and are interwoven, as in an analysis. Past and present are never divided by uncrossable borders; porosity of the mind is dramatically realised in Sayles's seamless editing, shifting from one temporal perspective in a character's inner world to another. At the same time, however, Sayles describes his film as a ‘story about borders’, which he defines not only geographically, setting the film in the mythical Frontera, Texas (the town's name actually means border in Spanish), but psychologically as well: ‘A border is where you draw a line and say, “This is where I end and somebody else begins”’ (West, 1997, p. 14). Sayles is interested in the lines drawn between people—sex, class, race, age. In this review, I also propose that he is interested in thresholds and epiphanic border crossings, if you will, that permit a dialectic whereby a rigid point of demarcation may be transformed. These ‘thresholds’ occur in dramatic moments within the film and between the film screen and the audience.

The film's serious theme, often comically underscored, is about the deformative social constructions of power, race, age and gender. Sayles's cinematic eye draws our attention to his theme through visual images: signs of proprietorship, borders, military rank, gender, race, signs evoking prejudice. The logo of the film, the Texas sheriff's ‘lone star’ badge, is a sign both of political power and corruption and decay. Projections of idealisation or devaluation are represented via the abandoned drive-in film screen, formerly the site of such projections. The barren Texas landscape of Lone Star is dotted with signs, actual and symbolic, signifying borders of all kinds—incestual, metonymic, interpersonal, ideological, geographical, political, racial and gendered. As well as portraying certain psychic rigidities associated with primitive mental states, Lone Star can also be read as a text on transformational border crossings.

responseSet.title: Kohti piilotajuntaa [Towards the unconscious].  Edited by Esa Roos, Vesa Manninen and Jukka Välimäki.: Helsinki: Yliopistopaino.  1996.  Pp. 219
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Kohti piilotajuntaa [Towards the unconscious]. Edited by Esa Roos, Vesa Manninen and Jukka Välimäki.

Helsinki: Yliopistopaino. 1996. Pp. 219

Rauno Juntumaa

Rauno Juntumaa

Tähdenlennonkuja 28, 02240 Espoo

Rauno Juntumaa  

This book consists of three themes, the unconscious, identity and femininity. Each subject is considered by two different authors.

Esa Roos questions whether the duality between the conscious and the unconscious has been formulated correctly. The idea of consciousness expressed by Descartes in the sixteenth century facilitated the conception of the unconscious. Spinoza formulated the theory of unconscious memories, desires and feelings. According to Leibniz a conscious structure differs from an unconscious one. He differentiated between a phenomenon and a representation, just as the philosophers of the romantic era had done. German Herbart in the eighteenth century had a theory of the unconscious that included dynamic dependencies, repression, censorship and dreams. One of the progenitors of psychoanalysis is Schopenhauer. His point of view is close to Freud's ideas on the power of sexuality, repression, free association and primary process. Roos moves on to Matte-Blanco, pointing out that our unconscious includes more dimensions than our conscious does.

Mikael Enckell considers the topic of mysticism in psychoanalysis. The object of our interest in psychoanalysis is the invisible unconscious. We only have representations of the unconscious. So the unconscious has analogies with the Jewish conception of God. Man interprets God's message as he inteprets the unconscious messages. Man has many interpretations and idioms, as in the disorder of languages in Babel. Judaism is a religion centred on memory and the Jewish tradition of interpretation is analogical with the method of interpretation developed by Freud. Enckell has studied Proust, Kafka and Freud and he has found many parallels between Judaism and psychoanalysis.

Turo Reenkola demonstrates clearly that Freud's theories were neither mechanistic nor reductionistic. In metapsychology the ego is conceived as a striving towards identity. Consciousness emerges when identity has not been reached. The ego is a coherent organisation of psychic processes, not a part of the organisation. The term ego cannot be replaced by the term self. Rather, the self should be seen as an organisation of those self-representations that are used to maintain the identity of the ego. Environmental effects always function via psychic work. Thus the psyche is produced through its own activity.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Correspondencia de Sigmund Freud. Volumes 1-3. Edited by Nicolás Caparrós.

Madrid: Editorial Biblioteca Nueva. 1997. Pp. 600 + xix

Jordi Freixas

Jordi Freixas

Pau Claris 184, 08037 Barcelona

Jordi Freixas  

In 1997 Editorial Biblioteca Nueva brought out the third volume of a critical edition of the correspondence of Sigmund Freud edited and with a commentary by Nicolás Caparrós.

The author is a doctor of medicine and psychiatrist practising in Madrid, Spain. He has spent fourteen years producing this critical edition of Sigmund Freud's correspondence. His previous publications are Crisis de la familia [Family crisis] (Madrid: Fundamentos, 1973, 1977); Psicología de la liberación [The psychology of liberation] (Madrid: Fundamentos, 1976); La construcción de la personalidad. Las psicopatías [The construction of personality. The psychopathies] (Madrid: Fundamentos, 1981); Psicopatología Analítico-Vincular I and II [Psychopathology in terms of analysis and linking I and II] (Madrid: Quipú Ediciones, 1981, 1992); and Tiempo, Temporalidad y Psicoanálisis [Time, temporality and psychoanalysis] (Madrid: Quipú Ediciones, 1994).

Caparrós has sought to publish Freud's complete letters in chronological order of writing with the aim of putting an end to the current situation in which they are divided among a large number of partial editions. Although these are admittedly all interesting, the fragmentation tends to hinder rather than facilitate the achievement of an overall view. The author attributes this state of affairs to two factors.

Firstly, with the important exception of the Freud-Fliess letters brought out in 1950—an edition with many omissions and abbreviations—the correspondences have been published in dual form. For instance, we have volumes for Freud/Ferenczi, Freud/Pfister, Freud/Andreas-Salomé and Freud/Zweig. Caparrós quotes Allouch on the consequences of this approach to publication: ‘It seems odd and even foolish. Imagine a physicist studying the motion of the bodies in the Solar System in twos—Earth/Moon, Saturn/Sun, Venus/Mars, Jupiter/Moon, etc. Such a method would never have led to the discovery of the laws of gravitation' (p. xviii). I have my own hypothesis about the reasons for this strange custom on the part of publishers. Bion repeatedly drew attention to the association between psychoanalysis and the fundamental notion of dyad formation. Perhaps the publication of a large number of correspondences of couples that resulted in the birth, or new developments in the history, of psychoanalysis is a representation of the nuclear fantasy of Bion's basic assumption-pairing.

responseSet.title: The Origins and Psychodynamics of Creativity: A Psychoanalytic Perspective.  By Jerome D. Oremland.: Madison, Connecticut: International Universities Press.  1997. Pp. 200 + xvi.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Origins and Psychodynamics of Creativity: A Psychoanalytic Perspective. By Jerome D. Oremland.

Madison, Connecticut: International Universities Press. 1997. Pp. 200 + xvi.

Paul Schwaber

Paul Schwaber

258 Bradley Street, New Haven, CT 06511

Paul Schwaber  

In this astute contribution to considerations of creativity, Jerome Oremland brings an object-relations and developmental perspective and his own distinct attunement to the psychodynamics—and works—of accomplished artists. Rather than limit himself to writers and visual artists, he includes such diverse figures as theatre directors, performers, scientists and business people in his purview, democratising the focus while continuing a psychoanalytic tradition of arriving at general truths by way of extreme instances: here, the achievement of gifted individuals. The clinical examples he provides reveal creativity released from neurotic binds as well as talents relieved of the compulsion to perform.

Like Trilling, Kris and others since, he stresses the essential health of creativity, scorning assumptions of pathology as a necessary accompaniment of artistic production; and like Loewald, he views sublimation as significantly more than a defence. He follows Winnicott in locating art and the creative process in the multiply active transitional space that interdigitates self and other, where each of us interacts subjectively with ongoing social reality. Oremland focuses attention on ontogenetics and psychodynamics, on the internal past and present of creative activity and the psychology of aesthetic response. Psychoanalysis offers basic ground, he argues, because it opens up to apposite approaches and respects the socialisation, interests and distinctiveness of individuals—all of whom, necessarily, go from infancy through bodily maturation and expectable stages of psychological development. He posits this development as universal—perhaps a bold claim in post-modern, global times—but, granted that supposition, proceeds to synthesise previous work cogently and to advance our understanding.

Briefly revisiting well-charted territory, Oremland touches on the comparability of dreams and works of art, recognising differences but pondering both as communications—aspects of dialogue—and stressing condensation of potential meanings for interpreters. He scans the biographical path that Freud took in the Leonardo study (1910; S.E. 11) and indicates subsequent improvements on the way. Admiring more the directions opened up in ‘The Moses of Michelangelo’ essay (1914; S.E. 13), he praises its scrupulous attentiveness to detail and whole, which yielded new understanding of that great sculpture. He commends Freud's use of relevant scholarly literature in the essay and provides a telling instance of his own, early on, when commenting on Francesco Maffei's ‘Judith’ (one of several works for which he provides a photograph), in which that seventeenth-century painter merged traditional iconographic images of Salomé with the head of John the Baptist and Judith with a sword and the head of Holofernes. Guided by Panofsky's work, he notes both motifs in the painting and reasons from precedent to explain its title, then sheds psychoanalytic light on its enduring power. In Oremland's hands, art history and psychoanalysis complement each other to inform appreciation.

responseSet.title: ‘From instinct to self’
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

‘From instinct to self’

Graham Clarke

Graham Clarke

Room 5B.535
University of Essex
Colchester
Essex CO4 3SQ

Graham Clarke  

Dear Sir,

Whilst I welcome, and am in agreement with, most that Birtles and Scharff say in their recent paper on Fairbairn (IJPA, 78: 1085-1103), and believe that their book From Instinct to Self is an invaluable resource for all psychoanalytic scholars and theorists, I do think that their suggestion for modifying Fairbairn's model is open to (Fairbairnian) criticism.

I believe that Fairbairn's model is still relevant, if under-explored, and that it lends itself to a wide variety of potential applications. In my view, if it does need to be amended, it is in the direction of clarifying the dynamics of the model.

604

responseSet.title: The Creation of Dr. B: A Biography of Bruno Bettelheim.  By Richard Pollak.: New York: Simon & Schuster.  1997.  Pp. 478
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Creation of Dr. B: A Biography of Bruno Bettelheim. By Richard Pollak.

New York: Simon & Schuster. 1997. Pp. 478

Michael Beldoch

Michael Beldoch

1130 Park Avenue, New York, NY 10128
mbeldoch@mindspring.com

Michael Beldoch  

‘As a Freudian’, Bruno Bettelheim wrote in the introduction to his last book, ‘I believe what Freud said about biographers … namely that the person who undertakes such a task “binds himself to lying, to concealment, to flummery”’ (p. 15). In The Creation of Dr. B: A Biography of Bruno Bettelheim, Richard Pollak repeats the same exact passage from Freud's letter of 5 May 1936 to Arnold Zweig, discouraging him from becoming Freud's biographer. Pollak, however, finds himself disagreeing with Freud: ‘I don't think’, he writes, ‘that biographers are doomed to break up on the shoals of Freud's hyperbole, but truth was clearly a problematic issue for Bettelheim’ (p. 15; my italics).

But which truth is it here; narrative or historical? And which Bruno Bettelheim, Pollak's, or another biographer's published at about the same time? Is the truth his reputation as a world-renowned child psychologist, a Freudian trained expert on the treatment of autism, or is it that Bruno Bettelheim fabricated his credentials and plagiarised much of what he wrote? After the announcement of his suicide in 1990, newspapers were flooded with reports that Bettelheim, far from having created a safe haven for the variously disturbed children under his care at the Orthogenic School of the University of Chicago, had in fact abused some of those children as well as some of their teacher/therapists, and had run his school in some ways like the concentration camps in which he had been interred in Europe before he emigrated to America.

In this book Pollak exposes Bettelheim not only as a bully, but as a ‘psychologist’ who never had a degree in psychology, was never trained by Freud (very possibly never even met him), who became a dictatorial supervisor of therapists without ever having done any psychotherapy himself. But, Pollak tells us, Bettelheim was so widely and highly regarded, both by the masses and by many who were famous and respected in their own right, that few dared criticise him publicly.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Response

Emanuel Berman

Emanuel Berman

Haifa 31905

Emanuel Berman  

Dear Sir,

This response by Danielle Quinodoz to my paper extends an ongoing dialogue between us, which I find very intriguing.

While Mme. Quinodoz approaches Hitch-cock's Vertigo from a different starting point from my own (she takes advantage of her study of a specific clinical phenomenon, while I worked mostly from my subjective transferential experiences), our conclusions are compatible and interrelated. I see her analysis of the different experiences of reality and magic in the film as a valuable innovative contribution.

Sincerely yours
Emanuel Berman
Dept. of Psychology
University of Haifa
Haifa 31905

393

responseSet.title: Albert Dickson 1928-1997
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Albert Dickson 1928-1997

Clifford Yorke

Clifford Yorke

Fieldings
South Moreton
Near Didcot
Oxon
OX11 9AH

Clifford Yorke  

Albert Dickson, who died in hospital on 17 September 1997, had served the International Journal of Psycho-Analysis and the Publications Committee of the British Psycho-Analytical Society over many years in several significant ways. He was responsible for the Index of the Journal, compiling it with skill and accuracy; and it can fairly be said that no one excelled him in this exacting editorial task. Following the death of Angela Richards, who was still working at the time on the Penguin Freud edition, he took over the editorial work that remained to be completed on Volumes 12-15, and saw it through to its successful conclusion. Lastly, he was actively engaged for some time on the early work for a revised Standard Edition of the Complete Psychological Works of Sigmund Freud, but failing health enforced his withdrawal from a task that was by then beyond his strength.

Dickson was born in Dundee on 28 July 1928, and remained proud of his Scottish ancestry. Always a gifted linguist, he discharged his military service in Germany and it was there, stationed in Osnabruck, that his command of the German language was so greatly strengthened. He spent some years in Freiburg, was very fond of the Black Forest, and it was during his long sojourn in a country that he came to love that he demonstrated his talents as a translator, working mainly on English classic novels. He travelled widely, and continued his work as a translator in Sweden and Norway. His degree in Arabic was largely the fruit of a visit to Alexandria and elsewhere in Egypt and the Sudan that lasted well over two years and during which he made many friends. Unhappily, he returned from that country with a pulmonary infection, one episode in a long history of recurrent ill health.

But his interest in languages and travel was unabated, and his extensive knowledge of the history of the Middle East was added to his deep understanding of European history and culture. He knew many countries, including Yugoslavia, at first hand.

responseSet.title: Letters, Words and Metaphors:  A Psychoanalytic Reading of Michael Radford's Il Postino
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Letters, Words and Metaphors: A Psychoanalytic Reading of Michael Radford's Il Postino

Andrea Sabbadini

Andrea Sabbadini

38 Berkeley Road
London N8 8RU

Andrea Sabbadini  

Il Postino (1994), 105 minutes

Director: Michael Radford

Producer: Mario Cecchi Gori for Miramax

Distributed on Touchstone Home Video

‘WANTED—POSTMAN WITH BICYCLE.’ Only a temporary job, but almost tailor-made for Mario (Massimo Troisi), an introverted and uneducated yet sharply insightful young man, protagonist of Michael Radford's delightfully bitter-sweet, humane, humorous, moving yet never sentimental, comedy Il Postino. The job involves carrying the mail to just one illustrious addressee, the Chilean poet Pablo Neruda (Philippe Noiret) exiled in the early 1950s to the Southern Italian island of Procida because of his communist views. Every day Mario pedals up to the poet's new residence—a villa located on the top of a hill, surrounded by wild Mediterranean vegetation and overlooking the sea—carrying a leather bag full of correspondence: mostly, at least in his adolescent mind fuelled by newsreel mythology, love letters from adoring women all over the world.

At first, of course, Mario and Neruda hardly talk. What is there to be said, anyway, between them? Intrigued by his own thoughts and feelings, the postman cannot find the words to express them and the poet has better things to do than listen to him. But, as the relationship develops, Mario finds an interlocutor and, with it, a voice: tentative at first, when he dares, after a farcical rehearsal in front of the mirror, ask the poet for an autograph in the hope that this will impress his girlfriends in Naples—then progressively more secure. In Neruda-the-Man he gradually discovers the parental figure to identify with and idealise; in Neruda-the-Poet the language to make sense of his inner world. If Mario's real father is a silent, down-to-earth (or down-to-sea) fisherman with little understanding of his son's existential problems (‘I am tired of being a man’, Mario says echoing Neruda's words), his dead mother is entirely absent—other than, that is, in the guises of Nature, both literally in the external world, and literarily in Neruda's, and then Mario's own, poetry: an all-embracing, all-containing and nurturing sea surrounding the beautiful—part lush, part desert—island.

[[RunningHead]]

Quale psicoanalisi per le psicosi? [What form of psychoanalysis for the psychoses?]. Edited by A. Correale and L. Rinaldi.

Milan: Raffaello Cortina Editore. 1997. Pp. 305.

Anna Ferruta

Anna Ferruta

Viale Bianca Maria 5, 20122 Milan
(Translated by Philip Slotkin, MA, MITI.)

Anna Ferruta  

This book is an original contribution to the international debate on the psychoanalytic therapy of the psychoses. The wealth of clinical material it contains constitutes the foundation of experience underlying what the authors hold to be an innovative theoretical and methodological approach.

The book was written and composed by a specialised working group known as the Serious Pathologies Study Committee, set up many years ago within the Italian Psychoanalytical Society. The Committee's intensive and consistent work has become a beacon for others working in the field, an instrument to encourage fresh thinking and exchanges of views. The material is presented in a number of voices and comprises wide-ranging and discriminating reflections on a subject that neither psychoanalysis nor other theories have yet described satisfactorily. Yet the approach is not eclectic; owing to their common foundation of research, the basic theoretical and technical considerations presented form a unified whole. The authors have chosen to concentrate on the theoretical and practical side of psychoanalysis with a view to testing, deepening and deploying it in the therapy of the psychoses while eschewing all extraneous orientations, however numerous and valid.

The authors, as psychoanalyst-members of the Italian Psychoanalytical Society who have worked in psychiatric institutions, all remain firmly anchored within the field of psychoanalysis; they avoid yielding to suggestions from different fields in their descriptions of their research work. Favourable conditions for such an approach have existed in Italy for the last twenty years, with the involvement of large numbers of qualified psychoanalysts in the country's psychiatric structures (hospital wards and area treatment centres). A particularly important factor was the 1979 psychiatric reform programme, under which mental hospitals were closed down and various therapeutic institutions were established. The authors' work has attained such a maturity and depth of experience that it is now worth publishing, both for its own sake and as a spur to further research; that is the significance of the question mark in the title, which means ‘What form of psychoanalysis is appropriate for the psychoses?

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Recovered Memories of Trauma: Transferring the Present to the Past. By C. Brooks Brenneis.

Madison, CT: International Universities Press. 1997. Pp. 204 + xvii

Howard Levine

Howard Levine

124 Dean Road, Brookline, MA 02146

Howard Levine  

Adults who may have been abused or severely traumatised in childhood, but who have no continuous, conscious explicit memory of such events prior to beginning treatment, present analysts with a difficult clinical problem. With what degree of confidence can we assume that new memories which appear or are reconstructed in the course of treatment represent historical truth rather than fantasy? To what extent are such new memories the product of the lifting of repression and the integration of previously dissociated past experiences or the result of suggestion, compliance and other wishful and defensive forces at work within the analytic dyad?

Traditionally, the psychoanalytic rules of evidence—at least in theory—have been thought to rest securely upon concepts such as ‘neutrality’, ‘objectivity’ and the analyst's capacity to keep his/her countertransference in check. Contemporary formulations (e.g. Gill, 1994; Levine, 1994, 1997; Renik, 1993, 1996), however, have increasingly emphasised the degree to which such concepts are unattainable ideals. As such, they offer only limited protection in practice from unconscious subjective elements that may arise from either participant in the analytic process and distort the historical accuracy of reconstructions and memories. To make matters worse, in cases of suspected sexual abuse or incest, the pressure to distinguish true memory from false may be further exacerbated by our current cultural climate, in which the truth or falsity of reconstructed and recovered memories may become the subject of passionate and polemical debate—or even the basis for legal action.

The prevailing clinical wisdom about the optimal analytic stance needed for the successful treatment of patients who may have been abused or severely traumatised in the past, but who have no continuous explicit memory for the events, appears to be deeply divided. On the one hand stand the dangers of compliance and suggestion.

Response

Glen Gabbard MD

Glen Gabbard

PO Box 829
Topeka KS 66601-0829

Glen Gabbard  

Dear Sir

I must confess that I am baffled by Dr Werman's letter. He takes me to task for leaving out of my taxonomy of time-honored psychoanalytic approaches to film the ‘experiential world of the spectator’. He is incorrect in his assertion. The fifth approach in my list of seven is ‘the analysis of spectatorship’. In my explication of this category, I note the ‘heavy emphasis on the perspective of the spectator or audience member’ (pp. 431-432). I think my editorial makes it clear that Dr Werman and I share the view that the experiences of audience members viewing a film are a critically important part of an understanding of that film.

Sincerely
Glen O. Gabbard MD
The Menninger Clinic
PO Box 829
Topeka KS 66601-0829

Response

Glen Gabbard MD

Glen Gabbard

The Menninger Clinic
PO Box 829
Topeka
KS 66601-0829

Glen Gabbard  

Dear Sir,

In his letter regarding my psychoanalytic perspective on Neil Jordan's The Crying Game, Dr. Schrut appears to conflate the ‘hardwired’ phenomena of gender identity disorder and certain varieties of homosexuality and heterosexuality with the kind of fluidity one observes in the analytic setting. For both analyst and analysand there is an ongoing dialectical tension between one's nominal gender or one's nominal sexual orientation and the transference/countertransference identifications experienced in the crucible of analytic work. My own clinical experience as an analyst and a supervisor of other analysts is quite different from that described by Dr. Schrut.

Psychoanalytic data are unique to that setting and do not always corroborate observations of children where inferences are made from external behaviors. Nonetheless, even non-analytic data support my contention that in many cases sexual desire may be fluid. Margaret Mead's anthropological research led her to conclude that when nominally heterosexual individuals are confined to a situation where only same-sex partners are available, sexual desire for and sexual activity with persons of the same gender are common.

Finally, it is unclear to me how Dr. Schrut's relationship with the late Robert Stoller is relevant to his argument. For what it is worth, Stoller was also a mentor of mine, and much of what I have learned about gender, sexual orientation, and the ‘sexualities’ came from conversations with him and from his writings in those areas.

Glen O. Gabbard, MD
The Menninger Clinic
PO Box 829
Topeka
KS 66601-0829

responseSet.title: Being and Not Being; Clinical Applications of the Death Instinct.  By Otto Weininger.: Madison, CT: International Universities Press. 1996. Pp. 171
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Being and Not Being; Clinical Applications of the Death Instinct. By Otto Weininger.

Madison, CT: International Universities Press. 1996. Pp. 171

David Milrod

David Milrod

7 West 81st Street, New York, NY 10024

David Milrod  

Dr Weininger has written an interesting book on the application of Kleinian theory. Although entitled Being and not Being, it is the subtitle that conveys the main focus of the book's content, namely the death instinct. He examines the manifestations of the death instinct as he sees them in the clinical situation. The first part of the book examines the author's work mainly with children. The second part makes a powerful plea for society (especially hospitals, clinics, and parents) to come to a better understanding of the impact on children of the experience of death, whether the death affects a parent, sibling, or the child himself. His goal is to find a better way to handle the emotional disturbances stirred up by these tragic situations. Thus one part of the book is clinical and theoretical while the other has a more sociological emphasis.

The more important section to this reviewer is the first, dealing with clinical and theoretical issues. Here it seemed that Weininger wanted to spell out an integrated outline of Kleinian theory concerning projection and introjection mechanisms, good and bad introjects, aggression and the death instinct, and the special Kleinian view of the superego, all illustrated by clinical data. In the end it becomes more of a collection of case vignettes interconnected by a link to the death instinct, and interspersed with scattered explanations of Kleinian theory. In Weininger's description of Kleinian theory, superego punishment can come equally from sources that are internal (superego) or external (objects). Similarly, the punishment can be directed at the ego or at the external world. This is implied, for example, when he speaks of an extreme superego response that makes the baby see the world as dangerous (p. 9).

There are many statements referring to parental anger as an experience of the death instinct, suggesting that its origin lies in the external world. In fact Weininger treats anything related to death as a manifestation of the death instinct: children's theories and fantasies about death, their death wishes, subjective feelings of being dead, experiences of loved ones dying, fears of danger or damage, thoughts of suicide, wishes to hurt, vengeance, fantasies of punishment from external objects or from the superego. It is a usage far removed from Freud's, which referred to an inborn drive parallel to the libido, the aim of which was to return the individual to a lifeless inorganic state. In the service of that aim, the death instinct divides, separates, and fragments, rather than synthetically integrates psychic elements as does the libido. Many modern analysts in the United States view the death instinct as a philosophical concept, an overview of the clinical situation in which it manifests itself as aggression. In fact many would substitute aggression for Weininger's references to the death instinct without a loss of meaning.

responseSet.title: Gaudi: Multiple Approaches: Chaired by Pere Folch Mateu, Barcelona
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Gaudi: Multiple Approaches

Chaired by Pere Folch Mateu, Barcelona

Ralph Roughton

Ralph Roughton

1175 Peachtree Street
Atlanta, GA 30361

Ralph Roughton  

Gaudi was everywhere during the 40th Congress of the International Psychoanalytical Association in Barcelona in the summer of 1997. From the logo for the Congress, inspired by his ceramic architectural mosaics, to the awesome spires of his unfinished ‘Sagrada Familia’ cathedral that had introduced the city to the world's television audience of the 1992 Olympics; from the exuberant whimsy of Guell Park to the modernist apartment house ‘La Pedrera’—coffee break conversations at the Congress were sprinkled with, ‘Have you seen …?’

Thus it was fitting that the Congress programme ended with a panel discussion that attempted an understanding of this complex architectural genius and his work, the undisputed exemplar of Catalan modernism.

Folch began with some general reflections on the nature of psychoanalytic approaches to art and, in particular, to architecture. We may begin with the artist's life and look for ways in which his biographical experiences and peculiar characteristics are reflected in the creative effort; or we may begin with the work itself and make connections with the artist's individuality and feelings through the relationship created within us when we experience his work. This latter approach is particularly apt when the work is structural, because we can place ourselves affectively in the place and context of its creation—that is, with the artist, we inhabit his creation's spaces and, within our own mental spaces, atmospheres and moods are unveiled that convey the message of his work. Thus the work becomes both symbol and symbolished.

With Gaudi's work, both deliberate symbols and unintentional symbols intertwine to express his message, which he conveys through the multiple forms of materials, colour, sculpture, acoustic properties, and even words engraved on the stones. However, according to Folch, Gaudi's strongest symbolism in the Sagrada Familia cathedral is not in the decorative detail but in the use of the hyperbolic paraboloid form, which symbolises the Holy Trinity. Characteristic of Gaudi's work is the internalisation of light and of natural shapes put to functional use. His work is not primarily concerned with ordering space but with ordering light, and he sought to reproduce nature's constructions within architectural forms.

responseSet.documentRef: Brook, A. (1998) Castration as an Organiser of Bisexuality: Chaired by Erik Gann, San Rrancisco. International Journal of Psychoanalysis  79:380-383

responseSet.title: Castration as an Organiser of Bisexuality: Chaired by Erik Gann, San Rrancisco
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Castration as an Organiser of Bisexuality

Chaired by Erik Gann, San Rrancisco

Andrew Brook

Andrew Brook

Ottawa
ONT K1S 5B6

Andrew Brook  

Erik Gann opened the panel by repeating the reaction of one of his patients on finding out that Gann was participating in a panel on castration and bisexuality: the patient found the topic ‘one of those bizarre-sounding … esoteric, seemingly nonsensical and yet intimidating psychoanalytic topics’. People doubtless have various reasons for reacting to the psychoanalytic view of castration and bisexuality so strongly. One of the peculiarities of the treatment of the topic in the literature is that psychoanalytic theory harbours three very different notions of bisexuality. There are the two biological or constitutional ways in which everyone is bisexual, namely, having some measure of libidinal interest in objects of both sexes and harbouring a mixture of masculine and feminine features, and there is the clinical or manifest notion in which ‘bisexual’ refers to a specific kind of sexual object choice, namely, the choice of both males and females as sexual objects.

The Chairman centred his remarks on the clinical notion. According to Gann, the traditional psychoanalytic account found in Freud is that sexual interest in general and object choice in particular (male, female or bisexual) is determined by how the Oedipus complex is resolved, where the predominant shaping force is castration anxiety. In line with the anatomical differences between boys and girls, this anxiety takes two distinct forms. In boys, Freud held it to consist in ‘the terrifying fantasy of the threat of castration’, as Gann put it. In girls, Freud held it to take the form of the angry, despairing, envious fantasy of having been castrated. Depending on the vicissitudes of the passage through the oedipal stage, a boy or girl will become predominantly heterosexual, predominantly homosexual, or clinically or manifestly bisexual.

We are over ninety years beyond Freud's first important articulation of these ideas (1905, Three Essays on Sexuality, S.E. 7) and a great deal has happened in the meantime. Gann summarised a few of the more recent developments. One important development is that the oedipal stage with its emphasis on castration has been supplemented with pre-oedipal stages, emphasising attachment and separation in one tradition, archaic fantasies of destruction and reparation in another. In yet another tradition, the notion of the narcissistic line of development has been added to Freud's line of psychosexual development.

responseSet.documentRef: Schrut, A. (1998) Glen O. Gabbard's Review of The Crying Game. International Journal of Psychoanalysis  79:601-602

responseSet.title: Glen O. Gabbard's Review of The Crying Game
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Glen O. Gabbard's Review of The Crying Game

Albert Schrut MD

Albert Schrut

911 Honeywood Road
Los Angeles
CA 90049-1307

Albert Schrut  

Dear Sir,

Gabbard restates Robert Stoller's words that heterosexuality is no more natural or normal than homosexuality. The probable truth of these words is being demonstrated by modern research and clinical findings. However, there is little evidence to demonstrate Gabbard's contention, as he is reminded by the film, ‘that we are all on shaky ground when it comes to our sexual identities and preferences’ (my italics). I have not found this to be so in my own work in analysis with the large majority of patients over the past nearly forty years, or in my supervision of patients of a number of clinical associates (candidates). The vast majority of patients have been pretty firm in their basic sexual identities and preferences.

Gabbard repeats another long-standing unproven contention regarding children: that during the so-called pre-oedipal phase the recognition of anatomical gender differences occurs, which ‘signals a period of mourning that accompanies the realization that gender entail limits. For the boy child, it may also trigger anxiety that his genitals may be vulnerable’ (commonly summarised as ‘castration anxiety’ during the so called oedipal phase, my italics).

In my own years of research into infancy and early childhood at the Cedars/Sinai Medical Center of Los Angeles, or in my many years of work in child analysis, I did not commonly find a period of anxiety, mourning, or depression during those same so called ‘oedipal’ years relating to their genitals, except in a small minority of children wherein possible explanations for such reactions were variable.

I am still looking for any substantive research done by psychoanalysts or researchers outside of psychoanalysis that demonstrates that the majority of children go through a period of anxiety or depression related to the so-called Oedipal period, and I have written about my questions on this matter.

About thirty years ago I presented to Robert Stoller my analysis of a child with a gender identity disorder at a combined meeting of the Southern California Psychoanalytic and the Los Angeles Psychoanalytic Institutes at the UCLA medical center. Stoller was always creative, resourceful, and open-minded in his thinking.
