Sexuality and Gender Identity

Chaired by Jorge Canestri, Rome

Alejandro Morales

Alejandro Morales

Rio Tiber No. 132
Col. del Valle
66220 Garza Garcia
Monterrey
Mexico
atamez@ccr.dsi.uanl.mx

Alejandro Morales  

The Chairman defined his role as someone who should direct and not comment. After presenting the panellists he asked Reimut Reiche to begin with his paper ‘Sexuality and gender identity’, which criticised first Stoller's concept of core gender identity (CGI) (1968, Sex and Gender. New York: Science House); and then Reiche defined the concept of gender: ‘[This] emphasizes psychic or social connotations of sex or of sexual concepts’, and then the term ‘gender identity’, neither of which is genuinely psychoanalytic. He also warned about the incorrect use of ‘CGI’ (Wallerstein's and Sandler's would be my understanding) to refer to an empirical or clinical fact when it actually indicates a scientific or metapsychological concept.

Person & Ovesey's (1983, ‘Psychoanalytic theories of gender identity. J. Amer. Acad. Psychoanal., 11: 203-226) study of a primary CGI free of conflict from which gender and sexual roles are developed was discussed by Reiche. For Reiche, CGI develops in parallel with a ‘core object choice’, ‘core’ referring to the sexual proto-object-choice, which gives origin to a sexual proto-identity, and this, in turn, permits an explication of the ideas of constitutional bisexuality developed by Freud (1937, Analysis Terminable and Interminable. S.E. 23.).

Later, Reiche emphasised that in ‘the primal-object messages (the parental messages that deal with sex assignment) leading to the formation of CGI, plus the traces of elements that generally cannot be assimilated by the child’, these traces organise in the unconscious identity as well as object choice. The case was presented of a woman who developed a manifest transvestite perversion in her adolescence. Periodically, in hardly bearable sexually tense states, she would ‘dress up like a man who dresses up like a woman’. This phenomenon was given the name ‘inner transvestite’. The patient had had a father who was hoping for a male child at the birth of each of his daughters. The patient presented, in the Person & Ovesey sense, an ambiguous gender identity, in Stoller's sense an isomorphic CGI.

responseSet.title: ‘The Elusive Concept of “Internal Objects” (1934-1943)’ by R. D. HinshelWood
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

‘The Elusive Concept of “Internal Objects” (1934-1943)’ by R. D. HinshelWood

Paul Williams

Paul Williams

Flat 1, 15 Rosslyn Hill,
London NW3 5UJ

Paul Williams  

Hinshelwood's paper is an unusual amalgam of theoretical, historical and social-structural enquiry into the intellectual and group life of the British Society between 1934 and 1943, from the perspective of the evolution of the role of the concept of ‘internal objects’. Hinshelwood uses the debate over, and the fate of, this important concept to offer insight into ways in which the dispute between Anna Freud and Melanie Klein and the parallel schism within the Society were conducted, and into the broader condition of psychoanalysis at the time. Hinshelwood's paper was made available on the IJPA Web Site (http://www.ijpa.org) and an extensive discussion followed. The key points made in Hinshelwood's paper are given here, along with the main discussion comments.

The emergence of the Klein Group in the British Society, the effect of the war years and the arrival of the Freud family in London is the historical context of Hinshelwood's theme. In the years before the outbreak of the Second World War the main psychoanalytic conflict in London was with the Viennese. By 1944 this conflict had transformed itself into warring camps within the British Society, a complex struggle that culminated in the creation of the British tripartite group system, which pertains to this day. Hinshelwood identifies Klein's 1935 paper ‘A contribution to the psychogenesis of manic-depressive states’ as pivotal to the struggles within the British Society. Klein's emphasis on the concrete, internal status of objects experienced as existing within the body, and their relationship to the depressive position, posed important questions in relation to fantasy and sublimation (amongst other things). Acclaim and interest in Klein's paper and her development of thinking around processes of internalisation rapidly gave way to fervent, often acrimonious, argument, and the previously regulated tensions between London and Vienna were gradually incorporated into and fermented the scientific meetings that followed the presentation of Klein's paper. At the same time psychoanalysis across much of Europe had been all but decimated in the shadows of war: the Freud family had been forced to move to London, and this was an arrival that deeply threatened the London Klein Group. Klein gave a further paper (in 1938)—‘Mourning and its relation to manic-depressive states’—which was followed promptly by Susan Isaacs' paper on unconscious phantasy, indicating the assertiveness of the newly established Kleinian views.

responseSet.title: The Holocaust: Chaired by Ilany Kogan, Rehovot
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Holocaust

Chaired by Ilany Kogan, Rehovot

David Scharff

David Scharff

6612 Kennedy Drive
Chevy Chase
MD 20815

David Scharff  

This panel was designed to study the impact of the Holocaust on the sexuality of survivors and their children, and the impact of the Nazi experience on the sexuality and gender identity of the families of the perpetrators. Clearly, said Ilany Kogan, trying to understand these issues is part of our own attempt to master the trauma.

Marion Oliner of New York presented ‘The unresolved puzzle of trauma: did the experience of the Holocaust infringe on sexuality?’ She employed André Green's idea of presentations and representations in the organisation of psychic experience. Presentations describe perceptions and the material world presented to the senses without psychic processing, while representations, arising in the absence of direct gratification, represent psychic processing as in dreams and symbol formation. Representations owe their existence in memory to absence, whereas presentations are based on the presence of objects. Presentations are internally transformed into representations imbued with personal and sexualised meaning—the familiar ground of analytic meaning. Trauma is often preserved by presentations, which seem not to be processed by psychic reality. They carry the impact of unprocessable external reality. It is analysis that can give presentations meaning through representation. By studying trauma, analysts have been able to understand that memories that are overwhelming can be carried in the mind as unprocessed presentations devoid of meaning or connection to the individual's personal history. Although analysts study representations they need to accept the duality of memories implied in these separate processes, and that the deadening of meaning is the result of actual trauma.

Oliner discussed an analysis reported by Friedman (1978, ‘Psychoanalytic study of an unusual perversion’, J. Amer. Psychoanal. Assn., 26: 749-778) of a Holocaust survivor referred for analysis after requesting sex reassignment surgery. Following his family's annihilation, the patient became a hero in the Polish resistance. After the war, he developed a perversion in response to life-threatening situations. Requesting a shave from a barber, he complained repeatedly that it was not smooth enough until the exasperated barber, breathing heavily, drew the razor over his neck at which point the patient would have an orgasm. Friedman analysed the perversion in terms of early castration fears, oedipal strivings and primal scene events, and through an understanding that a reversal of subject and object allowed the patient to persecute his childhood persecutors in fantasy.

responseSet.title: Behind the Scenes.  Freud in Correspondence. Edited by Patrick Mahony, Carlo Bonomi and Jan Stensson.: Oxford: Scandinavian University Press.  1997.  Pp. 432
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Behind the Scenes. Freud in Correspondence. Edited by Patrick Mahony, Carlo Bonomi and Jan Stensson.

Oxford: Scandinavian University Press. 1997. Pp. 432

Sara Flanders

Sara Flanders

33 King Henry's Road, London NW3

Sara Flanders  

In Behind the Scenes, sub-titled Freud in Correspondence, a trio of editors have gathered together a collection of papers, each of which considers some aspect of the information made public in the ever-widening flow of Freud's available and subsequently published correspondence. Patrick Mahony, one of the two contributing editors to this volume, tells us in the preface that if all of Freud's existing letters were published, which is 70 per cent of those he actually wrote, they might extend to three times the length of the Standard Edition. As Alain de Mijolla states in one of the most thoughtful papers in the collection, Freud's letter-writing, however self-conscious, was also compulsive, and crucial to the maintenance of the distanced intimacy on which he relied as a condition of his prodigious creativity. De Mijolla explores and convinces us of the consuming urgency of this process, the passionate intensity of the correspondence, which formed a creative crucible out of which psychoanalysis, as a theory and a practice, developed. As readers of this collection we can be grateful to those contributors who allowed themselves to get close enough to the heat to bring us an illumination that is sometimes disturbing. One of the authors' themes is that Freud's greatness, no longer in any reasonable doubt, requires no cover-up, we can risk disturbing conventional wisdom. An essay in this selection looks at the censorship suggested by Anna Freud prior to the publication of the Jung letters. Her suggestions are fully understandable, considering her powerful and unique identification with the good name of the psychoanalytical movement. In the case of the Jung-Freud letters, however, her views were resisted.

responseSet.title: Robert Caper on ‘A mind of one's own’ (IJPA, 78: 265-278)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Robert Caper on ‘A mind of one's own’ (IJPA, 78: 265-278)

Raymond Poggi

Raymond Poggi

2041 Bancroft Way, Suite 307
Berkeley
CA 94704

Raymond Poggi  

Dear Sir,

I agree with the importance of finding a theoretical basis for the autonomy of the analyst. Without recognizing the essential need of such autonomy to analysis, we are in danger of tacitly assuming that the sole contribution of the analyst is to be empathically in tune with the patient. However, I think to achieve a solid theoretical basis for the necessary autonomy of the analyst there needs to be an change in the theory of object relations. In an effort to avoid making such a change, Dr Caper becomes confusing.

The change or rethinking that needs doing at least concerns an old question. Are we always and for ever and in all instances objectrelated? This question has to do with the issue of primary narcissism and primary envy. The latter being a response to the former that implies no concern for the object but still a response to the object. Winnicott struggled with this and Heimann in her articles on sublimation came squarely down on the side of there being at all times a capacity in the infant for gratification that has no dependence on the object.

388

responseSet.title: Back to Freud's Texts, Making Silent Documents Speak.  By Ilse Grubrich-Simitis.  Translated by Philip Slotkin.: New Haven and London: Yale University Press.  1996.  Pp. 322
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Back to Freud's Texts, Making Silent Documents Speak. By Ilse Grubrich-Simitis. Translated by Philip Slotkin.

New Haven and London: Yale University Press. 1996. Pp. 322

Stephen Grosz

Stephen Grosz

52 Downshire Hill, London NW3 1PA

Stephen Grosz  

This study of Freud's texts, originally published in German in 1993 and skilfully translated here by Philip Slotkin, is an important event in the development of our understanding of Freud's ideas and writing. The original German edition Züruck zu Freuds Texten, Stumme Dokumente sprechen machen was the subject of a thoughtful review in these pages by Mark Solms (1994, Ilse Grubrich-Simitis, Züruck zu Freuds Texten, Stumme Dokumente sprechen machen. Int. J. Psycho-Anal., 75: 153-159) and readers who would like to have a full summary and discussion of the book's contents should read Solms's longer essay.

Grubrich-Simitis is a training and supervising psychoanalyst in Frankfurt and a member of the German Psychoanalytical Association. During the 1960s and 1970s she worked at Fischer Verlag, Freud's post-war publisher in Germany, as an editor of Freud's collected works. This book grows out of her singular, extensive experience as editor, Freud scholar and clinician. The book's structure reflects its genesis.

Part I is a revised version of Grubrich-Simitis's 1989 essay on the history of the Germanlanguage editions of Freud (I. Zur Geschichte der deutschsprachigen Freud-Ausgaben, Psyche, 43: 773-802; 889-917). There are three chapters, named after the places where Freud was originally published in German: ‘Vienna: from the beginnings until 1938’, ‘London: from 1938 to 1960’ and ‘Frankfurt: from 1960 to the present’. In the first two chapters Grubrich-Simitis demonstrates how deeply involved Freud was in the publication, translation and distribution of his works; she also shows how important it was, personally, to Freud to establish and develop a psychoanalytic press. Chapter Three describes the attempts to prepare a critical edition of Freud in German and how the situation that Strachey complained of—namely, that there is no complete, reliable edition of Freud's writings in German—continues today.

responseSet.title: Manifestations of Sexuality in Child Analysis: Chaired by Erna Furman, Cleveland, Oh
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Manifestations of Sexuality in Child Analysis

Chaired by Erna Furman, Cleveland, Oh

Rita Frankiel

Rita Frankiel

Rita V. Frankiel
11 West 73rd St.
New York
NY 10023

Rita Frankiel  

Peter Blos introduced the speakers, and turned the meeting over to Erna Furman. Furman then introduced the theme of the meeting with a spirited presentation and defence of libido theory, emphasising its centrality and indispensability in the understanding of children and their communications in analysis.

Reviewing the variable position of sexuality in contemporary psychoanalysis, Furman noted that many analysts would prefer to have a psychoanalysis without it. She cited Renik's recent suggestion that sexuality may be viewed in terms of its components and integrated as a part of other motivational concepts (1996; Reflections on the 40th IPA Congress theme. International Psychoanalysis: The Newsletter of the International Psychoanalytical Association, 5: 1: 38-40). She argued that object-relations theory, which was initially thought to be an addition to the role of the drives, now seems to be focused on dispensing with sexuality altogether and reducing psychoanalytic data and process solely to interpersonal relationships. In her view, both cases to be presented offered multiple manifestations of psychosexual development from all levels and lent themselves to comparison of views on infantile sexuality.

Furman pointed out that the first case, presented by Joana Tous d'Adroer of Barcelona, demonstrated the way in which primitive aspects of infantile sexuality were expressed in the transference. Pathology from all levels of development was observable. The case particularly highlighted the way in which primitive sexual elements in the transference were hidden behind more mature aspects of psychosexual development, for example, how a seeming need for latency fairness warded off not only displaced oedipal rivalry but anal-sadistic impulses to control the object.

Of the second case, reported by Calvern Narcisi of Denver, she emphasised that clinical evidence of the anal-sadistic drive was prominent in the analysis of a latency girl with faecal retention. That case highlighted the connection between the onset and meaning of symptomatic faecal retention and the loss of a father who maintained mainly anal and phallic-sadistic relationships with the child and her mother.

responseSet.title: Somatic Illness and the Patient's Other Story.  By Brian Broom.: London/New York: Free Association Books.  1997.  Pp. 187
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Somatic Illness and the Patient's Other Story. By Brian Broom.

London/New York: Free Association Books. 1997. Pp. 187

Kenneth Sanders

Kenneth Sanders

20 Basing Hill, London NW11 8TH

Kenneth Sanders  

Brian Broom describes his work as a mind/body/spirit approach to disease. He writes about his method of urging patients selected as ‘somatisers’ to tell their ‘other story’, defined as: ‘amongst many other things, a woven tapestry of events, and of highly idiosyncratic responses to events. Many of the very significant events have to do with the vicissitudes of the patient's relationships with the world, and with other significant persions' (p. 1).

The name of the great psychosomatic physician, Groddeck, is invoked in tribute to the man who suggested the term ‘the Id’ to Freud, and who wrote imaginatively about the meaning of illness. Dr Broom is a consultant physician and psychotherapist, and there is a difference from the psychoanalytic approach of Balint, who initiated seminars at the Tavistock Clinic in London in 1969 to promote greater understanding between doctor and patient in general practice.

The author sees

physical disease not merely as some sort of biological disarray, but as a crystallisation of a complex set of interacting processes including the somatic and the psychological, and to do with language and relationship. In this view I find myself closely aligned with McDougall. My approach to the psychotherapy of somatic disorders is rather different from hers. We share an emphasis on object relations theory, self psychology and interpersonal theory, but I am less Freudian and often work in a briefer psychotherapeutic mode (p. 172).

In 1986 he set up with two others the Arahura (New Zealand indigenous people's term for ‘pathway to discovery’) Christian Counselling and Medical Centre, accepting referrals in connection with his previous speciality of immunology and allergic disorders. Since then the Centre has increased its staff and scope. Broom confesses to being a passionate educator and provides an opportunity for many with psychosomatic disorders to tell their story of anxiety and confusion.

The first chapter describes a dozen short case histories of ‘somatisers’ of all ages, most of whose symptoms improved over a short period of psychotherapy, while the second gives greater detail in a case of prostatitis treated with twenty-three sessions of psychotherapy over a period of one year.

<autaff affid="A01"><addr><ln>Beate Lohser</ln> <ln>The Wright Institute</ln> <ln>2728 Durant Avenue</ln> <ln>Berkeley</ln> <ln>CA 94704</ln></addr></autaff></artauth></artinfo>
responseSet.title: Unorthodox Freud: The View from the Couch
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Unorthodox Freud: The View from the Couch

Peter Newton

Peter Newton

Beate Lohser
The Wright Institute
2728 Durant Avenue
Berkeley
CA 94704

Peter Newton  

Dear Sir,

Makari's recent review of our book, Unorthodox Freud: The View from the Couch, is an example of a careless review (Int. J. Psychoanal., 1997, 78: 1039-1041). Although he generally likes the book, he fails to notice its most important findings and instead imagines it to support a stereotype about Freud's clinical work which we question in the introduction and energetically refute throughout the body of the book and in its conclusion. We do not find, as Makari represents, that Freud wrote one thing about treatment in his papers on technique and did another in practice—the old stereotype of the starched Freud in print and the undisciplined libertine in the consulting room. From a close study of his analysands' accounts, we found that Freud behaved in a natural, personally engaged, and expressive manner towards them. Further, we found that this way of relating to patients was, on the whole, consistent with his writings on technique.

Able to read Freud in German, we show that Strachey's translation of Freud's papers pervasively over-emphasized the aseptic aspects of Freud's advice about technique (sic—Strachey changed Freud's word, Ratschläge, meaning pieces of advice, into recommendations in the ‘Recommendations to Physicians’ series). Worse, those aspects of Freud's suggestions which survived translation concerning kindness, empathy, and openminded respect for the great variability of psychotherapeutic phenomena were already in Freud's day being ignored in Europe and scarcely weathered the crossing to the United States. We concluded that what is considered mainstream and orthodox technique, at least in the United States, is based upon a fundamental misunderstanding not only of Freud's practice, but of his writings as well. What is now considered orthodox technique is a neo-orthodoxy; this doesn't make it bad, only different.

Ongoing misunderstandings concern such critical matters as Freud's ideas about the handling of resistance and transference. In the cases we examine, Freud did not analyze resistance. Further and contrary to settled opinion, neither did he write that resistance should be analyzed. Instead, he consistently wrote of resistance being uncovered (aufdecken) and overcome (überwinden), not analyzed (analysieren would have been the word, if he had wanted to say that). Analyzing resistance for its drive and defensive properties may be just the thing we ought to be doing, but it is not what Freud did or wrote that we should do. He believed, in word and in deed, that the thing to do with resistance was to uncover it for the patient, show it to him, and then urge him, armed with this new knowledge, to resume full free association in spite of the resistance. Working through meant pushing through the resistance in an act of will power.

Nathan Kravis

141 East 55th Street

NY 10022

Nathan Kravis  

Dear Sir

As evidence that I exaggerate and quote out of context Dr O'Neil cites my contention that Rado's characterization of Anna Freud is derisive. The passage in question (quoted in my review) reads: ‘The nightmare was that she had no inkling what science is. She was educated to be a nursery school teacher, not even a grammar school instructor; that was the extent of her scientific equipment’ (p. 105). Readers can judge whether or not this description of one of the pre-eminent psychoanalysts of this century passes muster as derision.

Yes, I do hold Rado partly to blame for his own obliteration from the psychoanalytic firmament. Interestingly, this view is not radically different from Swerdloff's in 1986. At that time she wrote that Rado was ‘impatient and pugnacious’ when he encountered disagreement, and noted that ‘Those qualities engendered hostility which prompted opposition to his concepts’ (Swerdloff, 1986, p. 118). In my review I gave several examples drawn from the book to support my characterization of Rado as bilious and vituperative. If these examples seriously misrepresent Rado, then it falls to the editors to explain why they elected to publish them and why their subject's personal memoir is utterly devoid of a kind word for anyone.

Though Dr O'Neil portrays me as callously unmoved, I made clear in my review that, quite to the contrary, I find the story of what I have called Rado's intellectual suicide enormously sad. This had more to do with his insistence upon what in my review I dubbed his idiolect than with Rado's personality per se. Nevertheless, the spectacle of his trashing so many important figures in his career makes his obviously embittered remembrances an unfortunate final chapter to an embattled life and a rather unlikely catalyst for posthumous rediscovery. This, too, reflects the editors' decisions, not mine.

599

Psychosis

Chaired by Jorge García Badaracco, Buenos Aires

Jonathan Sklar

Jonathan Sklar

15 Well Road
London NW3 1LH

Jonathan Sklar  

The panel consisted of two clinical presentations and a theoretical presentation.

Nicolò began by reporting the periodic emergence of delirious ideas in a 26-year-old man called Alfredo. Her theme was that the psychotic patient tried to recreate forms of his earlier relationships in an attempt to communicate and share those disturbed relationships that were the origin of the psychotic process. The history explored the patient's neglect by parents who were more involved in an older brother's illness, and with the patient's twin sister's regressive behaviour. Mother was described as cold, distant and hysterical. She had had many surgical operations. Mother wanted to save the twin sister, who was ill at birth, even if it cost the patient his own life. During adolescence Alfredo took heavy drugs and began to have a polymorphously perverse sexual life.

Some weeks into analytic treatment, the referring analyst told Nicolò privately that the twins had been born from an illegitimate affair—an example of an enactment of trauma pushed into the transference, such that the knowing and not-knowing became a potent theme in the analysis. Later the patient mentioned his idea of a family romance that the children belonged to another father. Nicolò was interested in the transgenerátional dimension in relation to secrets in the family, which prevented her patient developing a separate identity.

Clinically, the recurrence of psychotic regression was an expression and a means of communicating transgenerational trauma. Self-therapy such as urine drinking was an attempt to heal as well as a communication of aggression towards object and self. The analyst spoke of her need to maintain her own sanity in the face of the patient's aggressive eruptions, at the same time as working ‘along the ridge of the delirium’. Nicolò described points of fracture in the analysis and areas of disintegration, which the patient was unable to think about, and in the shadows was the family's inability to think about nodal experiences. ‘The transgenerational trauma is not thought, nor is it thinkable. Nor is there a place to begin to think into the play-space that is discovered in analysis other than a psychotic way of thinking.’ Alfredo's psychosis was perceived by him as a means of saving the family.

Historicising the Origins of Kleinian Psychoanalysis (IJPA, 78: 1165-1182)

William Gillespie

William Gillespie

4 Eton Villas
London NW3 4SX

William Gillespie  

Dear Sir,

Joseph Aguayo's historical article in the Journal of December 1997 interested me, particularly his attempt to understand Ernest Jones's motives in encouraging Melanie Klein to settle in London. I have long believed that one of his aims was to have her analyse one or more of his children. But Aguayo suggests that he was concerned with the succession to Sigmund Freud: should there not be a British rival to Anna Freud, and should not London replace Vienna?

My own experience tends to support this idea. When I came to London at the end of 1931, after a year spent in Vienna as a holder of an Edinburgh University travelling scholarship, I had an initial interview with Ernest Jones with a view to continuing my psychoanalytic training in London. One of Jones's first questions was ‘Why did you choose Vienna instead of London?’ I had the good sense to suppress what my natural response would have been: ‘Because I thought Vienna was the Mecca of psychoanalysis’. Instead I said: ‘London would not have been regarded as travelling’. Now I realise that what Jones meant was: ‘How could you not know that London is superior to Vienna?’.

Yours sincerely
William Gillespie
4 Eton Villas
London NW3 4SX

[[RunningHead]]

Supervision and its Vicissitudes. Edited by Brian Martindale, Margareta Mörner, María Eugenia, Cid Rodríguez and Jean-Pierre Vidit.

London: Karnac Books. 1997. Pp. 191

Patrick Casement

Patrick Casement

122 Mansfield Road, London NW3 2JB

Patrick Casement  

This monograph, produced on behalf of the European Federation for Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy, is a truly international collaboration. With a foreword by Robert Wallerstein and an introduction by Brian Martindale, it has contributors from Britain, Germany, Spain, Argentina, Sweden, Switzerland and the US. It covers a wide range of clinical practice.

In easily readable chapters, León Grinberg addresses the subject of supervising psychoanalytic candidates; Victor Sedlak, that of supervising untrained psychotherapists; Montserrat Martínez del Pozo describes supervision in institutional settings, particularly within a Kleinian framework; and Ulrich Streeck discusses various opportunities for psychoanalytically based supervision within mental health settings. Dorothy Lloyd-Owen then describes her supervision of mental health workers with forensic patients, and Peter-Christian Miest recounts his supervision of staff in a hospice for AIDS patients. In addition there are chapters by Imre Szecsödy and Kurt Gordan on the established work of teaching supervisors in Sweden, and a chapter by Robert Langs on the framework of supervision in psychoanalytic psychotherapy.

Grinberg insists that ‘supervision is not, and must not be, therapy’ (p. 9). As well as considering the issues of transference and countertransference, in relation to supervision, he explores the problem of the supervisee's transference to the supervisor, the supervisor's transference to the supervisee and parallel process in the supervision. He also revisits the distinction he makes between countertransference and projective-counteridentification reactions. Among the problems for supervisors, he touches on such issues as those of the rivalrous supervisee. In all of this Grinberg conveys his overall attitude to supervision in saying: ‘I consider that the most important thing to teach [supervisees] is how to think, so that they can think on their own’ (p. 14).

Sedlak feels that it is important to help untrained therapists to become more professional in their work, as they will be practising therapy in any case—whether or not they receive good supervision. So, in Leeds and in Poland, he has been supervising therapists who have not had the opportunities to become ‘properly’ trained. He focuses in particular upon the vulnerability that an untrained therapist is most likely to be exposed to, in that of a strong negative countertransference. He points out that it is particularly difficult for the unanalysed to become sufficiently aware of their own countertransference to be able to manage this appropriately, especially when under pressure. But he emphasises the contribution that another mind can make towards helping the untrained therapist to overcome these difficulties more easily. He therefore recommends that one approaches the supervision of untrained therapists with due respect and empathy for their situation.

[[RunningHead]]

Sexuality, Sublimation and Psychic Activity

Chaired by Jacqueline Amati-Mehler, Rome

Nicola Abel-Hirsch

Nicola Abel-Hirsch

Nicola Abel-Hirsch
65 Lofting Road
London N1 1JA

Nicola Abel-Hirsch  

In her opening remarks Jacqueline Amati-Mehler commented that although the Freudian definition of sublimation seemed quite straightforward, from a dynamic viewpoint it was one of the most obscure concepts. She also noted that a paper on sublimation was one of the three Freudian essays that were lost. The three papers by Campos, Jacobs and Danckwardt made use of the term sublimation, but applied it in different ways. Campos's interest is in sublimation as a ‘creative process’; Jacobs considers the importance of play as a sublimatory activity for a person's sexuality; whilst Danckwardt understands sublimation to be the development of thought through the movement between the paranoid-schizoid and depressive positions and as an ‘intersubjective’ process.

In the discussions a number of questions were asked by the panellists and the audience about the relationship between on the one hand play, symbolisation, creativity and the development of thought and on the other hand sublimation. Is sublimation a term that can be used interchangeably with the others, is it redundant, or is there something specific about it as a process? Whilst no shared understanding of sublimation was arrived at explicitly by the work of the panel, Amati-Mehler pointed out that there was an interesting area of convergence between the papers in the recurring theme of the element of surprise.

Campos referred in his paper ‘The erotic life of the great creators’ to Freud's 1919 paper on ‘The uncanny’ and Reik's paper on ‘Surprise’. Campos stated that for Freud the ‘uncanny’ was ‘the expected-unexpected end, full of profound and revealing contents’. Under the name of ‘The uncanny’ and ‘Surprise’, Campos argued, Freud and Reik attempt to describe one's feelings when meeting the unconscious. In his paper ‘How does sublimation work?—Sublimation as an intersubjective process-object’, Danckwardt postulated a change in Freud from his being identified with his parents in their admiration of ‘Sigi the wonderboy’ to his having a capacity himself for astonishment and wonder in the world. Danckwardt suggested that this then led to Freud's recommendation to analysts of the important capacity for ‘letting oneself be surprised’ and he linked this with Bion's ‘selected fact’ and ‘selected emotion’. Jacobs, in discussion, referred to the element of surprise when he drew a contrast between the difficulty many people had in accepting any change and the considerable fluidity and capacity for change shown by ‘creators’.

[[RunningHead]]

Therapy or Coercion? Does Psychoanalysis Differ from Brainwashing? By R. D. Hinshelwood.

London: Karnac Books. 1997. Pp. 249

Catia Galatariotou

Catia Galatariotou

33 Elm Tree Road, London NW8 9JR

Catia Galatariotou  

The subject matter of this book is the ethics of psychoanalysis. The author explores it within an interdisciplinary framework that encompasses aspects of psychoanalysis (and all psychoanalytic psychotherapies), psychiatry and medicine in general, philosophy, society and history. All these apparently disparate subjects coalesce at one point, where they all share a common subject, raise the same question, and pose the same challenge: the subject is the way one human being (or institution) influences another; the question is what is it that distinguishes benevolent from malevolent influencing; the challenge is to formulate a criterion for such a crucial ethical distinction. Hinshelwood charts an impressive course through this demanding journey. That he carries the reader with him despite its complexities and inevitable frustrations is no doubt because the book is not only intellectually engaging and immensely thought-provoking; it is also, surprisingly, very moving.

Of course, the subject matter itself cannot but deeply touch the psychoanalytic reader. Further, Hinshelwood's contact with his subject and with his reader is never a purely intellectual one. From the first page to the last this is a very personal book, imbued with its author's humanity, thoughtfulness and generosity of spirit. This applies both to the way he treats his subject and to the way he treats his reader. In contrast to so many authors of interdisciplinary works, Hinshelwood never leaves his reader alone and never takes for granted his reader's knowledge or even memory: he defines his terms clearly and consistently, carefully introduces new units in the book, summarises previous ones and makes explicit the links between them. The result is a clarity quite exceptional for an interdisciplinary work, which helps to minimise a major difficulty inherent in such works—the inevitable unfamiliarity of all but the most exceptional readers with all the subjects discussed. What this means is that you don't have to be a philosopher to follow the philosophical argumentation; you are held through it by an author who cares for but never patronises his reader.

Wells Goodrich

Wells Goodrich

Box 3200-E, Hillsboro, VA 20134

Wells Goodrich  

Over twenty years ago James F. Masterson and his close associates developed a training programme for inexperienced psychodynamically oriented psychotherapists, teaching them how to treat borderline personality disorders. This training programme is now known as the Masterson Institute. Over time, the group has gradually expanded and matured. The theoretical orientation of the group has been towards developmental, self- and object-relations concepts, often with a focus on the separation-individuation phase of development as it expresses itself in the transference and countertransference. The teaching has been consistent and systematic.

Following Masterson & Rinsley's paper on ‘The borderline syndrome’ (1975, Int. J. Psychoanal., 56: 163-177) Masterson produced four more books on psychotherapeutic technique which dealt with transference and countertransference issues manifested in treatment with narcissistic and borderline patients (1972, 1976, 1981, 1983).

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Princess Who Did Not Smile: A Clinical Account of the Development of Primitive Psychic Structure in a 4-Year-Old Child

Torhild Leira

Torhild Leira

Bjerkelundsvn. 84 B
N-1340 Bekkestua
Norway

Torhild Leira  

On the basis of clinical phenomena observed in the treatment of a 4-year-old child, the author attempts to show that the formation of psychic structure at a primitive level of development does occur when certain conditions are fulfilled. Through listening in a specific way, i.e. looking for form in the clinical material, it was possible to recognise key points in the treatment process. Such key points were often heralded by new therapeutic material, which in turn was accompanied by some new quality in the therapeutic relationship. On the basis of some of these key points it was possible to describe a sequence of levels, which could be regarded as levels in the formation of psychic structure. The therapeutic interplay in the phases of treatment that are described occurred mainly through non-verbal affective exchanges in which the analyst contributed to the process by the quality of her presence. The non-verbal interplay between the patient and the analyst took on the quality of an interactive language that seemed necessary in the initial phase of treatment. It is assumed that it was this quality in the therapeutic relationship that led to the formation and development of psychic structure.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

From Simplicity Through Contradiction to Paradox: The Evolving Psychic Reality of the Borderline Patient in Treatment

Salman Akhtar

Salman Akhtar

Jefferson Medical College
1201 Chestnut Street, Suite 1400C
Philadelphia
PA 19107-4192

Salman Akhtar  

The thesis of this paper is two-fold. First, it proposes that the psychic reality of the borderline patient evolves in a three-step process during the analytic treatment. These steps include: (i) experiencing and presenting disparate psychic states without the awareness of inherent contradictions, (ii) observing, acknowledging, and mending the splits responsible for these contradictions, and (iii) developing a capacity for feeling and accepting paradox, i.e. coexistence of multiple meanings at different levels of abstraction. Second, the author suggests that this three-step movement vis-à-vis the associative material takes place first within the analyst's reverie and then in the patient's psychic reality. The analyst's holding, unmasking, bridging and depth-rendering interventions (and the vicissitudes of identification) make the patient's advance possible. The author presents two clinical vignettes, highlighting these hypotheses and their technical implications. A vignette from the non-clinical realm of a mother's interaction with her children is also included in the hope of demonstrating a developmental ‘Anlage’ of the analyst's interventions outlined above. The paper concludes with the observation that the practising analyst also needs to traverse a ‘simplicity-contradiction-paradox’ sequence in his view of the divergent theoretical approaches to the analytic understanding and treatment of severe character pathology.

Moses and Civilization: The Meaning Behind Freud's Myth. By Robert A. Paul.

New Haven: Yale University Press. 1996. Pp. 268

Theodore Shapiro

Theodore Shapiro

525 East 68th Street, New York, NY 10021

Theodore Shapiro  

Psychoanalysis has survived the anthropologists' dismissal of Freud's Primal Horde Myth and, with some apologies, has backed off from the application to groups of ‘facts’ learned from the psychoanalysis of patients. However, the broad application of the clinical psychoanalytic method to the larger cultural arena was as much a part of Freud's original work as was his pursuit of understanding of the mind and man's inner conflict. Now Robert Paul serves as Freud's advocate to represent his position on anthropology almost a hundred years later.

Dr Paul, a cultural anthropologist who has been educated as a psychoanalyst as well, has written an exciting reinterpretation of the Primal Horde Myth and has used the myth to help us understand the Bible and various religious practices. I was initially a sceptical reader, but became so fascinated with Paul's thesis that I not only read on but longed for the next chapter to see where Paul would take me in his reinterpretation of the Pentateuch and, ultimately, the New Testament and the ritual of the Eucharist.

Paul carefully states his aim as follows: ‘to show how the concept of unconscious guilt can be put to the service of an understanding of how the compelling authority of the law arises’ (p. 72). From this central thesis, Paul revives our interest in Freud's Primal Horde as a generative myth that he uses to interpret the tale of the Exodus as a transitional watershed marking the shift from a pre-moral primal state of man into civilisation and the establishment of the moral order.

Paul notes: ‘I regard Freud's myth of the Primal Horde as being an analysis, if perhaps unwitting, of the central narrative around which the Judeo-Christian tradition is organized’ (p. 17). Using various techniques of ‘decomposition’ and applying the mechanisms of dream work, Paul shows that the central elements of male-female and senior male-junior male, become the formulaic background of the Mosaic narrative. He then carefully uses the Midrash ‘as the unconscious of the tale’. Its various versions, explanations, and arguments, like free association, are viewed as a way to convince the reader of the meaning of Biblical texts. The profound impact of the narrative of the Exodus is seen in what became the future practices of Judaism and Christianity, the ritual Seder of Passover and the Eucharist meal of communion.

responseSet.title: ‘The Changing Aims of Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy: An Integrative Perspective’ by Jeremy Holmes
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

‘The Changing Aims of Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy: An Integrative Perspective’ by Jeremy Holmes

Paul Williams

Paul Williams

Flat 1, 15 Rosslyn Hill
London NW3 5UJ

Paul Williams  

Jeremy Holmes is a psychiatrist and psychoanalytic psychotherapist who works in the British National Health Service. He has written extensively on the political, economic and clinical challenges associated with the provision of psychoanalytic therapy, particularly in a public service setting. His paper was made available for free downloading at the IJPA Web Site at http://www.ijpa.org on 28 April 1998 (complete issues of the International Journal are now also available for free viewing via the same site address). Unusually for this discussion group, the initial response to the paper was slow—in fact, silent—and this is taken up by a number of contributors (see below). There then ensued an unusually long discussion!

What follows is only a précis of Holmes's paper: the full paper must be read if its detailed argument is to be understood. Holmes is concerned to relate traditional psychoanalytic ideas regarding aims and outcomes to the financial, theoretical and technical factors that affect and characterise contemporary psychoanalytic psychotherapy. For example, there is an emphasis today on splitting rather than repression, on issues of attachment and on achieving realistic goals. Cognitive behavioural psychotherapy shows possible areas of overlap with psychoanalytic psychotherapy. Attachment theory proposes that patient-therapist attunement is likely to be a key variable in outcome (Holmes wishes to stress the importance of this attachment perspective). He opens by quoting John Stuart Mill, who never renounced Bentham's utilitarianism, but who at the same time sensed that a society that aimed directly at happiness would be unlikely to achieve it. For Mill, autonomy and choice were goods in themselves, producing benefits as important as pleasure. Happiness, he believed, could not be legislated for: it was always a by-product of some other activity. Holmes sees parallels in the Bentham-Mill divide with the problem of aims and ‘aimlessness’ in the current debate within psychoanalytic psychotherapy. Contemporary health services exist in a Benthamite world of objectives and outcome measures and psychotherapy is attempting to adjust to this situation (mainly in its cognitive-behavioural therapy form). Psychoanalysis has always been suspicious of frameworks of narrowly defined science. Wallerstein emphasised goallessness as a tool of analytic work and that psychoanalysis posits uniquely far-reaching goals in terms of personality reorganisation. Holmes's paper may be seen as a commentary on Wallerstein's paradoxical comments, from the perspective of psychoanalytic psychotherapy rather than psychoanalysis proper. Holmes believes that attachment theory may provide the empirically based ideas that can integrate diverse therapeutic approaches.

responseSet.title: Consuming the Dead: A Kleinian Perspective on Death Rituals Cross-Culturally
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Consuming the Dead: A Kleinian Perspective on Death Rituals Cross-Culturally

Michele Stephen

Michele Stephen

Melbourne
Australia

Michele Stephen  

The author argues that Melanie Klein's theories of mourning shed light on certain funerary practices encountered widely in ethnographic literature, namely ‘second burial’. Pointing out that the death of a loved person is experienced in fantasy as the destruction of the internalised mother imago, the author shows how various Kleinian processes involved in infantile fears of maternal loss—such as persecutory anxiety, guilt, depression, and attempts at reparation—are clearly expressed in rituals of mourning cross-culturally. The argument is illustrated with two extensive case studies, Bali (Indonesia) and the Mekeo of coastal Papua New Guinea. A number of other cultures are considered briefly to indicate the relevance of Kleinian theory to the symbolism of death rituals more broadly, including the role played by sorcery and witchcraft beliefs, fears of malevolent ghosts, repeated re-burial, mortuary gift exchange, cremation, mortuary cannibalism, and the denial of death in modern western funeral rites.

responseSet.title: Leo Stone (1903-1997)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Leo Stone (1903-1997)

Jacob Arlow

Jacob Arlow

94 Wildwood Road
Great Neck
New York
NY 11024

Jacob Arlow  

Dr Leo Stone, who died this past summer at the age of 94, occupied a special place in the American psychoanalytic movement. The affection and respect he was accorded bordered on reverence. He served as president both of the New York Psychoanalytic Society and the Institute, was a recipient of the Sigourney Award, and delivered both the Brill and Freud Lectures, in addition to many other tokens of distinction. His contributions to the literature of psychoanalysis, small in number as they may have been, were most influential. His ideas, in fact, may be considered as a point of departure for many of the current technical innovations in psychoanalytic treatment, those that emphasise the contribution of the analyst's personality in the treatment process. Central to his approach to technique was the idea of the analyst as a healer.

Starting with his paper on ‘The widening scope of indications for psychoanalysis’ (1954b) and ‘The psychoanalytic situation and transference’ (1967), a paper which he designated ‘Postscript to an earlier communication’, and in practically all of his subsequent contributions, Stone emphasised the role of the analyst as a benign, nurturing, helpful force. Fundamental to Stone's view of the therapeutic relationship was the idea that it had its roots in the very early, protective, nurturing, sustaining dependency on the mother or other caretakers. This was the baseline, according to Stone, from which all other considerations of technique derived.

In an earlier contribution, both witty and scholarly, ‘On the principal obscene word of the English language (an inquiry with hypothesis regarding its origin and persistence)’ (1954a), Stone traced the origin and the persistence of the word ‘fuck’ to its source in the word ‘suck’. The same line of approach may be found in one of his later papers (Stone, 1986), on the origin of depressive illness. There he states the view that the commonly emphasised elements entering into the formation of depressive illness, e.g. pathological narcissism, aggression towards the primary object, ambivalent aggression and ‘identification’ with the disappointing object, are in varying degrees important in most, if not all, cases of pure depressive illness. He goes on to say, ‘While each of these factors is a dynamic entity in its own right, they operate synergistically in the complex of depressive illness. This synergistic relation may derive in part from the common origin of these dynamic elements in the same or repeated disturbances of neonatal experience’ (p. 359).

responseSet.title: Hitchcock's Vertigo: the collapse of a rescue fantasy, by Emanuel Berman (Int. J. Psychoanal., 78: 975-996).
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Hitchcock's Vertigo: the collapse of a rescue fantasy, by Emanuel Berman (Int. J. Psychoanal., 78: 975-996).

Danielle Quinodoz

Danielle Quinodoz

53 A chemin des Fourches
1223 Cologny (Genève)
Switzerland

Danielle Quinodoz  

Dear Sir,

Hitchcock's film Vertigo leads us to question the relationship between reality and fantasies through the symptom of vertigo. I am especially interested in this film because I have studied the psychic meanings of vertigo presented by several patients during their psychoanalysis (D. Quinodoz, 1990, Vertigo and object relationship, Int. J. Psychoanal., 71: 53-63; 1994, Emotional Vertigo, between Anxiety

391

and Pleasure, trans. A. Pomerans. London and New York: Routledge, 1997).

The starting point of Scotty's mountain-vertigo is not a pathological one. If we are standing by an abyss we can have vertigo without it being the sign of a pathological disease. Mountain-vertigo appears when different information coming from sensorial organs involved in equilibrium is in contradiction and so difficult to coordinate. For instance, someone standing by an abyss, feels the ground stable under his feet, but at the same time, sees it being one thousand meters below. These two pieces of information are contradictory and we may experience an impression of vertigo. But, generally, someone suffering from this sort of vertigo can learn to control his vertigo. He can learn to eliminate or relativise the extraneous information. But some people, like Scotty, cannot control their mountain-vertigo any more, because they are also suffering from another vertigo, an emotional vertigo from psychic origin. This psychic vertigo, like the somatic one, is linked with contradictory information, but it is emotional information, not somatic. It is what happens to Scotty.

In the first part of the film, the spectator's point of view is merged with Scottie's point of view; like Scotty, the spectator is overwhelmed with contradictory information: what is real? Are the events happening to Madeleine real? Magic? Madness? Are they fantasies? Lies? A plot?

At first the detective Scotty refuses to take care of Madeleine, because he only considers objective facts as real. But in a second time Scotty is overwhelmed by contradictory considerations, some coming from the external reality and some coming from a magic world in him: he sees Madeleine at the window and realises that she is not here; Carlotta is dead, but she acts in Madeleine's life; he doubts: infantile omnipotence (everything is possible) and objective reality are fighting in him. Scotty cannot link these pieces of information because they are incompatible and this is why he has vertigo.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Film Review Essay: I Have Not Spoken: Silence in the Piano

Bruce Sklarew

Bruce Sklarew

5480 Wisconsin Avenue #211
Chevy Chase
MD 20815

Bruce Sklarew  

The Piano (1993)

Director: Jane Campion

Distributed by Live Home Video Inc.

Ada (Holly Hunter) meets Stewart (Sam Neill), her husband by an arranged marriage, on a beach full of foreboding in the dream-like wilderness of mid-nineteenth century New Zealand. Arriving by ship from Scotland, she has waited overnight with her 9-year-old daughter, Flora. Her most precious possession, a piano, is left on the beach by the awkward and unempathic Stewart. It is later rescued by Baines (Harvey Keitel), another settler, in an exchange for land with Stewart. Feigning interest in piano lessons from Ada, Baines offers to return the piano to her in exchange for sexual favours. Eventually they fall in love. When Flora reveals to Stewart that her mother's affair continues, Stewart chops off part of Ada's finger. Having relinquished hope of gaining Ada's love, Stewart allows Ada and Flora to leave with Baines, and they establish a family.

This voice-over accompanies establishing shots of Ada's life in Scotland before her journey to New Zealand.

The voice you hear is not my speaking voice, but my

mind's voice.

I have not spoken since I was 6 years old. No one

knows why, not even me. My father says it is a dark

talent and the day

I take it into my head to stop breathing will be my last.

responseSet.title: ‘Triangulation, One's Own Mind and Objectivity’
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

‘Triangulation, One's Own Mind and Objectivity’

Arnold Goldberg

Arnold Goldberg

122 South Michigan Avenue
Suite 1305B
Chicago, IL 60603

Arnold Goldberg  

Dear Sir,

I would like to offer a rejoinder to Marcia Cavell's very informative and persuasive article entitled ‘Triangulation, one's own mind and objectivity’, in which she argued for the concept of a real, shared, external world. My effort involves a thought experiment.

Imagine you are in total darkness with no smells or sounds or any sensation at all. Just for a moment. Then the lights go on and you see it all and smell and hear as well: the whole world. Now that is, of necessity and just for the moment, your world. It is not solipsistic, since you surely do not deny that it is a real honest-to-goodness world. It is the world and it is filled with people. Now along comes Dr K, who tells you that you see the world wrong. He advises you that the correct way to (say) get along with people is to do it as he does. If it turns out that it indeed succeeds in improving your relationships, then it works, and so can be said to be correct and true. However, Dr K is convinced that he sees it—the whole world—right, and you see it wrong, and surely though this is true of getting along, it need not be so of other things. So you conclude that Dr K has one version of the world which is his world, and you have another. You certainly cannot see that there is some absolute Dr K-type truth out there, but rather just a whole hunch of relative renditions. Some work better than others, and those that work best can be called true, or real or correct, since you make no claim as K does. So far you probably agree.

1222

responseSet.title: ‘The Theory of Seduction and the Problem of the Other’ by Jean Laplanche
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

‘The Theory of Seduction and the Problem of the Other’ by Jean Laplanche

Paul Williams

Paul Williams

Flat 1, 15 Rosslyn Hill
London NW3 5UJ

Paul Williams  

Laplanche's paper was made available for free downloading via the IJPA Internet Site (http://www.ijpa.org) on 15 July 1997. The ensuing discussion was not as extensive as has followed earlier papers, and the question of what evokes responses in subscribers is an important one that the discussion group may wish to consider.

In a lucid paper, Laplanche surveyed his general theory of seduction, addressing the origins of the psychic apparatus and drives in terms of the asymmetry of the sexual relation between adult and infant. The sexual message to the infant originates, crucially, in the adult other: the implications of this primacy of the other for sublimation and for psychoanalytic treatment are reviewed.

Laplanche began his argument by reminding the reader that Western philosophy has always stumbled over the problem of the other, whose essential ‘otherness’ has been recognised not as foundational or originary but persistently as a facet of subjective representation. It fell to psychoanalysis, through the invocation of a ‘Copernican revolution’, to ascertain the nature of the other, which is evident in the extreme otherness of the unconscious. The quality and dimension of otherness inherent in human existence does not recede with the passing of childhood but becomes a lifelong companion visible in an array of guises such as dreams, persecution, sublimation, transference etc. Yet there remains a further, pressing conceptual task, Laplanche stated; namely, the explication of the founding of internal otherness upon external otherness (this, he maintains, was the hidden implication of the seduction theory).

Laplanche began his summary of the nature of this dialectical relationship by citing first the unbridgeable differences between the original, self-centred gravitational system espoused by Ptolemy and the ensuing, other-centred, Copernican perspective of which psychoanalysis became a paradigm. The Ptolemaic false hypothesis, he reminded us, was shored up for a millennium by increasingly complex, ‘corrective’ adjustments that served ultimately to demonstrate its theoretical sterility. With this historical picture in mind Laplanche pondered on the fate of Freudian and post-Freudian metapsychology. No psychoanalyst envisages, it would appear, the rebuilding of the Freudian system: new concepts are added periodically by various psychoanalytic schools as difficulties arise, all of whom claim to leave the central Freudian corpus intact, yet rarely is there consideration of the degree of coherence of new developments with the whole.

responseSet.title: A Cat as Fetish: A Contribution to the Theory of Fetishism
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A Cat as Fetish: A Contribution to the Theory of Fetishism

Edward Nersessian

Edward Nersessian

72 East 91st Street
New York
NY 10128

Edward Nersessian  

Since Freud's original study of fetishism and his description of the genesis of the symptom, many additions and elaborations have been made to those propositions. In the past decade, fetishism has been increasingly applied to a wide variety of behaviour and mentation, some with no overt sexual component. In this paper, the author, drawing on his observations made during a very lengthy analysis of a female patient, underscores the need to broaden the applicability of the diagnosis of fetishism but sees value in limiting it to the sexual situation. He suggests that a fetish does not need to be an inanimate object and expresses agreement with those who feel that fetishism is not limited to males. Additionally, he offers for consideration the idea that fetishism belongs on a continuum with a whole gamut of phenomena such as the need for a transitional object, the use of amulets, and the endowment of magical properties to words, ideas, rituals and objects. All these phenomena have in common the use of an outside object bestowed by magic and illusion to control anxiety. The hierarchical level of such anxiety and its ideational content (oedipal versus pre-oedipal) are of less causative value than the ego's inability to master it without resorting to an outside object.

responseSet.title: Sexuality and Object Love: Chaired by Michael Parsons, London
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Sexuality and Object Love

Chaired by Michael Parsons, London

James Rose

James Rose

12 Drylands Road
London N8 9HN

James Rose  

The three panellists, from North and South and old and new worlds, brought together by the Congress to discuss this matter, brought very widely ranging perspectives. Yet despite these differences, they found there was much to agree upon and, at the same time, many divergences. The first panellist, Matilde Ureta Caplansky, sought to offer some ideas on female sexuality and object relationships. In essence, she sought to show that the nature of the initial relationship with the primary object—the mother—was fundamental to the form and process of object relationships later in life. But, when focusing on women, she took the position that penis envy should be considered as part of being female. This apparent desire to be like a man appears as a ‘motor element’ of becoming a woman. She emphasised that penis envy is always the envy of an idealised penis and that to be a woman entailed, in addition to the search for a penis, a separation from the pregenital mother. This task needed to be achieved without damage to either her or her maternal image.

She considered this interpretation of penis envy to be an aspect that had been little emphasised. This concerned the feminine need for individuality and autonomy that is an aspect closely related to the stability and maturity attained in the initial maternal relationship. Such is the significance of the initial object relationship that the subsequent ‘refinding of the object’ in adolescence will be heavily influenced by the early experience. To illustrate her points, Caplansky brought material from the analysis of a 36-year-old woman. The significant event in this patient's life was her mother's death from cancer when she was 12 years old. After that, her life became ‘erratic and anguish filled’.

This patient, M, sought help because of acute feelings of anxiety, a poor marital relationship and depression. She presented as a sensitive, vulnerable and desolate creature who attached herself in an adhesive way to her analyst. She showed the precariousness of her inner world by lying so close to the edge of the couch that she gave the impression of being about to fall off. The clinical material centred around a dream. The patient reported that she had had a ‘very strange dream about a little baby’. The strange thing was that it was born in a different way, as if it came from somewhere else, she didn't know where. She was taking it out of the clinic thinking how wonderful it would be if the baby was hers. She got it home and it slipped from her, as it kept on doing. She experienced the dream as a comical one. The telling of the dream brought back a memory of her father buying her first pair of high-heeled shoes that marked her first sight of the land of womanhood. She said ‘I felt beautiful for the first time in my life’.

responseSet.title: Truffaut and the Failure of Introjection
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Truffaut and the Failure of Introjection

Simona Argentieri

Simona Argentieri

Via Giovan Battista Martini 6
00198 Rome

Simona Argentieri  

My reflections are on two films by François Truffaut: L'homme qui aimait les femmes (‘The Man Who Loved Women’, 1977) and La chambre verte (‘The Green Room’, 1978), produced within a year of each other. At a first viewing, they seem to be two very different works. La chambre verte, dramatic and painful, is based on a sombre short story by Henry James, ‘The Altar of the Dead’, and on two other of his works, ‘The Beast in the Jungle’ and ‘The Friends of Friends’. It is the story of Julian Davenne, a ‘virtuoso’ in necrology, and his passionate loving obsession for one woman alone—his wife who died young—even after her death and until his own death. The other film, L'homme qui aimait les femmes, is a delightfully ironic comedy of the autobiographical type, in which the protagonist continually falls in love with every woman he meets, and in which there is the hint of a delightful touch of foot and leg fetishism.

Both films, however, in spite of the great differences in language, style and narrative, basically tell the same story of the incapacity to love and to make one's own internal impulse coincide with the encounter with a real person. The protagonist of L'homme qui aimait les femmes does not know how to distinguish or to choose, in a giddy erotic round of seduction, unfaithfulness and disillusion in which the illusion is repeatedly rekindled. In La chambre verte, Julian is imprisoned in a repetitive trap of an opposite kind. Tenaciously faithful to the image of one woman alone, he cannot conceive of a love object unless it is eternally identical to itself. Whether in a state of immobility or in flight, both characters are living outside of real time, enslaved by an obsession.

In La chambre verte, Truffaut's outstanding intuition lies in showing us how the impossibility of completing the mourning process coincides with the impossibility of loving. The drama of Julian Davenne, a kind of ‘platonic’ necrophile, is not so much the sorrow of having lost his real wife; it is the suffering caused by not being able to keep his love for her alive within himself, and his fear of losing the remembrance of her, of being unable to keep her image in his mind. The many photographs and portraits with which he surrounds himself are fragile, inadequate substitutes for the memory, and his obsessive worship of them is the symptom of the precariousness of his internal symbolic world. This is dramatically illustrated by the grotesque failure of the wax figure that he tries to make as a ‘faithful’ copy of his dead wife, but that turns out to be only a useless fetish. Or in the scene of his first conversation with Cecilie, the woman who tries to bring him back to love and to life as she tenderly recalls their first meeting long ago, while he—who lives in the cult of the past—remembers nothing.

responseSet.title: Obituary: Mark Kanzer 1908 - 1998
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Obituary: Mark Kanzer 1908 - 1998

Marvin Nierenberg

Marvin Nierenberg

15 West 81st Street
New York
NY 10024

Marvin Nierenberg  

At the age of 89 years, Dr Mark Kanzer died of congestive heart failure in Rye Brook, New York on 14 April 1998. He received his BSc from Yale University, an MA in psychology from Harvard University and a medical degree from the University of Berlin. His analytic training was at the New York Psychoanalytic Institute, from which he graduated in 1943. During World War II, in the US Army, he was Assistant Director of the School of Military Neuropsychiatry in San Antonio, Texas. He was married to Viva Schatia, (d. 1971), a psychiatrist, and the couple had two sons, Paul (d. 1974) and Alan, an attorney in New York city.

A prolific author and esteemed psychoanalytic educator, Dr Kanzer was a founding member in 1950 of the Division of Psychoanalytic Education of the Downstate Medical Center of the State University of New York (now the NYU Psychoanalytic Institute) of which he served as director from 1962 to 1965. In addition, he was a founder of the Psychoanalytic Association of New York. He was a delegate to the Executive Council and the Board of Professional Standards of the American Psychoanalytic Association for seventeen years and participated in many panel discussions at the scientific meetings of that organisation. Among more than 150 articles written by Dr Kanzer, many focused on clinical and technical subjects (‘The past and present in the transference’, ‘The communicative function of the dream’, ‘Visual communication in the analytic situation’), literary subjects (‘Dostoyevsky's matricidal impulses’, ‘The passing of the Oedipus complex in Greek drama’) and studies of Freud's history and works (‘The transference neurosis of the Rat Man’, ‘Sigmund and Alexander Freud on the Acropolis’, ‘Freud's uses of the terms “autoerotism” and “narcissism”’). In addition, Dr Kanzer was the editor of one book, The Unconscious Today (1970) and the co-editor of two others with Dr Jules Glenn, Freud and his Self-Analysis (1979) and Freud and his Patients (1980).

Dr Kanzer was also a philanthropist, establishing in 1974 the Mark and Viva Kanzer Fund at Yale University, which promoted interchange between psychoanalysts, scholars and students of the humanities. This was accomplished through an annual Freud Lecture, monthly interdisciplinary seminars, and fellowships for graduate students. He later funded enhanced services for the Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association, on whose editorial board he had served for nine years. In 1980, fellowships were established by him at the Sigmund Freud Center in Jerusalem. Dr Kanzer also supported the school for children of immigrants at the kibbutz Ein Hashloch, Israel, which his father had established in 1957.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Klein-Lacan Dialogues. Edited by Bernard Burgoyne and Mary Sullivan.

London: Rebus Press. 1997. Pp. 228

Giuseppina Antinucci-Mark

Giuseppina Antinucci-Mark

14 Montenotte Road, London N8 8RL

Giuseppina Antinucci-Mark  

The Higher Education Network for Research and Information on Psychoanalysis is an organisation that was founded in 1988 to provide a forum for the discussion and exchange of psychoanalytic ideas in Britain and abroad. In the academic year 1994-95 THERIP arranged a series of panel discussions on topics addressed by Kleinian and Lacanian analysts, which have been edited for this collection. The format of these dialogues centres around the presentation of two papers, followed by a wider discussion. The lectures cover the following topics: child analysis, interpretation and technique, phantasy, sexuality, countertransference and the unconscious, with contributions by Margaret Rustin, Bice Benvenuto, Catalina Bronstein, Bernard Burgoyne, Robert Young. Darian Leader, Jane Temperley, Dany Nobus, Robert Hinshelwood, Vicente Palomera, Robin Anderson and Filip Geerardyn.

A brief interview with Donald Meltzer and a lucid, systematic and disciplined paper entitled ‘Rethinking Kleinian interpretation: what difference does it make?’ conclude this collection.

As is often the case with compilations, it is an arduous task to give the reader a synthetic and critical account of the book. Therefore I can only focus on some of the papers in some detail. Nevertheless, wanting to give an impressionistic view of the structure of the papers, I would say that the Kleinian writers follow a format that the British readers will find familiar, where a theoretical exposition is followed by a brief review and finally some observational or clinical material is given to support the theory. On the other hand, the Lacanian contributors emphasise the logic of difference and separation, in keeping with their theoretical focus on the symbolic and language, which create discontinuity and diversification. They handle clinical material by offering a Lacanian reading of cases already published in psychoanalytic literature.

Under the heading ‘Technique and interpretation in Klein’ Bronstein declares herself in agreement with Isaacs's interpretation of Klein's notion of the ubiquity of unconscious phantasy. Furthermore, linking the transference with unconscious phantasy, of which the former represents an externalisation in the here-and-now in the analysis, Bronstein reaches a nodal point of Kleinian theory, making a claim for the possibility of knowing the unconscious.

responseSet.title: ‘Transitions in psychic structures in the light of deterministic chaos theory’
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

‘Transitions in psychic structures in the light of deterministic chaos theory’

Matthias von der Tann OTHER

Matthias von der Tann

8 Elsworthy Terrace
London NW3 3DR

Matthias von der Tann  

Dear Sir,

Quinodoz (IJPA, 78: 699-719) postulates that the psyche behaves like a chaotic system and thus according to a sensitive dependency to initial conditions. He writes: ‘In my view, sensitivity to initial conditions is indeed the characteristic feature of the unpredictability of human behaviour and psychic functioning. For example, it is never possible to tell in advance what course an individual's thoughts will take, how he will decide a particular issue or in what way he will act, because even seemingly minor causes may have unexpected effects … It is only after the thought has entered the mind, the decision has been taken or the action performed that one can retrospectively reconstruct the succession of psychic events that have brought about a specific thought, decision or action, and the initial conditions from which the divergence stemmed will be discoverable only afterwards' (p. 704). It is not easy to understand how this can be brought together with the fact that sensitive dependency to initial conditions in chaotic systems always destroys history and makes reconstruction impossible. Here the unpredictability in one direction goes together with the impossibility of reconstruction in the other (Küppers, 1996, p. 171).

Furthermore, Quinodoz claims to show ‘that the unconscious fantasies constitute an organising structure that has all the characteristics of a fractal object as observed in strange attractors’ (pp. 706-7). As all fractal objects are geometrical objects, this is difficult to conceive. An unconscious fantasy is, I guess, not a geometrical object; it is rather something like a concept certainly to be identified on different ‘scales’ in the consulting room (cf. Galatzer-Levy, 1995). But does this suffice to see it as equivalent to a ‘strange attractor?’

Quinodoz goes on to state that the stability of a system, its capability ‘of damping out perturbations of the system and of thereby maintaining a certain stability’, is related to the dimension of the attractor (p. 707). This is equivalent to stating that a system would gain stability relative to the number of variables by which it can be influenced. Applying this notion to the difference between animals and humans regarding the possibility of understanding human language, a cartoon comes into my mind.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Samlade skrifter av Sigmund Freud [The Collected Works of Sigmund Freud] Volumes I-IV.

Stockholm: Natur och Kultur. 1996-98. Pp. 640, 714, 653, 655

Henning Paikin

Henning Paikin

Kildevaenget 37, DK 2100 Copenhagen 0

Henning Paikin  

To publish Freud's Collected Works in Swedish amounts to a cultural event in Scandinavia. In an international context, Swedish is a linguistically small area (even though Swedish can be read and understood in the other two Scandinavian countries, Denmark and Norway), but the above publication has a number of qualities that justify mention in this journal. Also, any new translation of Freud's work is in itself a contribution to the current debate concerning the specific problems associated with the translation of Freud's language.

For the past twenty years, beginning with Mahony (1977), Ornston (1982) and Bettelheim (1983), there has been a critical focus on Strachey's Standard Edition. The literature on the translation of Freud into English is so voluminous that a survey would be outside the scope of this review; a few comments will suffice.

An often heard criticism is that Freud's particular language and style have not been taken sufficiently into consideration. Freud used the living German everyday language with all its emotional connotations and ambiguities, while Strachey in his translation, as is well known, to a certain extent chose Latinised artificial words. As Bettelheim says, the result was that Freud's texts lost their soul (one might even say that they became ‘mindless’!). To mention just one example, the personal ‘Ich’ [I] became the impersonal abstract ‘Ego’, with many fewer associations.

Even though it may be a truism—especially for analysts—and has often been mentioned in various contexts, it should be emphasised again that any translation is an interpretation. And any interpretation is made on the basis of specific basic assumptions, be they scientific, cultural, historical or linguistic.

Furthermore, Freud's texts are special because Freud himself was a stylistic master, and because, imperceptibly, he crossed the borderline between natural science and the humanities. Strachey turned psychoanalysis into some kind of natural science (which Freud himself to a certain extent may have wanted), but whenever Freud wrote or treated his patients, he was a humanist.

responseSet.title: On Borderline Phenomena
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On Borderline Phenomena

Shailesh Kapadia

Shailesh Kapadia

3/42 Nanik Niwas
B. Desai Road
Bombay 4000026

Shailesh Kapadia  

The author describes his struggle to resolve some theoretical and technical problems posed by borderline patients. For this he has made use of an ancient Indian myth of King Trishanku taken from Bhagwat Puran—the Hindu Book of Genesis. He has tried to demonstrate, step by step, how fruitfully this myth has helped him to understand such patients. Special emphasis has been laid on the language used by borderline patients. The contrary and apparently meaningless nature of this language is likened to the formulation of King Trishanku's unique desire, ‘to go to heaven without actually dying’. The author then proceeds to show that this language is not quite as meaningless as it appears to be—that it is the result of a complicated system of projective identification devised by these patients to manipulate their objects in order to achieve their own omnipotent ends. Detailed clinical examples are given to describe the handling of the problems of appropriation, manipulation and consequent suspended state of such patients. Taken from three different stages of various analyses these examples show how a very slow and gradual change was brought about in the mental structures of the patients treated by the author.
