responseSet.title: Contemporary Review of Psychosexual Phases of Development: Chaired by Inga Villareal, Bogotá
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Contemporary Review of Psychosexual Phases of Development

Chaired by Inga Villareal, Bogotá

Susan Davison

Susan Davison

43 Rosendale Road
London SE21 8DY

Susan Davison  

Freud's original theory of psychosexual development has never been without its critics (Jung, 1912; Fairbairn, 1949). In the century that has passed since Freud first formulated his theory of infantile sexuality and its relation to adult neurosis, the cultural and scientific contexts of psychoanalysis have altered immeasurably. Although still mainly embedded in Judaeo-Christian cultures, psychoanalysis has spread throughout Europe to the Americas and has evolved in many subtly different ways in the various places it has taken root and flourished.

As a discipline, psychoanalysis is able to tolerate a remarkable degree of heterogeneity, contradiction and obscurity concerning metapsychology while remaining true to the basic tenets of clinical practice (Wallerstein, 1998). Direct observation and experimentation in the field of infant and child development has brought a new rigour to the psychoanalytic theories of mental development (e.g. Stern, 1985).

Our presenters, from three continents, each in their way pointed to contemporary cultural and scientific developments as they challenge psychoanalytic ideas about gender identity, human sexuality and the centrality of infantile sexuality in organising psychic structure and development.

In his paper ‘On phallic organisation’ Pentti Ikonen proposed that it is at this level of pregenital organisation, as it mimics mature genital sexuality, that violence most readily enters into the sexual act. The ‘phallic’ child's version of the primal scene is one of violent penetration, or from the feminine position being broken into, without mutuality. He suggested that the phallic organisation, because of the compensations it offers to the humiliations of the Oedipus complex, very readily presents itself as a defensive organisation. Indeed, he asked whether the entire phallic libidinal organisation could be a defensive variation on the genital advances of childhood, employing omnipotence, contempt, violence and self-sufficiency in an essentially masturbatory act in which the other is treated as a mere accessory.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Balancing Empathy and Interpretation: Relational Character Analysis. By Lawrence Josephs.

New Jersey: Jason Aronson. 1995. Pp. 435 + xxxii

Mark Gehrie

Mark Gehrie

122 South Michigan Avenue, Suite 1300
Chicago, IL 60603

Mark Gehrie  

This volume attempts many things: it is clearly a primer, a collection of introductory essays on psychoanalytic technique that are well documented and that will be of use to psychoanalytic candidates who wish for an informed overview of the evolution of psychoanalytic technique. These essays contain a heavy emphasis on what Josephs understands as a fundamental organising principle: a ‘dialectic’, expressed as the tension between opposing views of ‘character analysis’ on the one hand, and ‘empathy’ on the other. Throughout the volume there is intermingled, in addition, a philosophical essay focused on the dilemma that modern vs. postmodernist perspectives pose for psychoanalytic theory and technique; the author constantly poses one point of view against another, stating, re-stating, and often stating again the conflicts in theory and technique that are produced if we shift our view of the nature of the issue. It's a ‘Rashomon’ story, in which we are constantly shifting lenses to view and re-view the scene in question.

responseSet.title: Response
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Response

David Scharff

David Scharff

6612 Kennedy Drive
Chevy Chase
MD 20815

David Scharff  

Dear Sir,

We share Graham Clarke's interest in preserving the integrity of Fairbairn's model, and at the same time, in promoting it's evolution. So we welcome his thoughtful critique, which gives us an opportunity to clarify our ideas about extending Fairbairn's model.

We believe our modifications are not only consistent with the dynamic core of Fairbairn's contribution, but that they enhance the range of his model, answer some of the linguistic ambiguity of his argument, and provide a model for the meaning of synthesis and integration which he thought was at the heart of maturation and psychoanalytic growth.

Fairbairn's concept of the libidinal ego is complex. He found Freud's concept of libido problematic, as he did Freud's concept of the superego in its role as the agent of repression (Fairbairn, 1929a, b, 1930). Fairbairn used the term libidinal ego to denote a repressed area of the self tied painfully to an unsatisfying, and thus frustrating and over-stimulating, internal object. The outcome of the infant's unsatisfactory experience is the split off libidinal ego which, precisely because it is split off, is limited and so operates solely in a satisfaction-seeking mode. It is implicit in Fairbairn's model of endopsychic structure that the libidinal ego incorporates an inalienable part of the self, motivated by the need for self-affirming relationships throughout life. However Fairbairn never fully delineated this constellation. Our model is, perhaps more than anything, an attempt to address this problem.

Clarke argues against our revision of Fairbairn in three areas: first, he states that secondary repression, which Fairbairn described as the anti-libidinal ego's attack on the libidinal ego and object, was Fairbairn's equivalent of Freud's notion of repression. We do not completely agree: Fairbairn argued that the central ego itself is the agent of primary repression, not the superego as Freud had said. The central ego acts to split and repress libidinal and anti-libidinal objects because of the pain of these relationships, at first externally, and later inside the unsplit, pristine ego. Secondary repression, on the other hand, occurs because of continuing pain. Fairbairn (1952) most clearly described this condition in hysterical patients whose anti-libidinal egos attacked their libidinal longing because dependency was so painful for them. While this clinical description offered a new understanding of hysterical states, it could not explain the complementary state—also seen in some hysterical patients—in which a too-good-to-be true organization acts to further repress the anti-libidinal constellation. The alternation of these two internal situations is illustrated by the nursery rhyme of the hysterically seductive little girl whose little curl down the middle of her forehead neatly splits her experience: ‘When she was good/She was very, very good! And when she was bad! She was horrid’. That is Fairbairn's point: all the subsidiary ego and object organizations are in dynamic relationship to each other. Our model seeks to amplify this matter.

[[RunningHead]]

Nella stanza d'analisi. [In the analytic consulting room]. By Antonino Ferro.

Milan: Cortina. 1996. Pp. 206

Alberto Meotti

Alberto Meotti

Via Stefini 14, 20125 Milan

Alberto Meotti  

The author of this book, ‘In the analytic consulting room’, is a training analyst of the Italian Psychoanalytical Society, and the volume presents a rich collection of themes suffused with his living clinical experience. Dr Ferro has enlightened us before, in La tecnica nella psicoanalisi infantile (1992), on the techniques and clinical practice of child analysis. He has also published a large number of papers in many international journals. It is impossible to approach any of Ferro's writings—including the work under review—without being struck by his genuine, deep passion for clinical psychoanalytic practice and by the many stories and narrations that unfold within it. This volume offers the reader an alternating sequence of conceptual and theoretical considerations with frequent descriptions of clinical situations in which we can share. That is entertaining and at the same time makes for stimulating and involving reading.

The topics covered in this book include—among many others—the question of analysability (seen as the capacity of the analyst and of the analytic couple to allow themselves to be put to the test), termination (signalled not by contents but by the introjection of functions—in particular, the capacity to think), theories, models, the transference and relating.

Ferro's central ideas concern: the transformation of deep emotions by (a) their narration between characters, (b) sharing by virtue of a story and (c) elaboration by means of unsaturated interpretations; narrative interventions; consideration of characters as functional aggregates, i.e.

(Translated by Philip Slotkin, MA, MITI)

responseSet.documentRef: Nuetzel, E. J. (1998) Over-Exposure:: Terry Zwigoff's Crumb. International Journal of Psychoanalysis  79:1225-1227
responseSet.documentRefHTML: Nuetzel, E. J. (1998) Over-Exposure:: Terry Zwigoff's Crumb. International Journal of Psychoanalysis 79:1225-1227

responseSet.title: Over-Exposure:: Terry Zwigoff's Crumb
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Over-Exposure:

Terry Zwigoff's Crumb

Eric Nuetzel

Eric Nuetzel

4524 Forest Park Avenue
St. Louis
MO 63108

Eric Nuetzel  

Crumb (1994).

Directed by Terry Zwigoff.

Distributed by Sony Pictures Classics.

Documentary films complicate the task of psychoanalytic film criticism; they are renderings of fact, not fiction. They try not to traffic in the realm of fantasy, except as a subject, as they strive to disturb the viewer with difficult truths. Yet their aesthetic effect is under the influence of their film-maker's subjectivity. Conscious as well as unconscious identifications, transferences, countertransferences, and resistances inevitably complicate the film-maker's interpretation of events, and shape the presentation.

Terry Zwigoff's Crumb seems no exception. The film is, by design, a psychobiography, a film portrait of the famed underground cartoonist and sketch artist R. (for Robert) Crumb. Robert burst into American consciousness in the late 1960s with his hallucinogen inspired stream-of-consciousness cartoon style. With its own terrifying cuteness, Robert's art aims to skewer psychosocial and psycho-sexual hypocrisy. For better or for worse, his art is raw, honest and astonishingly revealing. In an interview after completion of the film, Zwigoff emphasised that he wanted to be as honest about Robert as Robert is in his own work, and added that if erred it was on the side of harshness (Sragow, 1995, ‘The man behind the man in Crumb. The New York Times, 23 April, Section H, pp. 13 & 20). A former psychology major and close friend of the artist, Zwigoff was in his own therapy as he exposed his famous friend on film. Zwigoff takes us into Robert's life, past as well as present, and into the heart of his deeply troubled family. His film is a disturbing meditation on creativity, trauma, character pathology, perversion, and the variability of psychopathology expressed in three brothers: Robert, his older brother Charles, and his younger brother Maxon. Two sisters would not participate. Robert found viewing the film ‘excruciating’ (Crumb & Crumb, 1995, p. 96, ‘Head for the hills! Trying to escape the “R. Crumb Thing”’. The New Yorker Magazine, 24 April, pp. 96-7).

responseSet.title: Las figuras de la madre [The forms of the mother].  Edited by Silvia Tubert.: Madrid: Ediciones Cátedra.  1996. Pp. 322
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Las figuras de la madre [The forms of the mother]. Edited by Silvia Tubert.

Madrid: Ediciones Cátedra. 1996. Pp. 322

Mechthild Zeul

Mechthild Zeul

Pza. Ciudad de Viena 6, Apto. 1017, 28040 Madrid
(Translated by Philip Slotkin, MA, MITI)

Mechthild Zeul  

This collective work is made up of contributions from women psychoanalysts, philologists, historians and anthropologists of various nationalities. The volume's editor seeks by this compilation to demonstrate the constructivistic character of motherhood as expressed in the Judaeo-Christian tradition of patriarchal societies extending from biblical times to present-day western civilisation. The book is an inventory of the history and praxis of the concept of motherhood as seen through feminist eyes. A central hypothesis of almost all the papers here assembled is that the female biological characters are the criterion adopted by patriarchal societies, which therefore once and for all dictate the identity of womanhood and motherhood, so that the former exists solely in terms of the latter. Yet a female debate and also female political activity have developed within the male-determined political and cultural arena. Their origins surely lie in the contributions of Simone de Beauvoir at the end of the 1940s and more particularly in the 1950s, as well as in the American feminist movement of the 1970s, whose demands and fighting strategies quickly spread to Europe. This aspect is merely touched upon in the book under review, whose principal aim is the deconstruction of motherhood as the fruit of the patriarchal order.

Two of the contributions depart from this basic orientation. Esther Sánchez-Prado Gonzales's study entitled ‘Virginia Woolf's mothers’ features both womanhood and motherhood in its description of Woolf's repeated attempts to recreate the dead mother in her writing—and, I would add, in Woolf's chosen mode of suicide, namely drowning in the River Ouse to achieve reunion with the dead mother. The ambivalence of the bond between mother and daughter also emerges here. Secondly, the female fighting strategy is exemplified in the paper on the mothers and grandmothers of the Plaza de Mayo in Buenos Aires searching for their children and grandchildren, murdered and caused to ‘disappear’ under the Argentinian dictatorship, although the author ultimately fails to explain this strategy.

responseSet.title: Presentation of Clinical Material on Sexuality
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Presentation of Clinical Material on Sexuality

Ana-María Rizzuto and Lucy Lafarge

Lucy Lafarge

75 Gardner Road
Brookline
MA 02146-4523

New York

Lucy Lafarge  

The moderator introduced the presenter, Otto Kernberg, the discussants, and the reporter and invited Kernberg to read his paper ‘A severe sexual inhibition in the course of the psychoanalytic treatment of a patient with narcissistic personality disorder’. Mr F was a 45-year-old successful businessman who sought analysis for his incapacity to maintain a relationship with a woman. He was twice divorced as the result of losing sexual interest in the women shortly after marriage. He was promiscuous and proud of his sexual exploits with women. Professionally his aggressive, ironic, and intense style made him thrive while he evoked fear and respect in his colleagues.

The case presented the vicissitudes of oedipal conflicts in the advanced stages of the analysis of a narcissistic personality. Mr F's analysis showed the appearance, persistence, and resolution through interpretation of castration anxiety, impotence and severe inhibition of sexual desire during the termination phase. What made the analysis specially interesting was the analyst's enactment in the countertransference of the patient's projected castration anxiety.

Mr F's relations with people were superficial, as manifested by his inability to describe his friends. He was afraid women would be interested in his money and frequently concealed his prosperity. His friendly and humorous disposition gave way to abrupt derogatory behaviour when he was frustrated. He was the youngest of three much older brothers and resented their privileges and dismissive behaviour towards him. The father was a dissatisfied small businessman, distant from his children and unable to stand up to his overpowering suspicious, hypochondriacal wife, who was constantly concerned about real and imaginary illnesses in herself and the family. Both parents were described as suspicious, controlling and chronically irritated.

The first five years of analysis achieved a slow but successful working through of Mr F's narcissistic character structure with minimal uncovering of childhood memories. He had developed late sexually, was small and frail, and did not masturbate until adolescence. His feeling less masculine than other men was later compensated for by promiscuous relationships with women.

responseSet.documentRef: Minerbo, V. (1998) The Oedipus Complex Reconsidered: Chaired by Jorge L. Ahumada, Buenos Aires. International Journal of Psychoanalysis  79:583-586

responseSet.title: The Oedipus Complex Reconsidered: Chaired by Jorge L. Ahumada, Buenos Aires
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Oedipus Complex Reconsidered

Chaired by Jorge L. Ahumada, Buenos Aires

Viviana Minerbo

Viviana Minerbo

5, Avenue St. Honoré d'Eylau
75016 Paris

Viviana Minerbo  

Citing Gaddini's Montreal paper, the chairman thought it timely to address the Oedipus complex amid changing socio-cultural patterns, family break-ups, and the influence of mass media: the core conflict of neurosis needs reassessment in terms of narcissistic and borderline pathologies. He then argued that, epistemically, the Oedipus complex is a sort of ‘frame’ theory making a place for different conceptual levels, open to clinical data and to other fields of knowledge and their ongoing research findings. Inviting the panellists to give their contributions, he noted their diverse approaches.

The first panellist, Isidoro Berenstein, focused on power, which, he said, psychoanalysis overlooks. The classical Oedipus complex involves three ‘places’: the desiring son, the desire of the mother, and the father standing for the prohibition, the law: Berenstein suggests a fourth ‘place’—that of absolute power. He differentiates two types of power, those of the life and the death instincts: the exercise of power by the father who represents the law to which he himself is subject, and the exercise of power putting individual will above the law, leading to omnipotence and megalomania, to the destruction of any subjectivity alien to the self, in short, to the destruction of links. More radical than the opposition sadism-masochism where each needs the other to exercise his power intersubjectively, absolute power finds parallels only in mythology.

Berenstein traces these two types of power in Sophocles' tragedies, in the struggle between Oedipus and his descendants, heirs to the Labdaco lineage, and Creonte, Jocasta's brother, who annihilates it. In Oedipus Rex, when Oedipus finds out his origin, his parricide and his incest, he accepts the law, blinds himself and goes into exile with his daughters. In Oedipus at Colonus, the struggle for power to dominate and control Thebes happens between Creonte and Oedipus' two sons. Creonte arbitrarily sequesters Oedipus' daughter, Antigone, his guide in his blindness; his power becomes firmer. In the third tragedy, Antigone dares question Creonte's edict-decree of a double punishment for her dead brother Polynices: he is to have no burial and is not to be mourned. With this decree, Creonte succeeds in his lust for absolute power, destroying Polynices who defied his power over Thebes by taking the throne, and exiling him even when dead. He does not honour the social contract, placing his will above the law. For daring to question this decree, Creonte has Antigone locked up in the family tomb where she hangs herself. The most destructive aspect is that with Antigone's death, the Labdaco lineage comes to an end. Eradicating those alien to himself, and destroying subjective growth, he represents absolute power and domination over others, depriving them of their identity.

responseSet.title: Reader-Response Criticism
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Reader-Response Criticism

Norman Holland

Norman Holland

P. O. Box 117310
Gainesville FL 32611-7310

Norman Holland  

The ‘triumph of theory’ in contemporary, postmodern literary criticism has toned much of our current intellectual climate. To those who are neither fans nor adepts, however, literary theory can seem a hopeless maze. The school known as reader-response criticism provides a point of entry into the labyrinth. It opens up questions, particularly psychological questions, that other theories often tacitly subsume. Reader-response provides a vantage-point from which to assess some of the ideas in postmodern literary theory.

To some extent, all studies of literature fall under the reader-response rubric, because all involve some notion of response. Plato banned poets from his Republic, because he assumed poetry enfeebled, while Aristotle defended drama's moral effect. Both were positing ideas of response, as do such twentieth-century theories as Brecht's alienation effect or the ‘making strange’ of today's ‘language poets’. It is only in the twentieth century, however, after Freud and others, that we have verifiable psychologies capable of addressing our responses to literature.

These days, reader-response criticism describes a school of critics who focus not on a text as such, but people's experience of that text. It was a reaction to New Criticism, which was in turn a reaction to still earlier practice. Early in this century, critics and professors of literature dealt with books through philology, bibliography, literary history, or intellectual background, information about a text. In the 1940s, '50s, and '60s, however, New Criticism began to dominate literary thinking in the US and UK (and somewhat later and differently on the continent under the name ‘structuralism’).

The New Critics ruled out (more or less) all background to ‘the text’, as ‘extrinsic’ knowledge, which didn't count, compared to ‘intrinsic’. Instead, New Critics treated the text as a free-standing, self-contained, unified object. They analysed the text itself (‘the words on the page’) for a ‘meaning’ or ‘theme’ embodied in the way the parts came together to form a unified whole. Critics would observe details on the ‘circumference’ or the words-on-the-page, then integrate them so as to formulate a centre, then check that formulation against other details on the circumference, and so on back and forth. From such a ‘hermeneutic circle’, one could achieve a complete understanding of a text, a ‘the’ reading, in which ambiguities and even lapses serve to develop the whole.

responseSet.title: Psicopatología Psicoanalítica.  El Model Freud-Abraham. [Psychoanalytic psychopathology.  The Freud-Abraham model.]  By J. Freixas.: Barcelona: Columna. 1997.  Pp. 581

[[RunningHead]]

Psicopatología Psicoanalítica. El Model Freud-Abraham. [Psychoanalytic psychopathology. The Freud-Abraham model.] By J. Freixas.

Barcelona: Columna. 1997. Pp. 581

Jose Marti Quiros

Jose Marti Quiros

Císcar, 55, 10a, 46005 Valencia

Jose Marti Quiros  

Anyone who approaches the psychoanalytic literature, whether as an analyst in training or as a student of psychoanalysis, soon encounters a remarkable limitation in that discipline, which has to do with the subject of psychopathology. Theoretical texts on psychoanalysis abound, and there is even a plethora of literature on psychoanalytic technique, but the number of monographs on psychopathology that systematically present the psychopathological model derived from psychoanalytic theory and clinical practice is very small. Psychopathology might be described as the poor relation within the set of component parts making up the doctrine of psychoanalysis.

However, psychoanalysis arose as an attempt to explain the symptoms of the neurotic patients who presented themselves at neurologists' consulting rooms at the end of the nineteenth century. When Freud commenced his medical practice he was very concerned to construct a psychopathological model that would help him to classify and understand these disorders. He gradually came to concentrate much more on the building of a structural model to explain the functioning of the mind, although he continued to undertake sporadic studies of non-neurotic clinical pictures. No subsequent author, except Fenichel, has attempted a systematisation of the aetiopathogenic and nosographic model derived from psychoanalysis.

The concern of psychoanalysis with the new clinical pictures that had appeared, either for sociocultural reasons or because advances in psychoanalysis itself had made them accessible to study, was not paralleled by an interest in the progressive incorporation of research on these pictures within the psychopathological edifice shared by the psychoanalysts.

(Translated by Philip Slotkin, MA, MITI)

Copyright © Institute of Psycho-Analysis, London, 1998

responseSet.title: The Impact of the Patient on the Analyst.  By Judy Leopold Kantrowitz.: Hillsdale, NJ: Analytic Press.  1996.  Pp. 320
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Impact of the Patient on the Analyst. By Judy Leopold Kantrowitz.

Hillsdale, NJ: Analytic Press. 1996. Pp. 320

Alan Skolnikoff

Alan Skolnikoff

205 Edgewood Avenue, San Francisco, CA 94117

Alan Skolnikoff  

For those of us who think of the analytic process as a joint venture between analyst and patient, this book will further stimulate our thinking about our experiences in being influenced by our patients. Firstly, I would like to go over some features of this volume that I discussed in an earlier review (Skolnikoff, in press; J. Amer. Psychoanal. Assn.). Kantrowitz, a leading researcher in psychoanalytic process, reviews the evolution of thinking about the analytic process within an ego-psychological framework, the dominant clinical orientation in North America. Changes have occurred recently that represent shifts from an earlier focus on intrapsychic phenomena in the life of the patient to the current interest in the interaction between patient and analyst. Following years of research measuring the process and outcome of control analyses conducted by trainees, this work shifts to more subjective descriptions of trained analysts' experiences with their patients as reported in written self-reports and telephone interviews. The focus is on the analysts' description of their self-analytic functions being stimulated by their work with their patients. The study vividly demonstrates how important the analysts' reactions to their patients are in influencing analytic work.

After establishing the ubiquity of the impact of the patient on the analyst, Kantrowitz gives detailed descriptions of interviews with the analyst respondents. She shows the variability in the type of responsiveness of analysts based on their character (cognitive or emotional), the different mutual effects depending on the match of patient and analyst, and the variety of ways that analysts change in response to their patients. Some examples: some analysts make a habit of studying their countertransference responses to their patients to stimulate changes in their thinking about these patients and/or themselves. Some experience the work with patients as stimulating more emotion in themselves; others experience this work as stimulating the need for tighter control of their emotions. Some experience analytic involvement as furthering therapeutic self-analysis; others as evoking thoughts of their personal limitations and the limits of the therapeutic value of analysis in general.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Response

Marcia Cavell

Marcia Cavell

1570 Olympus Avenue
Berkeley
CA 94708

Marcia Cavell  

Dear Sir,

I am sorry Dr Stolorow feels that I quoted him out of context, since I also think he and I are in fundamental agreement. He objects that whereas I was concerned ‘to explicate the preconditions for the sense that one's experiences are subjective, ours was to describe the intersubjective foundations of the sense that one's experiences are real’. Far more than ‘the sense of subjectivity’, I meant to be explicating (partially) the capacity for thought. One of my claims was that it requires not just ‘the sense of reality’, but a shared, public world that is objectively knowable (though not knowable for certain, etc.)

Perhaps I am unfair in pressing Stolorow's (and Atwood's)

1221

responseSet.title: Beyond Poetry, Music
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Beyond Poetry, Music

José Romero

José Romero

Fernández de los Ríos 95, 8o d
28015 Madrid

José Romero  

Dear Sir,

We must thank Christopher Bollas for having focused on the topic of sexuality in the session and for his excellent exposition in which he reflects on it from the economic point of view and that of meaning (IJPA, 78: 363-7).

As I see it, he shows how the ‘pleasure of transforming sexual energy into acts of meaning’ can be made possible by regulating an optimal level of drive activity.

He locates the way to achieve this on two levels, one through wording, the choice of words and sentences, and the other through telling, which as a second signifier brings new meanings that contextualize and alter the strict meaning of the signifier.

I shall now discuss several reflections arising out of such a stimulating idea.

Drive activation through the choice of words is clearly obvious, as Ferenczi demonstrated in 1910, in the use of coarse language to provoke a regression in the analysand to the hallucinatory-motor level of representation, which is perceived by the analysand as a sexual assault.

The same effect can be noted in word order. David Liberman, in speaking about the syntactic arrangement of words, describes different ‘styles’ and how by adopting a ‘counterstyle’ we may overcome the drive blockage that the style supports.

An example of this is use of the dramatic ‘counterstyle’ of the hysterical to reinvest the narrative style of the obsessive personality.

In both these cases we are working within the realm of meaning. Even if we deliberately stress our tone of voice, we only manage to reinforce it.

In contrast, from the standpoint that I am proposing, the voice viewed as a modulator of the level of drive activity is located, to my mind, at a pre-verbal and pre-linguistic level in which meaning has not yet been established.

I should now like to give some further explanation of this interpretation, which differs from that of Bollas inasmuch as he defines the voice as a second signifier.

responseSet.title: Response
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Response

Marcia Cavell

Marcia Cavell

1570 Olympus Avenue
Berkeley
CA 94708

Marcia Cavell  

Dear Sir,

Dr Goldberg has missed the point of my argument in ‘Triangulation, one's own mind, and objectivity’. But since it is often and easily missed, I am glad for the chance to make it again, perhaps more clearly.

My argument has to do with some of the necessary (but not sufficient) conditions on which the child can come to have something that is appropriately called ‘thought’ (as distinct from other kinds of intelligent, discriminatory, responses to the environment) in the first place, with what must have been the case in Dr Goldberg's own life that enables him even to pose his objections. The argument concludes that, among other conditions (e.g. embodiment), the child must be in communication of some kinds with other creatures. So if correct, the argument does not allow Dr Goldberg's thought experiment to get off the ground, since, of course, once someone is already a thinker (as Dr Goldberg, Descartes, and I, all are), he or she can imagine being the only person in the world, being a brain in a vat (the philosopher's favorite solipsistic image), etc. The question I posed is: what must already have happened in this thinker's life for her or him to be able to entertain this thought?

Dr Goldberg ends with a version of Bishop Berkeley's Idealist argument, which has been well criticized by a great number of philosophers (e.g. in our century, George Pitcher). To put the answer to it simply: it is obviously true that the only world we can talk about, know about, and so on, is the world that each of us experiences from his own point of view.

1223

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Work and Its Inhibitions: Psychoanalytic Essays. Edited by Charles W. Socarides and Selma Kramer.

Madison, CT: International Universities Press. 1997. Pp. 276

James Frosch

James Frosch

875 Massachusetts Avenue, Suite 54
Cambridge, MA 02139

James Frosch  

When I received the letter from the editor asking if I was interested in reviewing a book on work inhibitions I immediately became symptomatic. Of course I was tempted to read the book, perhaps it held the key to understanding my own work inhibitions. On the other hand, perhaps to agree to do the review was itself a manifestation of work inhibition, a distraction from the more difficult task of completing the half-finished paper that lay on my desk. Though momentarily paralysed, my curiosity about the book proved stronger than my hesitation and I do not regret my decision.

This book is an example of a certain genre of psychoanalytic books. An editor asks a group of world-class analysts to write chapters on a large, encompassing topic, in this case the psychology and psychopathology of work. The resulting collection is inevitably uneven and lacks a central organising thesis and point of view.

<autaff affid="A01"><addr><ln>Corazón de María 2</ln> <ln>28002 Madrid</ln></addr></autaff></artauth></artinfo>
responseSet.title: Acting in Revisited
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Acting in Revisited

Cecilio Paniagua

Cecilio Paniagua

Corazón de María 2
28002 Madrid

Cecilio Paniagua  

‘Acting in’ is a term that has not met with much success in the psychoanalytic literature. The literature has focused mainly on quite dramatic forms of acting during sessions rather than on subtler and more common forms of intraclinical acting. Zeligs (1957) gave the term ‘acting in’ a surprisingly restrictive denotation. A redefinition is suggested, characterising this concept with three features: it is a non-verbal action involving the somatic musculature; it has conscious or unconscious meaning conducive to the exploration of dynamics; and it takes place in the session. Inclusive and exclusive criteria are reviewed for the clarification of this important concept. ‘Acting in’ is not considered synonymous with ‘acting out in the transference’. Unlike ‘acting out’, ‘acting in’ can be predominantly of an extratransferential or a genetic nature. Enactments usually include episodes of acting in. What is considered most typical of acting in is the fact of its direct observability and consequent demonstrability to the patient. It is contended that instances of acting in represent a nodal point where the clinical surface becomes especially workable. Clinical vignettes are provided. It is suggested that in order to include acting in in the mainstream of resistance analysis, interpretive assistance should be given to analysands to translate these episodes consistently into verbal understanding.

responseSet.title: Response
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Response

Robert Caper

Robert Caper

360 North Bedford Drive
Beverly Hills
CA 90210

Robert Caper  

Dear Sir,

Thank you for giving me the opportunity to respond to Dr Poggi's comments. I agree with him that we need to recognize the necessity of the analyst's mental autonomy if we are to avoid the pitfall of assuming that all we need do as analysts is to be empathically attuned to our patients. That is certainly necessary, but as Dr Poggi and I agree, not sufficient. However, the main purpose of my paper was to explore the factors that affect the patient's mental autonomy. If I understand his comments, he feels we must revise our theories to include something like a healthy primary narcissism if we are to be able to account for anyone's mental autonomy in the presence of their objects. My understanding of primary narcissism is that it is a truly objectless mental state. Now certainly objectlessness guarantees mental autonomy from one's objects but the question seems to me to be, do we need to resort to such a concept to explain the development of a mind of one's own. Dr Poggi seems to feel that we do. I disagree.

The source of our disagreement may perhaps be understood by examining Dr Poggi's claim that I dismissed the patient's resistance to my exploration of her thinking about the agreement between her ideas and those of the author she admired, and thereby backed her into a position in which she was forced to agree with my point of view. Certainly, dismissing resistance can have this effect. But did I dismiss the resistance? As I indicate in my paper, I noted her resistance to examining the basis of her feeling that the author agreed with her, and in fact used it as the key to understanding both the dream of the previous session, and the clouded mental state she had been in over the previous weekend (p. 272). I then used this understanding as a tool for exploring her oedipal relationships. In brief, I analyzed the patient's resistance to help her understand some aspects of her mental functioning. If this is dismissing it, I am not sure what acknowledging it would look like. What I didn't do was agree, in the absence of evidence, with the patient's view of the relationship between her ideas and the author's. Perhaps this is what Dr Poggi means by dismissing her resistance. But if so, then my only alternative would have been to allow myself to be dominated by the patient's view, which might have been good empathic attunement, but would not, as Dr Poggi and I both agree, have been sufficient for analysis. (I should note here that the patient's resistance was in any event not to an interpretation of mine, but to examining her own view of something.)

responseSet.title: Reason and Passion: a Celebration of the Work of Hanna Segal.  Edited by David Bell.: London: Tavistock.  1997.  Pp. 178
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Reason and Passion: a Celebration of the Work of Hanna Segal. Edited by David Bell.

London: Tavistock. 1997. Pp. 178

Jean Bégoin

Jean Bégoin

28 rue Washington, 75008 Paris

Jean Bégoin  

This book is one of a new series of publications by London's Tavistock Clinic intended to disseminate the clinical and theoretical contributions that have been most influential in the training courses run by that famous institution in the various schools of psychotherapy. Edited by David Bell, it is a tribute to the work of Hanna Segal and her influence on the psychoanalysts and psychotherapists of the ‘Tavi’. What is particularly interesting about this original volume is that it is essentially clinical, nearly all the contributions being reports of analytically treated cases that give a good impression of the current state of—mainly Kleinian—psychoanalytic research on the therapy of ‘difficult cases’. Three of the ten authors—Joseph and Anne-Marie Sandler and Roy Schafer—concentrate on theoretical aspects, while nevertheless tackling the same problems, whereas all the others—Robert Caper, Leslie Sohn, Robin Anderson, Eric Brenman, Ruth Riesenberg Malcolm, Edna O'Shaughnessy and Betty Joseph—give detailed accounts of clinical cases, which clearly illustrate the working methods of these renowned analysts and the ‘technical’ concerns and theoretical positions that underlie their work.

The volume begins with an introductory essay by David Bell on the work and person of Hanna Segal. As Irma Brenman-Pick notes in her brief foreword, Segal's work is so well known and her influence so indisputable that she actually needs no introduction. However, it is good to learn more about her background from the biographical information gleaned by David Bell in direct interviews with Segal herself. In particular, we hear of her Polish origins and childhood and of her exile first in Geneva and later in Paris. Here she acquired her perfect French, which was subsequently to stand her in very good stead in her relations with her French-speaking supervisees as well as with the Francophone colleagues who included close friends with whom she would often spend holidays in France. Exiled once again to the United Kingdom, she went to Edinburgh to complete her medical studies, and there she met Fairbairn, who introduced her to the world of psycho-analysis by giving her two books, Melanie Klein's The Psycho-Analysis of Children and Anna Freud's The Ego and the Mechanisms of Defence. Her choice is a matter of record and led to her becoming the universally known presenter of Klein's oeuvre.

responseSet.title: Die frühe Kindheit: Entwicklungspsychologie der ersten Lebensjahre.  By Martin Dornes.  [Early childhood: developmental psychology of the first years of life.]: Frankfurt: Fischer Taschenbuch Verlag, Reihe Geist und Psyche.  1997.  Pp. 380
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Die frühe Kindheit: Entwicklungspsychologie der ersten Lebensjahre. By Martin Dornes. [Early childhood: developmental psychology of the first years of life.]

Frankfurt: Fischer Taschenbuch Verlag, Reihe Geist und Psyche. 1997. Pp. 380

Wilhelm Burian

Wilhelm Burian

Penzingerstr. 79/3, 1140 Vienna
(Translated by Philip Slotkin, MA, MITI)

Wilhelm Burian  

When necessary Freud himself made use, for the purposes of his clinical expositions, of both direct child observation (for instance in the famous example of the reel game) and reconstructions of the psychoanalytic situation. As he wrote in the 1920 preface to the fourth edition of Three Essays on the Theory of Sexuality, if ‘mankind had been able to learn from a direct observation of children, these three essays could have remained unwritten’ (1905, p. 133). Although the psychoanalytically informed direct observation of small and very small children features prominently in the work of such authors as Imre Hermann, Spitz and Mahler and, in our own day, in that of Emde and Fonagy, oddly enough our picture of the baby or infant has remained one of a more or less passive, undifferentiated creature at the mercy of its drives.

This one-sided view, which is becoming increasingly less scientifically tenable, has been the subject in the last few years of an international debate, the opposite position having been tellingly formulated by Martin Dornes with his idea of the ‘competent baby’ (Dornes, 1993). The topic has come to the fore in the German-language countries only in the last two years, whereas the discussion in the English-speaking world has been conducted between the opposing camps of psychoanalytic research (Fonagy and Gergely) and psychoanalytic tradition (Wolff). The German debate has made a very hesitant start (Uexküll, 1996) and has been confined to ‘specialists’, who have often denied the clinical relevance of the research results. Psychoanalysts are loth to scrutinise the scientific basis of their hypotheses very closely and all too readily rest content with the ‘practical effect’ of their concepts. At a time when we find ourselves under increasing attack, perhaps we should be more inclined to venture into the arena with neighbouring disciplines and not recoil in narrow-minded unconcern.

In this demanding book, whose title translates as ‘Early childhood: developmental psychology of the first years of life’ and which the unprepared psychoanalyst will find difficult reading, the author describes the interlocking of psychoanalytic theories with modern developmental psychology. He is attempting a synthesis of psychoanalytic concepts with the behavioural sciences: ‘The behavioural scientists describe how parental fantasies are communicated and understood in the preverbal period, whereas psychoanalysis illuminates the content of these fantasies and their interaction-determining force’ (p. 53). His meaning becomes clear from the example of the ‘internal image’. In the first year of life the baby has no fantasies at its disposal, but has more than mere sensorimotor representations. It probably does not have sequences of internal images until the second half of the first year. However, the baby wants not only to be acknowledged but also to acknowledge, and acquires this capability only in the course of its development: ‘Although the baby very early on recognises the world as independent of itself from the point of view of the psychology of perception, in order also to acknowledge the world as such emotionally … it must first have been confirmed in its need “to-wish-to-be-acknowledged”’ (p. 153).

<autaff affid="A01"><addr><ln>122 South Michigan Avenue, Suite 1305B</ln> <ln>Chicago</ln> <ln>Illinois 60603</ln></addr></autaff></artauth></artinfo>
responseSet.title: Deconstructing the Dialectic
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Deconstructing the Dialectic

Arnold Goldberg

Arnold Goldberg

122 South Michigan Avenue, Suite 1305B
Chicago
Illinois 60603

Arnold Goldberg  

The author examines some of the newly offered ideas and concepts about the interaction of the patient and the analyst. This interaction is termed a dialectic in the sense of interacting forces and this study is called a deconstruction, which is an effort to see how unstable and self-contradictory these new ideas may be. They are examined in terms of viewpoints of analysis as a developmental process, the location of the data of analysis, and the technical modifications that follow from this changed theoretical vision. A clinical example is offered to demonstrate the resultant problems. The suggested present status of the impact of these new ideas is that of a family of related concepts with no unifying theory.

responseSet.title: Trauma and Sexuality: Chaired by Marion Ulriksen de Viñar, Montevideo
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Trauma and Sexuality

Chaired by Marion Ulriksen de Viñar, Montevideo

Juan Jordán-Moore

Juan Jordán-Moore

Napoleon #3565 Dpto. 1004
Las Condes
Santiago
Chile

Juan Jordán-Moore  

The first speaker, Dr Prisciliano de León, read a paper entitled ‘Incest, theory and clinical reality’, based on a clinical-statistical study of child sexual abuse cases conducted between 1991 and 1997. De León gave some preliminary ideas based on the statistical data. (He had expected to read this in Spanish, so his presentation was curtailed.)

Then, Margaret-Ann Fitzpatrick presented her paper ‘Trauma, Sexuality and Dissociation’. She reported on the emergence of separate personalities during the analysis of a 54-year-old woman. She referred to the dissociation described in Ferenczi's classical paper, ‘Confusion of tongues between adult and child’, brought about by the intrusion of adult sexuality into the body and soul of the child. If the shocks suffered by the child continue, the personality splits are intensified. Finally, it is very difficult to keep the connection between these fragments, the failure of which brings about the appearance of separate personalities.

Fitzpatrick endeavoured to explain how separate personalities that take control of perception and of psychical and motor activity may emerge. She thinks that identification with the aggressor, the vertical splitting described by Freud in perversions, the denial and isolation defences that have been described as intensely at work in dissociated personalities that have suffered from sexual abuse, and the schizoid mechanisms acting in borderline personalities, are insufficient to explain how a separate personality takes control over the whole psychic life.

The author thinks that Glover's ego nuclei concept, that is, diverse clusters of instinctive experiences and the reactions to them, opens the way to understanding the appearance of separate personalities described by Ferenczi. Thus, separate personalities may be understood in the light of a regression to primitive patterns in which ego nuclei may be formed or existing ones may be reactivated. During the analysis of her patient the author observed that separate personalities emerged and disappeared in close connection with homosexual anxieties activated in the transference. This means that separate personalities emerge as an actual capacity of the psyche, beyond conscious control, and not simply as a consequence of reactivation of defensive patterns of the past. Furthermore, the ego nuclei theory may be connected with Shengold's suggestion that the patterns of cannibalistic incorporation—with the subsequent morbid alterations of the ego and superego—may be reactivated as a result of sexual abuse, bringing about dissociated aspects of the personality, which may acquire the capacity to control motor activity and the perception-conscience pole of the mental apparatus.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Sexuality in Clinical Psychoanalytical Treatment

Chaired by León Grinberg, Barcelona

Fred Pine

Fred Pine

55 East 87th Street (1B)
New York
NY 10128

Fred Pine  

In his introduction to the Panel, Grinberg centred the history and development of psychoanalysis on sexuality, alluding to Freud's thoughts on the biology and psychology of sexual development in the Three Essays and to more recently changing views on infantile sexuality and gender differences. He suggested that the polymorphous and perverse features of sexuality are of the most clinical interest.

Grinberg described the role of the mother and father in the development of infantile sexuality, commenting that the mother's unconscious projections on to the baby (projections also influenced by the father through his place in the mother's mind) colour the child's sexual development. Sexuality is subject to modification in accordance with a host of internal processes, not least the child's identifications with each parent in the resolution of sexual desire.

Addressing the specific topic of the panel, Grinberg emphasised that the analytic situation plays a fundamental role in the protection of the study of sexual functioning. On the other hand, he recognised that clinical approaches to sexuality vary widely in psychoanalysis today and that some of these variations would appear in the two presentations by the panellists—Bollas's paper on words ‘that alone give corporeality to the sexual fantasies’ and Meyers' paper on different aspects of perversion in everyday clinical practice.

Meyers' presentation came first. I shall summarise it briefly to make the ensuing discussion comprehensible. Her theme was a dual one—erotic transference and perversion—and she utilised a clinical example in which the two were interwoven and successfully worked through. Her context was the recognition that current theory would allow for the place of sexuality as a primary oedipal feature but also as a mode of stabilising self structure, maintaining early object ties, and, altogether, concretising a wide array of intrapsychic issues through sexual desire, fantasy and action—in and out of the transference. She contributed a description of what she termed ‘furtive enactments’, small-scale perverse actions, such as looking at pornography, which may appear in persons who would not otherwise allow perverse action. Clinically she discussed a movement from an earlier fully lived-out perverse masochistic relationship, through a phase in which there was some probable healing of underlying structural deficits, and subsequently to the emergence of a capacity to fantasise and to the use of perverse fantasy rather than action. In the analysis, rageful maternally directed wishes were played out and worked through, with a change in the fantasy from perverse and rageful to tender and loving.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Freud Among the Philosophers: The Psychoanalytic Unconscious and its Philosophical Critics. By Donald Levy.

New Haven and London: Yale University Press. 1996. Pp. 189.

Michael Trupp

Michael Trupp

969 Park Ave., New York, NY 10028

Michael Trupp  

Here is a situation, somewhat resembling an examination dream, that is experienced—sooner or later—by every ‘orthodox’ psychoanalyst: he finds himself at a cocktail party heavily peopled by academics, largely of a ‘humanities’ stripe. Amidst the high spirits and bubbly chatter, the topic of ‘Freud’ is (inevitably?) afloat and the drift seems strong towards a ‘post-Freudian’

Copyright © Institute of Psycho-Analysis, London, 1998

616

consensus. Psychoanalysis is identified, by a linguistics professor, perhaps, as the illegitimate offspring of logical positivism (bereft, however, of that school's disciplined concordance rules). Freud is further declared ‘semantically circular’ and lacking an organising semiotic ‘bed’ (laughter all around).

This fizzy chorus is suddenly challenged by a pro-Freudian guest—a philosopher, perhaps—who, joining the din, wine goblet in hand, launches a spirited and articulate objection to such glib unanimity.

The analyst, who had been experiencing a sudden wooziness unrelated to his vodka intake, is greatly relieved by this intervention. He is pleased that even here, at the university, his discipline has its champions …

But wait! As the analyst scrutinises this emerging ‘defence’ its unfolding seems increasingly odd; indeed, as fundamentally askew as the ‘attack’ it is designed to neutralise!

The analyst ‘awakens’ with a start. (But he was not, unfortunately, asleep.)

Such a ‘dream within a dream’ awaits the psychoanalyst reader of Freud Among the Philosophers by Donald Levy. For the hypothetical anti-Freudians substitute the likes of Ludwig Wittgenstein, William James, Ernest Nagel, Karl Popper and Adolf Grünbaum; and for the ‘defender’ of psychoanalysis, Levy himself, a professional philosopher.

Levy possesses, of course, a distinct advantage over the cocktail polemicist; for he need not ‘think on his feet’. On the contrary, he can pick and choose his adversarial counterparts, and, after calm and extended reflection, target their perceived fallibilities for finely polished ripostes. Such a format, at once Janusian and legitimately egocentric, has, as we shall see, its dangers; for it tends to expose rather more of an author than he might consciously intend.

[[RunningHead]]

The Many Faces of Deceit—Omissions, Lies and Disguise in Psychotherapy. By Helen K. Gediman and Janice S. Lieberman.

Northvale, NJ: Jason Aronson. 1995. Pp. 258.

Milton Viederman

Milton Viederman

525 East 68th St., New York, NY 10021

Milton Viederman  

The authors of this volume are concerned with the issues of lying, omission and deceit. They offer a broad classification and discuss the literature, emphasising the absence of systematic study in the psychodynamic literature for reasons that would appear to be obvious, namely, the unavailability of profoundly deceitful people to engage in a psychotherapeutic relationship that requires authenticity. The authors properly distinguish between conscious and unconscious deception, the latter being the essence of the unconscious itself, namely the defence against knowing. When they acknowledge the analyst's position as one involving ‘the suspension of disbelief’ they emphasise the naïvety of his view ‘that what is being said represents the whole truth and nothing but the truth’ (p. 7). Analysts would dispute whether they listen for truth or for meaning and indeed the constructionist view emphasises the ‘truth’ of unconscious wish and fantasy.

[[RunningHead]]

Response

George Markari

George Markari

525 East 68th Street
Box 171
New York
NY 10021

George Markari  

Dear Sir,

In my review of Lohser and Newton's Unorthodox Freud, perhaps I should have noted that the authors, at certain points, assert that Freud's papers on technique have been significantly misconstrued, and that in fact these papers advocate an analytic stance quite consonant with the more natural way that Freud seemed to work with his patients. The case for this assertion is mostly contained in an exceedingly scant (thirteen pages in all) and unconvincing chapter on the papers on technique. Lohser and Newton's contention is based on some opening remarks Freud made in these papers, a few re-translations (most of them recounted in their letter), no historical context, no sustained critical inquiry into the papers on technique, and some strong-armed interpretations that ignore a great deal of ambiguity (i.e. they make much of the difference between translating ‘Ratschläge’ as ‘advice’ versus ‘recommendations’ and emphasize a 1925 usage of the term ‘Takt’ by Freud that is contradicted by his usage of the same term in 1910). In the end, even if we were to simply grant the authors all of their re-interpretations without regard, many of the most important images and terms from the papers on technique—the analyst as mirror, the analyst as surgeon who operates with emotional coldness, the notion of ‘Indifferenz’, and the role of transference interpretation—remain completely unaltered. Contrary to their extreme claims in their letter to the Editor, the authors themselves concede this much more ambiguous state of affairs in their book by concluding that Freud in his technical advice oscillated between a position characterized by ‘flexibility, tact and individual preference’ and ‘absolute and authoritarian directives’ regarding ‘free association, analysis of transference, abstinence, neutrality and the uncovering and overcoming of resistance’ (p. 23).

responseSet.title: ‘Psychopathology and Primitive Mental States’ by Robert Caper
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

‘Psychopathology and Primitive Mental States’ by Robert Caper

Paul Williams

Paul Williams

15 Arden Road, London N3 3AB

Paul Williams  

Caper's paper is a discussion of ‘primitive’ mental states and focuses on the question of whether or not psychological illness in an adult can be thought of as a regression or fixation to a ‘primitive’ mental state (representing an early stage of normal development). An intensive discussion of fundamental issues raised by this question followed, although it took place between a small number of discussants. The paper begins by reviewing observations by Freud and others on the subject. Freud described an early egg-like state of hallucinatory gratification based on the pleasure principle (Freud, 1911). This state of unreality is usually only partially successful, as physical needs supervene (e.g. hunger), which indicates that the lack of contact with reality is not total. Klein, Bick, Bower, Stern and Emde, amongst others, have confirmed the validity of Freud's view—Caper feels that Emde's work on the mutuality of attunement between mother and infant is particularly relevant (Emde, 1988a, b).

Caper argues that there must exist from birth awareness of separateness of an object which permits learning from experience. The child's outlook will be modified by experience, and whatever magical thinking that might occur will also be altered. He draws a parallel between what he sees as ‘unconscious magical thinking’ in infants and delusional thinking in adults. Lack of modification will result in the infant being seen as abnormal. Caper states that an ill adult is not an older version of a normal child; the illness is the outcome of a non-progression from an already abnormal state. Failure to learn from experience in order to modify infantile delusions (in Kleinian terms, omnipotent unconscious fantasies of splitting, projection and introjection) leads to an experience of self and object as idealised (positively and negatively). There occurs identification with a good self/object and dis-identification with (ejection of) a bad self/object. Paranoid-schizoid thinking and part-object relationships are the outcome of such fantasies. The attribution of ‘good object’ qualities to the self and ‘bad object’ qualities to the other (and elsewhere) is a form of narcissistic identification.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Presentation of Case Material in Clinical Discourse. Edited by Ivan Ward.

London: Freud Museum Publications. 1997. Pp. 96

Victor Sedlak

Victor Sedlak

2, Foxhill Court, Leeds LS16 5PL

Victor Sedlak  

The publication of this short collection of papers, given at a Conference at the Freud Museum in London in 1995, is timely. The need is growing, from within and without the discipline, for clearer descriptions of what goes on in the consulting room. Allied to this are questions about how clinical data can be most effectively communicated and whether comparison and correlation of data can be made possible. The recent discussion in the literature prompted by the Seventy-Fifth Anniversary of the IJPA on the status of clinical facts is very pertinent to the issues discussed in this book. Also raised, of course, is the fundamental conflict between the patient's right to confidentiality and the psychoanalytic community's need for scientific knowledge.

The first paper is by Ronald Britton. Although he states at the outset that he is not primarily writing about the ethical issue of publishing clinical material, he quickly offers the reader the benefit of his experience and thinking on this matter. He of course counsels disguising the material to protect the anonymity of the patient and furthermore he advises obtaining the permission of the patient to publish. He then writes that this does not relieve the author of a sense of guilt: ‘[w]e may feel that we are not betraying a confidence but we are left with a feeling that we are betraying an affiliation’ (pp. 11-12). Britton goes on to state that not to do this can involve the betrayal of another affiliation, that is, to the scientific community of which one is a member. It is wise to address this matter, which will probably be at the forefront of many readers' minds at the beginning of the paper and of the book. Once the impossibility of its easy resolution and the inevitability of struggling with difficult feelings when publishing have been noted, the reader can pursue other issues with interest such as an author's other anxieties about publishing clinical work. Britton is particularly interested in the author's conflict between wanting to be original and wanting to be faithful to the views of significant others and hence forge further affiliation with them. He describes how this can make communication of scientific ideas difficult and he cites Darwin's reticence about publishing The Origin of Species. Britton uses this as an example of the greater difficulty an author will feel in publishing when a science is about to undergo a major shift in its basic paradigm. He judges psychoanalysis to be currently at such a stage.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Generations: Poems between Fathers, Mothers, Daughters, Sons. Edited by Melanie Hart and James Loader.

London: Penguin. 1998. Pp. xvii + 366

Margot Waddell

Margot Waddell

Tavistock Clinic, 120 Belsize Lane, London NW3

Margot Waddell  

That there are many congruencies between poetry and psychoanalysis has long been recognised. Finding symbolic forms for elusive and possibly as-yet-unknown emotions or psychic states—what Shakespeare described as giving to ‘airy nothing’ a ‘local habitation and a name’ (A Midsummer Night's Dream, V, i, ll. 16-17)—is a process shared by poets and psychoanalysts alike, each reaching for the meaning that may become evident to those who are ‘Willing to work and to be wrought upon’ (Wordsworth, ‘The Prelude’, XIII, l. 100).

The poet seeks to re-evoke the intensity of a feeling, to engage with the meaning of human life, with matters of value, of emotion and experience.

responseSet.title: Disavowal of Reality as an Act of Filial Piety
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Disavowal of Reality as an Act of Filial Piety

Bernard Penot

Bernard Penot

36 rue de l'Arbalète
75006 Paris

Bernard Penot  

The author argues that often the hidden power of disavowal of reality gradually makes itself felt in a treatment. The psychoanalytic process then seems to have been brought to a halt by something that defies meaning and continually eludes any genuine subjectivisation by the patient. In the famous case of the Wolf Man (1918), Freud speaks of a rejection of reality, which he clearly distinguishes from repression, in that the rejected representation has not in any way been erased from consciousness; rather, its meaning remains as if in suspension, no longer open to judgment. Certain key moments in the treatment of a borderline case help to illustrate the necessity for the analyst to grasp the peculiar nature of his own involvement. He may have been induced to assume a parental attitude reproducing a ‘community of disavowal’ that belongs to the patient's unrecognised history. By adopting an interpretive formulation acknowledging his own participation in the disavowal—which the parents must have been unable to do—the analyst can then allow the patient to get out from under it. These considerations allow us to shed light on two possible theoretical difficulties regarding, firstly, the difference between negation and disavowal of reality and, secondly, the way that the disavowal tends to hamper the very process of symbolisation necessary for the deployment of fantasy.

responseSet.title: On Psychoanalytic Truth
responseSet.authorMast: Robert D. Stolorow,  George E. Atwood and  Donna M. Orange
responseSet.authorCitation: Stolorow, R. D., Atwood, G. E. & Orange, D. M.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On Psychoanalytic Truth

Robert Stolorow , George Atwood and Donna Orange

Donna Orange

11726 San Vicente Blvd., 410
Los Angeles, CA 90049

Donna Orange  

Dear Sir,

In her otherwise very valuable article, Cavell (1998) mistakenly portrayed us (Stolorow & Atwood, 1992) as having rejected the idea of a real external world. Whereas Cavell's aim was to explicate the preconditions for the sense that one's experiences are subjective, ours was to describe the intersubjective foundations of the sense that one's experiences are real. In a section entitled ‘The Genesis of the Sense of the Real’, from which Cavell quoted a sentence out of context, we wrote:

We are concerned here with the process by which a child acquires an experience of the world and the self as real. ‘Reality’, as we use the term, refers to something subjective, something felt or sensed, rather than to an external realm of being existing independently of the human subject … It is our view that the development of a child's sense of the real occurs through the validating attunement of the caregiving surround … Reality [i.e., the sense of the real] thus crystallizes at the interface of interacting, affectively attuned subjectivities (Stolorow & Atwood, 1992, pp. 26-7).

We do not reject the ideas of truth, reality and the external world. Instead, it is our contention that psychoanalytic truth is concerned with lived emotional experiences (the patient's, the analyst's) rather than with objects in the world, a distinction emphasised by Wittgenstein (1953). There is no danger of solipsism or of Cartesian subjectivism here. There is a vision of psychoanalytic truth that is perspectival and fallibilistic (Orange, 1995), relentlessly contextual (Orange et al., 1997), and, in fundamental agreement with Cavell, dialogic and communitarian.

Robert D. Stolorow, George E. Atwood, and Donna M. Orange

Dr R. Stolorow
11726 San Vicente Blvd., 410
Los Angeles, CA 90049
August 1998

responseSet.title: Femininity and Masculinity in Post-Modernism: Chaired by Roger Dufresne, Montreal
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Femininity and Masculinity in Post-Modernism

Chaired by Roger Dufresne, Montreal

Marita Torsti

Marita Torsti

Maria Jotunintie 10J
00400 Helsinki

Marita Torsti  

The moderator opened the panel and gave the floor to the panellists. Cláudio Eizirik began as follows: ‘Post-modern critics, though not congruent with one another, all disavow the notion that human beings have any essential or unitary core. Post-modern accounts of human experience are united only in their opposition to the ideals of Enlightenment beliefs, which are considered an elaborate, if also convenient, set of “fictions”.

‘Femininity and masculinity are usually seen as the quality, nature or state of the female or male sex. Psychoanalytically, these terms designate a constellation of characteristics of each sex, including anatomy, appearance, gender identity, gender roles, sexual object preference and culturally determined social behaviour. In recent years, three major psychological differences have been established between the sexes: core gender identity, sexual behaviour, overt and fantasised and culturally determined institutionalised non-sexual attributes and behaviour. Although the psychoanalytic concepts of femininity and masculinity refer to a complex system of beliefs that each individual forms in relation to anatomy and anatomical distinctions and although unconscious fantasy leads each individual to form a personal sense of masculinity or femininity, consistent or not with biological sex, there is a widespread acknowledgement that cultural influences that contribute to masculinity and femininity are not absolute models, but change from one culture to another.’

Eizirik makes the point that the role and importance of the family in creating and maintaining human values is collapsing. Also the concept of power is breaking down into ‘pockets of micropowers’, present in almost all systems; power becomes relational. ‘There is a claim to the right of being different, the rights of women, pacifists, homosexuals and other minorities are overtly claimed.’

Eizirik's question is whether psychoanalysis, and its views on masculinity and femininity, have anything to do with post-modernism. He believes that such a relationship does exist. Eizirik examines the effect of post-modern thinking on our analysands and the changes in their norms, in particular with regard to sexual norms. He also comments on the effect of post-modernism on psychoanalytic concepts, in particular the denial of the centrality of penis envy in feminist theory. The new feminist approach in psychoanalytic theory applied psychoanalytic concepts in dealing with motherhood and power. Up to the last moment Eizirik avoids answering the question of a possible connection between psychoanalysis and post-modernism, but at the end he says, forcefully, that many psychoanalytic writers have scrutinised precisely those tools criticised also by post-modern thinking: ‘And this happens not because this is the up-to-date trend, but exclusively because the living tissue from which we take the essence of our condition—the interaction of two minds and subjectivities—can be better understood from a perspective that privileges the uncertainty, the complexity, the unknown, the fragmentary, the incomplete, the dark continents that we are daily invited to explore’.

responseSet.title: The Analyst's Subjectivity and the Analyst's Objectivity
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Analyst's Subjectivity and the Analyst's Objectivity

Owen Renik

Owen Renik

244 Myrtle Street
San Francisco
CA 94109

Owen Renik  

The author suggests that, increasingly, contemporary psychoanalysts have been acknowledging and examining the intersubjectivity of the clinical analytic encounter and that this trend has sometimes been misunderstood as necessitating the conclusion that objectivity is impossible to achieve in clinical psychoanalysis. In contrast to this, the author proposes a pragmatic definition of the analyst's objectivity that is entirely consistent with acceptance of the analyst's irreducible subjectivity, and illustrates his view by means of a clinical example. He suggests that objectivity concerns both objects and objectives, and indicates that a pragmatic conception of objectivity is employed throughout modern science. He explains why he regards psychoanalysis as a science, rather than as a hermeneutic discipline. He takes up the way in which implicit positivist assumptions can disadvantageously influence our thinking about the relation between an analyst's subjectivity and an analyst's objectivity. Finally, he discusses the important role of consensus in objectivity generally and in psychoanalytic objectivity particularly, as well as some of the differences that exist among analysts with regard to the specific objectives that take priority in clinical work.

responseSet.title: Transience: Its Beauty and Danger
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Transience: Its Beauty and Danger

Osamu Kitayama

Osamu Kitayama

Fuokuoka
812-8581 Japan

Osamu Kitayama  

The author argues that what he terms transition and what he will call transience to together quite often, but that analysts usually discuss transition rather than transience. In the first half of this paper, he examines Ukiyo-e and Japanese myths and folk tales, so as to introduce the Japanese concept of transience and its pathological connotation. In the second half, he quotes two treatments of self-destructive patients to show the importance of understanding the negative and positive aspects of transience in psychoanalytical terms. He concludes that masochistic identification with the transitory figure(s) can take place along with limitless accumulation of debt, causing everything, including one's own self, to be felt as transient. He points out that a Japanese word ‘Arigatou’, which is almost equivalent to ‘thank you’ in English, literally means ‘difficult to exist’, and thus the importance in Japan of appreciating the transience of valuable things. He considers that while readers may be reminded of Winnicott's discussion of transitional objects, transition is a phenomenological description of movement, while transience is mainly an emotional state, or one of painful feeling. Transience, he believes, is not only a feature of Japanese clinical phenomena but also universal.

responseSet.title: A Self-Report Contrasting Concentrated and Standard Psychoanalysis
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A Self-Report Contrasting Concentrated and Standard Psychoanalysis

Burton Hutto

Burton Hutto

CB #7160 University of North Carolina
Chapel Hill
NC 27599

Burton Hutto  

Dear Sir,

Recently within the International Psychoanalytical Association, there has been renewed controversy over the particular practice known as concentrated analysis (Junquiera Mattos, ‘Concentrated analysis: a three decades experience’. In Psychoanalysis in Process: Method, Theory, Applications. Festschrift in Homage to R. Horacio Etchegoyen. New York: Jason Aronson, 1996). Concentrated analysis as a format for psychoanalysis refers to meeting for more than one session per day, typically two sessions per day on two consecutive days. This technical alteration is controversial. Although it may make psychoanalysis more available to analysands who live far from their analyst, questions arise about whether treatment in this format remains truly psychoanalysis. I would like to use my own experience in training analysis of a period of concentrated analysis followed by standard psychoanalysis as a basis to contrast these formats with attention to analysis of resistance, analytic process, transference, and dreams. Such a self report is a risky if not hazardous endeavor, but I believe that this minimal self-disclosure may facilitate inquiry into the timely topic of concentrated analysis.

After I was accepted as an analytic candidate, I needed to begin my training analysis before beginning course work. At that time I lived 250 miles from the institute, but I planned to move to the city of the institute in six months. Any attempt to begin analysis four days per week before the move would have been untenable for me at that time. My training analyst suggested we could begin by meeting twice daily for two consecutive days each week until I could relocate six months later. We met late on Tuesdays and early on Wednesdays with only about an hour between the two sessions for each day. The remainder of my analysis took place four days per week.

The following sketchy and preliminary conclusions are based on my reflections and impressions of my analysis. As an analysand, my impressions are inherently distorted to some degree, but I believe that I raise important considerations in the comparision of concentrated and standard psychoanalysis. I cannot substantiate my opinions with more detailed clinical data without disclosing beyond my level of comfort.

responseSet.title: Religious Experience and Psychoanalysis: From Man-as-God to Man-with-God
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Religious Experience and Psychoanalysis: From Man-as-God to Man-with-God

Odilon Franco

Odilon Franco

Rua Sergipe 441, 4°, conj. 42
01243-001 São Paulo
Brazil

Odilon Franco  

The author notes that the Freudian psychoanalytic view of religion has become a saturated concept that causes psychoanalysts to disregard the complexity of the religious experience. Issue is taken with the reductionism inherent in the conception of religion as mere illusion and the image of God as being derived solely from the oedipal father. After a presentation of the ideas of some later authors and discussion of the transition from the state of ‘Man-as-God’ to the relationship of ‘Man-with-God’ as portrayed in a film, two clinical vignettes are given to illustrate this process of humanisation. The author suggests that religious manifestations in patients should be seen not only in the light of their infantile determinants but also as the result of processes whereby new meaning is assigned to psychic facts. In his opinion, psychoanalysis should not seek solely to reduce religious beliefs to their roots in unconscious fantasies but should lead to a reorganisation of the analysand's image of God and the assignment of new meaning to it. The resulting changes are shown as contributing to psychic growth on the part of both analysand and analyst. The author contends that if psychoanalysis dismisses religion as illusion, it is running the risk of itself becoming an established religion.

responseSet.title: The Changing Aims of Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy: An Integrative Perspective
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Changing Aims of Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy: An Integrative Perspective

Jeremy Holmes

Jeremy Holmes

North Devon District Hospital
Barnstaple
Devon EX31 4JB

Jeremy Holmes  

The purpose of this paper is to relate traditional psychoanalytic ideas on aims and outcomes to the changing world of contemporary psychoanalytic psychotherapy, with its financial constraints and theoretical and technical innovations. An emphasis on splitting rather than repression, on the nature of the attachment between therapist and patient, and on realistic goals and modest change all characterise contemporary perspectives. Recent developments in the cognitive behavioural psychotherapy literature indicate possible areas of overlap with psychoanalytic psychotherapy. Attachment theory proposes that patient-therapist attunement is likely to be a key variable in outcome. Since attunement is intrinsically responsive rather than directive, the paradox of psychoanalytic ‘aimlessness’ is inherent in the nature of psychoanalytic psychotherapy.
