responseSet.title: The IPA and the American Psychoanalytic Association: A Perspective On the Regional Association Agreement
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The IPA and the American Psychoanalytic Association: A Perspective On the Regional Association Agreement

Robert Wallerstein

Robert Wallerstein

655 Redwood Highway, Suite 261
Mill Valley
CA 94941

Robert Wallerstein  

Ever since 1938 the American Psychoanalytic Association has had a special autonomous relationship within the IPA accorded to no other component organisation. This Regional Association status has had two main features: (1) total internal control over training standards and membership criteria, with no accountability to the IPA; and (2) an ‘exclusive franchise’, so that the IPA was barred from recognising any other component within the United States. This unique Regional Association status reflected the resolution at the time (1938) of the long-standing controversy between the IPA and the American over the issue of ‘lay analysis’, and remained unaltered for half a century until, with the resolution of the 312-year long law-suit against the American (and secondarily against the IPA) in 1988, the Regional Association agreement was modified (but not totally abrogated) by the American's giving up the ‘exclusive franchise’ aspect (thus permitting IPA recognition of psychoanalytic groups in the US organised outside the American), but still retaining its internal full control over training and membership. The meanings and consequences for psychoanalysis of this special status of the American are explored.

responseSet.title: Quality Control, Condensed Analysis and Ethics
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Quality Control, Condensed Analysis and Ethics

Daniel Widlöcher

Daniel Widlöcher

248 Boulevard Raspail
75014 Paris

Daniel Widlöcher  

A doctor who once had an exciting and fruitful analysis with a ‘master’ of the time decides for personal reasons to embark upon another treatment many years later. This second analysis profoundly disappoints him. The quality of the exchanges seems to him to fall far short of what he had experienced before. The analyst appears absent-minded and bored as he listens, and his interventions are off the point. Nothing happens and the patient decides to break off the analysis after three years of regular sessions. He then goes to a third analyst, whose way of practising analysis is very different from that of the second. Substantial and fruitful psychic work is accomplished. Now the practice of the second analyst has proved incorrect in terms both of his behaviour in the sessions and of his interventions. In any other medical speciality, this negligence or incompetence would be common knowledge and would result in a loss of reputation or even a sanction. So the patient wonders what approach psychoanalytic organisations have towards monitoring the quality of the care provided by practitioners trained at their institutes and claiming adherence to the methods espoused in their soceties.

Why Evaluate?

This issue of the quality of care cannot be evaded for much longer. It is the patients themselves (the ‘consumers’) who are concerned about it, anticipated or followed by government and, more generally, by public opinion as a whole.

The insistence on the need for evaluation and quality control of psychoanalytic treatment is relatively recent (Chodoff, 1996). In the post-war period of rapid expansion of psychoanalysis in Western countries, the idea of evaluating its results by objective empirical procedures barely existed. From the beginnings of psychoanalysis to the mid-1950s, the position had scarcely changed. That is not to say that there were no ethical concerns about the utility of psychoanalysis.

responseSet.title: Freud's Presentation of ‘The Psychoanalytic Mode of Thought’ in Totem and Taboo and His Technical Papers
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Freud's Presentation of ‘The Psychoanalytic Mode of Thought’ in Totem and Taboo and His Technical Papers

William Grossman

William Grossman

45 Oak Avenue
Tenafly
NJ 07670

William Grossman  

Freud considered ‘the psychoanalytic mode of thought’ to be an instrument of research. In ‘Totem and Taboo’, he demonstrated his mode of thinking by applying it to the data of contemporary studies of totemism. While taking the reader along the paths of his selection and weighing of information, he also applied clinical discoveries about mental life in reconstructing ‘primitive psychological situations’ in the distant past. In the technical papers that appeared concurrently with his book, he applied to the clinical situation the ideas on transference, narcissism, primitive mental mechanisms and unconscious communication that he had explored in ‘Totem and Taboo’. In this paper, the author presents material showing the structure of Freud's exposition as a model of analytic exploration. Some of the principles of mental organisation mentioned by Freud are discussed. Examples are given of the close parallels between ‘Totem and Taboo’ and two technical papers. The author suggests that ‘Totem and Taboo’ is an important step in Freud's development of his theory of object relations and his ideas about the intersubjectivity of unconscious mental life.

responseSet.title: Narcissistic Influence on Erotic Love: Chaired by Donald Campbell, London
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Narcissistic Influence on Erotic Love

Chaired by Donald Campbell, London

Arlene Richards

Arlene Richards

200 East 89th Street
New York
NY 10128

Arlene Richards  

The panel on ‘Narcissism and Erotic Love’ had a new format. The first two papers were written by individual authors, but the third paper was a combined effort by three analysts from Madrid.

The first paper was Henry Smith's ‘Narcissistic Resistances to Erotic Love’. In it, Smith differentiated between a form of narcissistic resistance illustrated by Shakespeare's Richard III and another form along the lines suggested by Rostand's Cyrano de Bergerac. Smith used clinical material derived from work with two of his patients. Both patients elicited boredom in the analyst by their removal from him in the transference. The first patient was the sort who seemed more pathological inasmuch as hatred of the other induced destruction of the object. In the second kind of patient, surrender to the object's needs became a way of altruistically fulfilling desire by idealising the other at the expense of the self. Both patients used narcissism as a defence against object relations.

For both, it was a secondary structure, determined largely by conflict caused by fear and hatred towards love objects. For both of Smith's patients, attempts to win the love of the mother were foiled by her negative response to the patient's body. His female patient experienced her mother's loathing only when the daughter underwent early menarche at 10 years of age. His male patient experienced his mother's intrusive and unwanted seductiveness as well as her rejection of his body. Smith reported that the patient had learned as a little boy to control his asthma and sweating in his attempt to please his mother. Thus, for both patients, the constant need to protect the self from painful conflict led to building a wall between self and other, the experience of which in the countertransference led the analyst to spot the underlying conflict. Where Smith used Shakespeare and Rostand, Alírio Dantas Jr., the next speaker, used Octavio Paz's work. Dantas's paper explored the relationship between sex, love and eroticism.

responseSet.title: Tradition and Change in Psychoanalysis.  By Roy Schafer.: Madison, CT: International Universities Press.  1997.  Pp. 271 + xiii
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Tradition and Change in Psychoanalysis. By Roy Schafer.

Madison, CT: International Universities Press. 1997. Pp. 271 + xiii

Steven Levy

Steven Levy

1701 Uppergate Drive, Atlanta, GA 30322

Steven Levy  

In Tradition and Change in Psychoanalysis, Roy Schafer, one of our field's most articulate and thoughtful scholars, explores continuity and change within current psychoanalytic pluralism. In my view, he stakes out a logical, measured, and constructive position among competing psychoanalytic perspectives. The latter are often described in flamboyantly provocative rhetoric, as radical departures in seeming discontinuity with other more traditional, analytic perspectives, leading some to predict the collapse of a unified psychoanalytic world. Schafer recognises the common roots of psychoanalytic ideas and views change as transformation with inevitable lines of common descent that are traceable via a careful reading of the history of psychoanalytic ideas. Grounded within a post-modern narrative, hermeneutic position, Schafer champions different narrations of a psychoanalytic point of view, while maintaining ‘a deep respect for contributions to the discipline made under the banner of tradition’ (Preface, p. x).

In placing this collection of essays within the context of Schafer's previous psychoanalytic work, one can discern how he has been influenced by arguments against Freud's nineteenth-century mechanistic-evolutionary-naturalistic perspective. Schafer's embrace of modern Kleinian perspectives, especially dyadic dynamics and projective-introjective clinical phenomena, and his involvement in the realms of hermeneutics and intersubjectivity, represent his most recent view of psychoanalytic theory.

The book is divided into three sections. The first begins appropriately with an examination of the merits and limits of Heinz Hartmann's contributions to psychoanalysis as a general psychology. In an especially respectful and thoughtful way, he dissects within the grand edifice of Hartmann's theory of adaptational egopsychology, moral, epistemological and clinical blind spots, contradictions and inconsistencies that have led to the potentially emancipating new perspectives with which psychoanalysis currently struggles. Schafer takes on critics of Hartmann who read adaptation to mean conformity and superficial adjustment, and ego-psychology to mean focusing too exclusively on consciousness. Nonetheless, he exposes the problems inherent in Hartmann's ‘conservative, stewardly tendencies’, including biological positivism and a relatively uncritical attachment to the myth of analyst as objective and non-contributory partner in the analytic enterprise. For Schafer, the analytic work remains a dialogue to which both participants inevitably contribute. It can be described in different complemental as well as discontinuous ways without anchoring in a ‘mental apparatus’ whose characteristics can ever be characterised objectively and once and for all. For Schafer, analysis can only be discussed in terms of psychic reality, and must take into account each party's contribution to the meaning and significance of the events, feelings and relationships under consideration. Likewise, there can be no final theory of psychoanalysis. Pluralistic psychoanalysis has always been the rule and will always characterise its future development. Schafer examines the perceived dangers of a pluralistic psychoanalysis and the supposed safety of a more monolithic or inclusive single psychoanalytic theory. He argues persuasively that a comprehensive, committed and deep understanding of psychoanalysis viewed from many perspectives is a better safeguard for the integrity of the work and the science than attempting to ensure stability and an ethical approach via any single or comprehensive theoretical and clinical point of view.

responseSet.title: Growth and Risk in Infancy.  By Stephen Briggs.: London: Jessica Kingsley. 1997. Pp. 292
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Growth and Risk in Infancy. By Stephen Briggs.

London: Jessica Kingsley. 1997. Pp. 292

Denise Roberts

Denise Roberts

Flat 1, 10 Swains Lane, London N6 6QS

Denise Roberts  

This book details the unfolding development over their first two years of five infants, four of whom were considered potentially ‘at risk’. Briggs uses the technique of infant observation pioneered by Bick but he attempts to bring an additional quantifiable aspect by rating and tabulating his descriptions.

The accounts are painfully poignant. We read of Hashmat, the ninth child born into a Bengali family where his mother is pregnant again by the time he is 3 months old; of Michael, unexpected and unwanted, a ‘punishment’, born to a single mother of two, whose own mother had died when she was 3. We read of Hesther, the second child of a mother whose infant brother had died a cot death, the father out of work and preoccupied with the death of his father; and of Samantha, the second child of a family recently arrived in the UK and lacking support, and preoccupied with the worrying quasi-autistic withdrawal of their elder son.

With the degree of deprivation of the families he observes, Briggs suffers even more than usual the painful tension between detachment and intervention (which might help mother and baby but, in doing so, will affect what he hopes to observe). So he intervenes when one-year-old Hashmat and his elder brothers lean dangerously out of the tower block window. Michael's mother simply stuffs her baby in Briggs' arms and leaves the house; he writes interestingly of his own countertransference feelings such as an impulse to throw the unwanted Michael across the room. Irritatingly he often presents his own conclusion followed only then by his evidence for it, rather than allowing the reader to have their own thoughts about the material.

It is a short book (at under 300 pages including many tables) and Briggs could have made fuller use of his wealth of data. The five observations allow for a comparison of the differing developments of the babies in response to their particular family circumstances, providing evidence for what is common and what is distinct in the response of different babies to (for example) a detached, unresponsive, ‘flat’ mother. Evidence too of what is part of normal development and what is a specific response to an event: for example, in two cases Briggs described how he gained the first evidence of the mother's subsequent pregnancy by the infant's preoccupation and play with the insides of objects. However, in passing, we also hear of similar play in the other infants: this raises interesting questions such as whether there is a difference in the timing of the onset of such play or in its quality in response to a mother who is physically and emotionally ‘filled up’ with a new pregnancy.

responseSet.title: Chronicle of an Incest Foretold
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Chronicle of an Incest Foretold

Mariela Michelena

Mariela Michelena

Poniente 25, 2° A
28036 Madrid

Mariela Michelena  

In this paper the author discusses the situation of children handed over to grandparents or to other relatives of the natural parents to be brought up. She notes that such children are faced with the riddle of their own filiation and postulates that this scenario often conceals an oedipal fantasy to the effect that the child concerned is the fruit of an incestuous relationship between a grandparent and the relevant parent. Following the example of Freud, the author adduces literary models for illustration. As with the Oedipus of Sophocles, the author shows how efforts to thwart the workings of fate actually bring about the consummation of the tragedy in the form of incest, which is favoured by the confused oedipal configuration in the families of handed-over children. The main argument is based on the characters and situations of two novels by Gabriel García Márquez, written at different times in his life. With reference to the psychoanalytic literature on artistic creativity, the author shows the importance of the mid-life crisis in determining how García Márquez came to terms with the fact of having himself been entrusted to grandparents as a child and how this situation is reflected in the works concerned.

responseSet.documentRef: Ilahi, M. N. (1998) Sexuality and Somatic Illness: Chaired by Pedro J. Boschan, Buenos Aires. International Journal of Psychoanalysis  79:579-582
responseSet.documentRefHTML: Ilahi, M. N. (1998) Sexuality and Somatic Illness: Chaired by Pedro J. Boschan, Buenos Aires. International Journal of Psychoanalysis 79:579-582

responseSet.title: Sexuality and Somatic Illness: Chaired by Pedro J. Boschan, Buenos Aires
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Sexuality and Somatic Illness

Chaired by Pedro J. Boschan, Buenos Aires

M. Ilahi

M. Ilahi

M. Nasir Ilahi
41 Central Park West
New York
NY 10023

M. Ilahi  

This fascinating topic has historical links with the origins of psychoanalysis and with Freud's pioneering contributions on the role of sexuality in mental and somatic illness. It is also linked to the issue of the relation between the mind and body, something that has exercised philosophers from the earliest times and has subsequently been of great interest to psychology and psychoanalysis.

The Chairman opened the panel and proposed a format for the presentations and the subsequent discussions.

The first panellist, Clara Helena Portella Nuñes, began by briefly reviewing Freud's views on sexuality and hysteria and on the somatic aspects of sexuality. She observed that in current thinking on sexuality and somatic disease, however, what is implied is early sexuality in the context of the mother-baby relationship. Acknowledging the contributions of Klein, Winnicott and McDougall, she stated that the split between psyche and soma is a common feature of all psychosomatic patients. Their soma is felt as a foreign body persecuting their mind from outside. She described her work with a male patient in his early fifties who was entirely preoccupied with descriptions of his physical symptoms, ranging from urinary/prostrate complaints to rheumatic pains. Attempts to explore psychological meanings were rejected by the patient, who apparently insisted on the convincing quality of his ‘objective reality’.

He was also greatly hypochondriacal and subjected himself to numerous painfully invasive physical examinations. However, there appeared to be an inability to feel psychic pain. He felt unattached to everyone around him including the analyst. Transferentially, the analyst represented both the mother to whom he handed over his suffering body to be soothed and the envious mother who would punish him for being so rich by making him ill. The loss of his father in the third year of treatment brought up the theme of death. While his only feeling was relief at not having to visit his father any more, he felt utterly terrified at the thought of his own death. However, this anxiety could only take refuge in somatic symptoms.

responseSet.documentRef: Kirshner, L. A. (1998) Challenges Facing Today's Psychoanalytic Practice: Chaired by Maria Luisa Muñoz, Madrid. International Journal of Psychoanalysis  79:595-596
responseSet.documentRefHTML: Kirshner, L. A. (1998) Challenges Facing Today's Psychoanalytic Practice: Chaired by Maria Luisa Muñoz, Madrid. International Journal of Psychoanalysis 79:595-596
responseSet.documentRefXML: Kirshner, L. A. (1998) Challenges Facing Today's Psychoanalytic Practice: Chaired by Maria Luisa Muñoz, Madrid. International Journal of Psychoanalysis 79:595-596

responseSet.title: Challenges Facing Today's Psychoanalytic Practice: Chaired by Maria Luisa Muñoz, Madrid
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Challenges Facing Today's Psychoanalytic Practice

Chaired by Maria Luisa Muñoz, Madrid

Lewis Kirshner

Lewis Kirshner

306 Harvard St.
Cambridge
MA 02139

Lewis Kirshner  

The Panel, which gave voice to a widespread, if not universal, concern about the future of psychoanalytic practice, was well-attended, despite its inconvenient position during the very final hours of the Congress. There was a remarkable uniformity in the depiction of a significant decline in the stature and acceptance of psychoanalysis among both educated colleagues and the public at large, producing what Marvin Margolis termed ‘a grim picture’. Moreover, among the multitude of factors causing this state of affairs, internal structural and organisational problems within psychoanalysis were emphasised by each participant. In general, the panellists were also in agreement about remedial steps that should be undertaken.

The first speaker, Samuel Arbiser, outlined the current Argentinian situation in which the collapse of a bubble of enthusiasm for psychoanalysis has left an over-abundance of ‘analysts’, many ill-trained outside accredited institutes, while the demand for treatment has waned. He devoted many of his remarks to outlining his proposal for re-establishment of the essential values of psychoanalysis by ‘desacralising’ its quasi-religious or esoteric features, in favour of a more open and scientific approach. He proposed that a broader psychoanalytic perspective would break down barriers to dialogue with the academic and psychotherapy communities, opening access to a ‘potential demand’ for help not confined to patients presenting classical indications for psychoanalysis.

Carlos Amaral Dias likewise emphasised the historical isolation of psychoanalysts, which has had the dual effects of creating a dynamic of idealisation by outsiders, with all its inevitable and unfortunate consequences, and a bureaucratic rigidification within. Here, he stressed the continuing necessity for greater openness and democratisation as responses to the crisis. An example of one analytic community's attempt to work collaboratively with other institutions was provided by Ekkehard Gattig, who described the steps taken in Germany to participate in the national health system. He recounted some of the compromises that were made to achieve this partnership; for instance, the acceptance of a fixed number of total sessions. He also stressed that analysts need to work harder to document the efficacy of treatment, if they hope to be compensated by third-party payers like national health schemes.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Dream Discourse Today. Edited by Sara Flanders.

London and New York: Routledge. 1993. Pp. 237.

Bernard Barnett

Bernard Barnett

73 Fortune Green Road, London NW6 1DR

Bernard Barnett  

If we judge by the steady number of both non-specialist and psychoanalytic publications on dreams that have appeared in the literature, it is clear that the topic continues to fascinate both the lay public and the practising psychoanalyst. In the latter case, even though the dream is now regarded as only one of a number of ‘royal roads’ to the unconscious, the analyst will almost certainly ‘sit up and take notice’ when a patient says, ‘I had this dream …!’ As readers of this journal will be aware, the inclusion of a dream in the descriptive material of case reports also remains a favourite method of clinical illustration.

responseSet.title: ‘Thick-and Thin-Skinned Organisations and Enactment in Borderline and Narcissistic Disorders’ by Anthony W. Bateman
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

‘Thick-and Thin-Skinned Organisations and Enactment in Borderline and Narcissistic Disorders’ by Anthony W. Bateman

Paul Williams

Paul Williams

Flat 1
15 Rosslyn Hill
London NW3 5UJ

Paul Williams  

Bateman's paper appeared in the Journal (79: 13-25), and was placed on the journal web site some weeks in advance of publication. It was the ninth paper selected for discussion by the Discussion Group (see the IJPA Home Page for details) and achieved great interest and praise, particularly for its frankness and clarity. In the paper Bateman argued that enactment is ‘any mutual action within the patient/analyst relationship that arises in the context of difficulties in countertransference work’ (1998, p. 13). Such enactment is common during the treatment of borderline and narcissistic disorders. In order to delineate different forms of enactment the author described the analysis of a patient identified with a sadistic mother and who threatened the analyst with a knife during treatment. Three levels of enactment were delineated of which two (a collusive countertransference and a defensive countertransference) were detrimental to analytic work whilst the third was beneficial but only because the intervention made by the analyst was independent of the analytic process, and yet occurred in response to it.

The author stated that enactment was most likely to occur when a patient moved between thick-skinned and thin-skinned narcissistic positions, a conceptual distinction first made by Rosenfeld and which Bateman sought to develop. This movement between thin- and thick-skinned positions, which was a characteristic of borderline psychopathology, also presented an opportunity for interpretation, allowing progress. Bateman considered enactment to be a term embodying ideas related to acting out, acting in, actualisation, repetition, transference and countertransference. There exists no agreed definition of the concept, and this is theoretically and clinically problematical. Two themes regarding enactment tend to emerge in the literature: firstly, enactment is interpersonal, involving action between patient and analyst. This may be ‘actualisation’ of a patient's wished-for transference relationship or, more seriously, the joint overstepping of a boundary. Enactment involves the analyst being caught up in the relationship rather than remaining ‘alongside’ it. Secondly, there is the idea of enactment as a positive force if the analyst can extricate himself from it and enable the enactment to lead to further understanding. Bateman asked if this was different from ‘normal’ analysis. He emphasised the difficulties in countertransference work on the part of the analyst that affect enactment (which may have positive or negative consequences). He cited projective systems that oscillate between patient and analyst, the identificatory processes of the analyst (concordant countertransferences) and defensive countertransferences as the main countertransference contributors to enactment.

responseSet.title: Attachment Representations in Adult Years: Implications for Psychoanalysis: Chaired by Robert Emde, Denver
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Attachment Representations in Adult Years: Implications for Psychoanalysis

Chaired by Robert Emde, Denver

Ricardo Bernardi

Ricardo Bernardi

Santiago Vazquez 1140
11300 Montevideo
Uruguay

Ricardo Bernardi  

The study of attachment is one of the fields in which a very fruitful co-operation has taken place between psychoanalysis and allied disciplines. Introducing the panel, Robert Emde remarked that ‘recent attachment research, building on the theories of Bowlby and the research of Mary Ainsworth, and now two generations of students, has been influential in both developmental psychology and psychoanalysis’. This success has been, in a significant way, the result of the construction of reliable methods for the evaluation of attachment behaviours in children, and for attachment representations in adults (cf., for example, Goldberg et al., 1995, Attachment Theory. Social Development and Clinical Perspectives, London: Analytic Press).

Even though there are different methods, the most widely used have been Mary Ainsworth's ‘strange situation’ (for infants) and Mary Main's ‘adult attachment interview’ (AAI, for adults). According to this first method, infants are categorised into one of four attachment categories: (a) secure; (b) anxious/avoidant; (c) anxious/resistant and (d) disorganised/disoriented. When around a decade ago Mary Main established a system for evaluating autobiographical narratives of adults with categories comparable with the former, a wide field of investigation opened up, relating infantile behaviours to adult attachment representations. The scope of this field was pointed out by Emde, when he mentioned that current investigation faces problems such as the following: (1) how do attachment representations develop in adulthood, and how do they work outside the parental context? (2) How general or specific are these representations? Does normal development convey growing differentiation and specificity? (3) From the point of view of pathology: what are the conditions under which maladaptive, rigid patterns of attachment and representations in adult persist? (4) Which are the correcting processes that enable a modification of such patterns? (5) What degree of consciousness or intentionality do they have? (6) What is its relationship with personality traits? (7) How are they influenced by culture? (8) And by genetic and biological factors? The three panellists referred to ongoing investigations that allow progress in some of these questions, based on empirical or clinical evidence.

responseSet.title: The Clinical Thinking of Wilfred Bion.  By Joan and Neville Symington.: London and New York: Routledge.  1996. Pp. 184
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Clinical Thinking of Wilfred Bion. By Joan and Neville Symington.

London and New York: Routledge. 1996. Pp. 184

Robert Caper

Robert Caper

360 North Bedford Drive, Beverly Hills, CA 90210

Robert Caper  

In this new book, Joan & Neville Symington provide an introduction to the work of Wilfred Bion. They supply abundant clinical illustrations of Bion's theoretical ideas, and their work therefore comes as a welcome addition to the effort of Meltzer (1978) and of Grinberg et al.

Copyright © Institute of Psycho-Analysis, London, 1998

417

(1977). They summarise Bion's clinical thinking under the headings of ‘The Grid’, ‘Myth’, ‘Container/contained’, ‘Alpha function’, ‘Psychic reality’, ‘The growth of thought’, ‘Transformations’, ‘The study of groups’, ‘The phenomenology of psychosis’, ‘Without memory or desire’, and ‘Ultimate reality, the mystic and the Establishment’, ‘The theoretical disjunction between Bion and Freud/Klein’, ‘Bion: his character’ and an epilogue. The authors also provide a bibliography of Bion's writings.

The subtlety and compactness of Bion's ideas are among his great strengths, but they have also been sources of great frustration to generations of students. The Symingtons' account makes them accessible even to those with limited psychoanalytic experience. (This is, incidentally, more than Bion felt that he could do himself. He prefaced more than one of his books with the warning that nothing in it would make sense to anyone who was not a practising psychoanalyst.) The Symingtons may be criticised for not having captured all of the subtlety of Bion's thinking, but such a criticism would hardly be fair, since there is an inevitable trade-off between accessibility and subtlety in writing about the areas covered by Bion's work, and this will continue to be so until Shakespeare is reincarnated as a psychoanalyst.

In addition to introducing the reader to Bion's ideas, the Symingtons offer a highly interesting critique of his work. Because of the limitations of space, and because the critique is not only the most original part of the book, but is bound to be the most controversial as well, I will focus my attention on that. It centres on some important questions about the nature of psychoanalytic knowledge and the relation of theory to observation and practice. These questions were also quite important to Bion: in Learning from Experience, he described a psychotic patient who was unable to recognise the qualities that distinguish a mind from non-mental objects, and whose world, as a result, consisted only of inanimate material objects.

responseSet.title: Some Considerations in the Structure of the Self and Its Pathology
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Some Considerations in the Structure of the Self and Its Pathology

Salvador Adroer

Salvador Adroer

Plaza Gironella 2
08017 Barcelona

Salvador Adroer  

The author's intention is to contribute to the study of the structure of the self, both discussing the different processes of interiorisation and identification that play their parts in this development, and looking into the role played by the objects, and especially the superego. Interiorisation (the choice of this word is explained) is seen as a broad concept that covers incorporation, internalisation, introjection and introjective identification, and the dynamic of each of these mechanisms is discussed, along with its influence in the development of the structure of the self and of its constituent elements. Taking these concepts as a theoretical base, the author goes on to consider the field of ‘pathology of defect’ compared with the more classical pathology of conflict. Starting from the findings of different authors, especially Fairbairn and Kohut, he enlarges upon theoretical aspects of this ‘pathology of defect’ and its close relationship with Kohut's theories of narcissism, emphasising that, apart from other factors, an essential element in understanding this pathology is the development of the superego. Clinical material is presented to illustrate the theory.

responseSet.title: Homosexuality: Chaired by Aiban Hagelin, Buenos Aires
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Homosexuality

Chaired by Aiban Hagelin, Buenos Aires

Emanuel Berman

Emanuel Berman

Haifa 31905, Israel
E-mail: RSPS360@UVM.HAIFA.AC.IL

Emanuel Berman  

To the oft-repeated question, ‘one psychoanalysis or many?’, this panel gave a clear answer: many, and quite divergent. At times, this aroused a sense of frustration, as the differences of viewpoint appeared so great as to make a dialogue difficult. At other moments, the feeling was that the honest expression of the different positions was in itself valuable, even if a fruitful dialogue would require more time, more patience and more intimacy than could be achieved within the boundaries of a congress panel.

The moderator, Aiban Hagelin, opened with a question: ‘What is homosexuality? Is it an impulse neurosis? Is it a personality disorder? Is it a neurosis? Perhaps a psychosis, or a “transitional neurosis?”’ Hagelin quoted Socarides: ‘Of all the symptoms of emotional origin that serve simultaneously as defences, homosexuality is unique in its capacity to use profound psychic conflicts and struggles to attain, for limited intervals, a pseudo-adequate equilibrium and pleasure reward (orgasm), often permitting the individual to function, however marginally and erratically’. He suggested that these and many other issues could be addressed by the panel.

The first panellist, Lenise Lisboa Azoubel, presented a clinical case of male homosexuality. In her theoretical introduction she drew upon Chasseguet-Smirgel's emphasis on the regression to pregenitality motivated by castration anxiety, on the disavowal of the differences between genders and generations, and on the idealisation of anality. She also relied on Lander's differentiation between stable homosexual structures (not necessarily perverse) and perverse homosexual symptomatic acts (in acting out), and mentioned Amati-Mehler's questions as to the difference between symptoms, perverse defences, perverse thought and perverse transferences.

The patient, a man who started analysis at 27, described his father as violent and his mother as passive and submissive; he became homosexual in adolescence. Azoubel outlined numerous sadistic fantasies he expressed, about her being caught in a Vietnamese booby trap, being electrocuted in her consulting room, or her breasts being torn off. She interpreted to him his intense pleasure in transforming love into hate, credibility into corruption, and his need to attack and frighten her so that she could feel the fear and pain he was feeling.

responseSet.title: Some Thoughts on the Essence of the Tragic
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Some Thoughts on the Essence of the Tragic

K. Arvanitakis

K. Arvanitakis

653 Grosvenor Avenue
Westmount
Quebec
H3Y 2S9

K. Arvanitakis  

An attempt is made to define the essence of the tragic through an examination of Euripides' ‘The Bacchae’, a tragedy that deals with the origins of tragedy itself. The action here culminates in the dismemeberment of Pentheus by his mother. It is proposed that the tragic may be related to the earliest phases of differentiation of the subject as a separate entity breaking off from the original mother-infant unit. Tragedy, in this view, could be regarded as the enactment of a primal phantasy of the birth of the ‘I’ as the result of an archaic act of violence. The process of mourning for the loss of the original unity is central to this development. Pentheus' tragic flaw consists in his repudiation of contradictory dualities and his inability to mourn. The integrative function of ‘Logos’, both in tragedy and in the analytic process, is underlined. It is suggested that ‘Logos’ aims to generate meaning not by eliminating contradiction but by embodying the foundational human paradox.

responseSet.title: The Therapist as a Person Facing Death: The Hardest of External Realities and Therapeutic Action
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Therapist as a Person Facing Death: The Hardest of External Realities and Therapeutic Action

David Feinsilver

David Feinsilver

2800 McKinley Place, N.W.
Washington DC 20015
Rockville, MD

David Feinsilver  

The author notes a parallel between his heightened capacity to experience pleasure in life after discovering that he has metastatic cancer and the surprising mobilisation of therapeutic forces displayed by his patients after sharing with them the news of his illness. He presents four clinical vignettes and compares and contrasts his findings with those reported in the relatively sparse and inconclusive literature which emphasises the negative aspects of dealing with this kind of threatening reality. The author notes the usefulness of sharing external realities with his patients, which then functions in dialectical interplay with a classical focus on unconscious fantasy, both in extreme and ordinary therapeutic circumstances. This extends and elaborates on the author's previously reported work on ‘comprehensive countertransference’ being a ‘real person’ and therapeutic action.

responseSet.title: What is ‘Applied’ in ‘Applied’ Psychoanalysis?
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

What is ‘Applied’ in ‘Applied’ Psychoanalysis?

Aaron Esman

Aaron Esman

1100 Park Avenue
New York
NY 10128

Aaron Esman  

The ‘application’ of psychoanalytic concepts and methods to the products of culture has occupied a somewhat ambiguous position, seen by some as a secondary, derivative, even dubious procedure, by others as a valuable and legitimate extrapolation of the basic principles. This paper argues that such ‘applications’ were integral to the early development of the field and that, indeed, many of Freud's basic ideas were derived from non-clinical (i.e. cultural) sources. The continuing impact of cultural forces on clinical concepts can be seen in the recent reformulations of our views on the psychology of women. Psychoanalysis is to be seen, therefore, as a constantly evolving system of propositions and hypotheses that are capable of ‘application’ and study in both clinical and extra-clinical settings. It is further argued that the continued development—even survival—of psychoanalysis requires the integration of its institutions and training facilities into the university system, permitting the free exchange of ideas across disciplines and a flexible educational structure that will encourage much-needed training in research as well as clinical methods. A brief illustration of the value of a psychoanalytic approach to the understanding of a specific work of art (Man Ray's painting ‘Les Amoureux’) is provided.

<autaff affid="A01"><addr><ln>5 Ave. St. Honoré d'Eylau</ln> <ln>75116 Paris</ln> <ln><webx type="email" url="mailto:102565.100@compuserve.com">102565.100@compuserve.com</webx></ln></addr></autaff></artauth></artinfo>
responseSet.title: The Patient Without a Couch: An Analysis of a Patient with Terminal Cancer
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Patient Without a Couch: An Analysis of a Patient with Terminal Cancer

Viviana Minerbo

Viviana Minerbo

5 Ave. St. Honoré d'Eylau
75116 Paris
102565.100@compuserve.com

Viviana Minerbo  

The author reports an unusual clinical experience arising from the tragic circumstances of a patient who contracted cancer at the beginning of the fifth year of an analytic process. Instead of interrupting the analysis, the analyst suggested having sessions by telephone, as this patient could no longer leave her home when the terminal phase of her illness set in. The experience proved beneficial for the patient and enriching for the analyst. The patient was able to contain, work through and integrate the meaning and consequences of her disease, make reparations to her objects, and accept death with dignity. The analyst also emerged from the experience strengthened and more aware of her own vulnerability and mortality. The author brings up three relevant questions based on a review of the literature. These questions are: should the patient be told of his/her diagnosis and to what purpose? Can there be a productive analysis with such patients? What psychic structure and emotional conditions allow a patient to bear the truth?

responseSet.title: Thick- and Thin-Skinned Organisations and Enactment in Borderline and Narcissistic Disorders
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Thick- and Thin-Skinned Organisations and Enactment in Borderline and Narcissistic Disorders

Anthony Bateman

Anthony Bateman

St Ann's Road
London N153TH

Anthony Bateman  

In this paper the author argues that enactment is any mutual action within the patient/analyst relationship that arises in the context of difficulties in countertransference work. Such enactment is common during the treatment of borderline and narcissistic disorders. In order to delineate different forms of enactment, which in his view may be either to the detriment or to the benefit of the analytic process, the author describes a patient who was identified primarily with a sadistic mother and who threatened the analyst with a knife during treatment. Three levels of enactment involving countertransference responses are described of which two, namely a collusive countertransference and a defensive countertransference, were detrimental to the analytic process. The third level of enactment was beneficial but only because the intervention by the analyst was independent of the analytic process and yet in response to it. The author uses Rosenfeld's distinction between thin-skinned and thick-skinned narcissists to illustrate how enactment is most likely when a patient moves between thick-skinned and thin-skinned narcissistic positions. Nevertheless the move between thin and thick-skinned positions presents an opportunity for effective interpretation, allowing progress in treatment.

responseSet.title: A look at the Salomé-Freud Correspondence
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A look at the Salomé-Freud Correspondence

François Sirois

François Sirois

1020 Bougainville
Québec
Qc G1S 3A8

François Sirois  

The author notes that the changing emphasis of the correspondence between Lou Andreas-Salomé and Sigmund Freud over the years reflects certain aspects of the development of every analyst. Her contributions are shown to illustrate the evolution of the analyst's relationship with theory and the growth of his or her clinical capacity and involvement. Two phases in the analyst's clinical progression are stated to be discernible in the correspondence, marked by changes of direction that mirror the changing nature of her relationship with Freud. Theoretical considerations predominate in the first phase, while clinical aspects gradually come to the fore in the second. Freud is seen to be offering Salomé a kind of supervision by letter. Lou's underlying transference to Freud also emerges from the correspondence, its two aspects being the relationship to a big brother and to a father respectively. Freud's complementary transference to Salomé is also discussed, partly in connection with her relationship with his daughter Anna. The author's arguments are supported by extensive quotations from the correspondence, biographical works and Lou Andreas-Salomé's published diaries and reminiscences. Last but not least, her relationship with Freud in the author's view indirectly raises the issue of that of any analyst to the founder of psychoanalysis.

responseSet.title: The Recovered Memories Controversy
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Recovered Memories Controversy

Mary Target

Mary Target

26 King Henry's Road, London NW3 3RP

Mary Target  

Scharff, J. S. & Scharff, D. E. (1994). Object Relations Therapy of Physical and Sexual Trauma. Northvale, NJ: Jason Aronson. Pp. 370.

Hedges, L. E. (1994). Remembering, Repeating, and Working Through Childhood Trauma. Northvale, NJ: Jason Aronson. Pp. 336.

Pendergrast, M. (1996). Victims of Memory: Sex Abuse Accusations and Shattered Lives (2nd ed.). Hinesburg, VT: Upper Access. Pp. 746.

Scott, A. (1996). Real Events Revisited: Fantasy, Memory and Psychoanalysis. London: Virago. Pp. 190.

Brenneis, C. B. (1997). Recovered Memories of Trauma: Transferring the Present to the Past. Madison, CT: International Universities Press. Pp. 204.

Sandler, J. & Fonagy, P. (Eds.) (1997). Recovered Memories of Abuse: True or False? London: Karnac Books. Pp. 250.

The impact of trauma, the nature of memory, the proper role of the analyst, these issues are as old as psychoanalysis and at its heart. Over the last ten years, a furore has blown up over memories of abuse reported or reconstructed in psychotherapy, and assumed to have been previously repressed. The controversy began and has remained centred in the United States, but has also rumbled loudly elsewhere, with lobby groups being established by the parents of adults claiming early sexual abuse (several thousand in the US, perhaps 350 in the UK: Weiskrantz, 1997). These groups also included academic psychologists who were deeply alarmed by the reported practices of ‘recovered memory therapists’, and by claims that flew in the face of research evidence on the workings of memory systems and the impact of trauma. The debate revisits Freud's dilemma over the seduction hypothesis, but instead of remaining a question of mainly theoretical and professional importance, it has become embroiled in a contemporary climate favouring grievance, exposure and law suits.

Psychoanalysis and related therapies have been passionately accused on both sides of the recovered memories debate. On one side, which sees all or most recovered memories of abuse as false (i.e. historically untrue), the accusation is that these ‘memories’ are planted in the minds of suggestible patients by irresponsible therapists. On the other side, there is anger at the thought that survivors can be re-traumatised, by therapists who label painfully revived memories as fantasies, and who turn a blind eye, repeating the denial of reality that may at times be the most bitter betrayal in child sexual abuse.

responseSet.title: The Ambulatorium: Freud's Free Clinic in Vienna
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Ambulatorium: Freud's Free Clinic in Vienna

Elizabeth Danto

Elizabeth Danto

218 East Fifth Street
New York
NY 10003

Elizabeth Danto  

At the close of World War I, Freud proposed the creation of clinics providing free treatment, in the first of a series of politically liberal statements promoting the development of a kind of institution that is rarely associated with psychoanalysis today. Using archival and oral history research methods, this study offers a descriptive and statistical history of the Vienna Ambulatorium, the free psychoanalytic clinic and child guidance centre created—we can now surmise—under Freud's direction. Presented within the cultural context of central Europe's inter-war rush of progressivism in ‘Red Vienna’ and in Germany's Weimar Republic, little-known aspects of the history of psychoanalysis emerge. From 1922 to 1936, the staff of the Ambulatorium treated gratis patients of all ages and social classes, ranging from professional to unemployed. Candidates too were analysed at no cost. Reflecting the urban energy of his era, Freud believed that psychoanalysis could be both productive and free of cost. What emerges is an unexpectedly activist, community-oriented profile of some of the earliest participants in the psychoanalytic movement.

responseSet.title: Presentation of Clinical Material on Sexuality: Chaired by Guillermo Ferschtut, Buenos Aires
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Presentation of Clinical Material on Sexuality

Chaired by Guillermo Ferschtut, Buenos Aires

Mauricio Gitnacht

Mauricio Gitnacht

Avenue Dumas 13
1206 Geneva

Mauricio Gitnacht  

Guillermo Ferschtut opened the panel with some brief but warm remarks about Horacio Etchegoyen, who then read his paper. The paper dealt with a patient in her forties who was about to finish her treatment after several years. He gave the account of five sessions given in a week before vacation time. The first session on Monday began with a dream in which there were elements that related to the patient's fantasies about dangers that could threaten her body (sexual organs) and the loss of beloved members of her family, as well as her functioning as a differentiated sexual person. The analysis of this dream was the subject of all five sessions of the week. Although a full account of the clinical material will not be possible, I will try to emphasise the technique and the way in which transference and countertransference developments were handled by Etchegoyen.

The dream and clinical material was full of omnipotent fantasies, as well as fantasies related to her body and feelings of a damaged organism and doubts about her integrity as a sexual valuable person. A relevant aspect of the material was the patient's envy, expressed by projective identification into the analyst through attempts to devalue his functioning as an able and valuable analyst. He chose to interpret this material by giving emphasis to the early mother-child relationship, the early pre-oedipal phase, the female phase and Klein's theory of primary envy. Etchegoyen underlined how easy it would have been for a female analyst to follow this path, but how difficult it was for him (a male analyst) to account for his countertransference feelings. At this moment, the patient acted out by threatening to leave for one week just before the summer vacation. This situation aroused very strong countertransference feelings. Etchegoyen showed how he carefully evaluated his feelings, and waited until he understood the material before interpreting the patient's unconscious fantasies, after which she reversed her intention. Then the question of an analyst's difficulty in understanding and interpreting homosexuality with a patient of the opposite sex in the transference-countertransference relationship arose. Another relevant point was his decision to interpret one aspect of the material at a time, even if it might seem to leave other aspects of the material behind. The phallic fantasies, in their different forms, were in the forefront of his interpretation of the patient's material.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Therapeutic Alliance. By W. W. Meissner.

New Haven and London: Yale University Press. 1996. Pp. 385

Paul Dewald

Paul Dewald

4524 Forest Park Boulevard, St. Louis
MO 63108

Paul Dewald  

This is a scholarly and closely reasoned book that goes beyond the limits of its title, and provides a broad overview of the author's concepts of the process and technique of psychoanalysis. It is divided into four separate parts, with nineteen chapters plus an appendix of guidelines related to the management of treatment boundaries, and a section of chapter notes. The list of references covers forty-nine pages.

In the first chapter, Meissner develops a history of the concept of therapeutic alliance and stresses the importance of distinguishing the therapeutic alliance from both transference and countertransference, and from the real relationship between patient and analyst.

The fact that they intersect in complex patterns and are in continual interaction and mutual modification makes it difficult to preserve their distinct intelligibility, but I propose that the effort to keep them at least conceptually separate furthers our understanding better than prevailing approaches that tend to blur

responseSet.title: Impotence and Frigidity: Chaired by Sara Zac De Filc, Buenos Aires
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Impotence and Frigidity

Chaired by Sara Zac De Filc, Buenos Aires

Salvador Adroer

Salvador Adroer

Pl. Gironella 2, 2°
08017 Barcelona

Salvador Adroer  

A brief introduction by the moderator, Sara Zac de Filc, drew attention to the richness of material in the papers to be presented, both in the interesting points of view expressed by the authors and in their being based in different theories, all of which paved the way for the following truly fruitful presentations and subsequent discussion. The first panellist, Castellaro de Pozzi, basically re-examined the theories of Freud, Abraham, Klein and the post-Kleinian writers (Bion and Meltzer in particular), focusing on points that, according to the panellist, might help us to understand impotence and frigidity.

With regard to Freud's views on impotence, Castellaro de Pozzi stressed three points: incest and its repression, castration anxiety and the importance of the link between female identification and male homosexuality, this link originating in the fact that both are related to the castration complex.

Castellaro de Pozzi suggests that Freud relates frigidity in the woman to the characteristics of the Oedipus complex. Lacking a penis, the little girl experiences a narcissistic injury that awakens penis envy; she is unaware of the existence of her vagina and her masturbation is only clitoral. It is oedipal non-renunciation of her father that conditions frigidity in the woman, who is unable to tolerate the fact that her partner is not her father. Again, when the male identifies his female partner with his mother, impotence may appear. With frigidity, we also find aggression towards the male, especially when, not experiencing vaginal pleasure, the woman casts doubt upon the potency of her partner.

As for Abraham's contribution, Castellaro de Pozzi says that this writer relates envy to narcissism, with special emphasis on the latter, as narcissism is the cause of aggressive attitudes and of male denigration of women, jeopardising the transformation of the penis as urinary organ to the penis as the instrument of genital sexuality.

Castellaro de Pozzi, in her discussion of Klein's contribution and that of the post-Kleinians, gave us a general review of Kleinian theory regarding sexual neuroses. It would seem, according to this, that Klein's concept of primary envy and Bion's of hatred of emotions show us how the impotent patient experiences his penis as a sadism-executing organ, or feels that he himself might be the victim when his own sadism is projected into the vagina and on to the father's penis situated inside the mother's body. The little girl fears retaliation because of her envious attacks aimed at the inside of her mother's body and believes thus that her own body might be emptied of its contents and destroyed. First, the child turns to her father in her desire that he will give her babies, and it is thanks to anxiety about the persecutory mother that she adopts a masculine position. In the analysis of frigidity it is important that this anxiety be examined in depth. The panellist also expressed Meltzer's view that both men and women may employ perverse phantasies in order to achieve a ‘pseudopotency’ and apparently normal sexual relations.

responseSet.title: Review of the Psychoanalytic Theory of Sexuality: Chaired by Homer Curtis, Philadelphia
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Review of the Psychoanalytic Theory of Sexuality: Chaired by Homer Curtis, Philadelphia

Ruth Stein

Ruth Stein

3 Brody St.
Jerusalem 92502

Ruth Stein  

In the theme of the present Congress, ‘Psychoanalysis and sexuality’, ‘sexuality’ has been taken out of ‘psychoanalysis’ and juxtaposed to it with an ‘and’. Could the wording of the Congress theme reflect a conscious or unconscious decision to take sexuality outside of psychoanalysis in order to reexamine it in the wake of sexuality's changing place both within psychoanalysis and outside of it? Or have we analysts given up sexuality, being unable to find space either for the idea that sexuality is not fundamental in all cases, or for the precious psychoanalytic insights about the power and dynamic contradictoriness of psychosexuality?

These major questions seemed to reverberate in the foreground and background of the whole Congress, as well as in its first plenary session. In Spruiell's and Green's, as well as in Curtis's statements, it was asked whether the hallmark of psychoanalysis was still sexuality, when we knew that both object relations, intersubjective and self theories postulated a different crux from sexuality and that ‘non-neurotic’ (‘difficult’) cases pointed to factors other than sexual conflicts regarding what had gone wrong and what had to be cured.

The complexity of the implied attitudes mentioned above found prompt expression in Homer Curtis's address, which mentioned highly charged reactions to the Congress theme. Curtis mentioned the embarrassment of analysts in relating to sexual phenomena in clinical work and in case discussions. Later, commenting on Spruiell's presentation, Curtis stated the centrality and non-eradicable eternity of the drives and the unconscious in psychoanalytic theory, and indicated the need not to lose sight of the other drive, aggression, the counterpart to sexuality.

Spruiell also took up the theme of resistance to sexuality and used it as a metaphor for resistance to psychoanalysis in general. Spruiell's survey of the evolution of Freud's and of later theories about sexuality focused on the ambivalence endemic in the theme of sexuality, mentioning Freud's anticipationof a possible negative reception of his Three Essays (1905). Spruiell noted Freud's deepening insights about sexuality, the numerous references to it, in the course of his theoretical developments of its manifestations. There is no single, universally accepted theory of sexuality, but this does not preclude the profound importance of the experience of sexuality, which could militate against the tendency to discard the uniquely psychoanalytic theorisation of sexuality. Spruiell offered a view of sexuality seen through the prism of exchanges and displacements, even renunciation, as well as shame, aggression and castration anxiety, that accompany both psychoanalytic thinking on sexuality and sexuality itself (1997, pp. 360-1).

responseSet.title: Transvestism and Transsexualism: Chaired by Marianne Leuzinger-Bohleber, Frankfurt
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Transvestism and Transsexualism

Chaired by Marianne Leuzinger-Bohleber, Frankfurt

Colette Chiland

Colette Chiland

31 rue Censier
75005 Paris

Colette Chiland  

The Chair opened the session with a few words in memoriam of Agnès Oppenheimer (Paris), who died suddenly on 5 January 1997. A dedicated psychoanalyst, she was to have chaired this session. She was the author of a book on Stoller's works, entitled Le choix du sexe: à propos des théries de Stoller (Paris: Presses Univ. France, 1980), as well as many papers on transsexualism. Her most recent work was a book on Kohut, Kohut et la psychologie du self (Paris: Presses Univ. France, 1996).

Marianne Leuzinger-Bohleber said that all three panellists would address themselves to transvestism, not to transsexualism. Therefore she opened with the following few words on transsexualism as Robert Stoller had understood it, asking the reporter to complete the description after the presentations.

Stoller described true male transsexualism as a clear-cut entity with a rather precise clinical picture, both in its dynamics and in its aetiology. Such a boy is raised in a blissful symbiosis by a mother, who herself has a problem of gender identity; wanting to be a boy, she decides at puberty to live as a woman, but make her boy her feminised phallus. The boy has a primary feminine identity and cannot later disidentify from the mother.

After sex reassignment, transsexuals are often reported as functioning better, and that raises an important issue for psychoanalysts. But the data are not all in agreement as showed by Pfäfflin (1993).

The first panellist, Mervin Glasser, presented the analysis (five times a week) of a transvestite, Mr W. The analysis took place some years ago and was discussed in a previous paper (1979). He began with a few remarks on the core complex that influences the total structure; in transvestism, as in all perversions, there is a libidinisation of the intense aggression of the core complex, the object relations are always fundamentally sado-masochistic, at the cost of precluding true intimacy.

The patient dressed up as a woman, paying attention to the minutest details, and would then masturbate, while looking at himself in the mirror in such a way that his penis was not visible in the mirror. He felt sexual pleasure; he felt calm and relaxed.

Arthur Penn's Night Moves: A Film That Interprets Us

Emanuel Berman

Emanuel Berman

Haifa 31905
Israel
Fax 972-4-8240966
RSPS360@UVM.HAIFA.AC.IL

Emanuel Berman  

Night Moves: 1975

Director: Arthur Penn

Scriptwriter: Alan Sharp

Video distributor: Warner Home Video

‘So it's really just a series of concentric circles. The outer reality just goes out and out in those circles; the inner reality, and the inner detective story, is there to be examined—if he would examine it.’ Arthur Penn (in Gallagher, 1975, p. 88).

Having been interested in the detective's search as a metaphor for the psychoanalyst's quest, I found myself drawn to Night Moves ever since I first saw it. I offer the following interpretive viewing with the assumption that it represents neither an objective deciphering of the film's ‘true’ meaning, nor solely a projection of my inner world, but rather a new significance that has emerged in the transitional space opened up by my intense personal and transferential encounter with Night Moves (Berman, 1997, 1998).

While my earlier thinking focused on the way I could interpret the film, I recently became more cognisant of interpretation as one of the themes in the film itself (as often in drama; Simon, 1985), and of the film in toto as an attempted interpretation (‘The mystery is inward, and perhaps the solution is inward’; Penn, in Gallagher, 1975, p. 87). Contemporary art has absorbed (sometimes ambivalently) psychoanalytic interpretation, both as a topic and as a tool.

Ellen: ‘Who is winning?’

Harry: ‘Nobody. One side is losing slower than the other.’

The film's plot interweaves and juxtaposes two equally important stories: detective Harry Mosby's attempt to decipher the disappearance, and later the death, of an adolescent girl, Delly Grastner; and his struggle to save his disintegrating marriage to Ellen. At the background of both stories stands a common emotional theme: failed parenthood, and its sequela—the helpless yearning, despair and rage of the abandoned child.

Harry (Gene Hackman) was abandoned by his parents as a child, and brought up by relatives. We hear this story in the context of a renewed abandonment: his wife's infidelity. Harry discovers the affair in which Ellen (Susan Clark) is involved, but is unable and unwilling to speak to her. Instead he violently invades the house of Marty, Ellen's disabled lover. Marty (Harris Yulin) defends himself by interpreting: ‘I am beginning to get you in focus, Mosby. Ellen talks a lot about you, how you were left by your parents when you were very young … It's a clue, isn't that what you do, look for clues? Didn't you track down your parents? I am sure you were trailing Ellen when you saw us’.

responseSet.title: Illusion and Uncertainty in Psychoanalytic Writing
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Illusion and Uncertainty in Psychoanalytic Writing

Joyce Slochower

Joyce Slochower

15 West 75th Street
New York
NY 10023

Joyce Slochower  

Psychoanalytic writing may be viewed as a particular version of creative process. As such, it evokes many of the self and object-related anxieties and conflicts associated with creative expression. This paper considers the role of illusion in facilitating an entry into the psychoanalytic writing process in the light of contemporary relational and feminist perspectives. It is suggested that the writer who struggles to write may engage certain real or internalised object experiences to retreat to a transitional idealised self-state and to enter the writing process. Two variants of this subjective state are described, based on Winnicott's notion of ‘being’ and ‘doing’ as underlying dimensions of human experience. Each self-state excludes a particular set of self- and object-related anxieties and creates a temporary illusion of creative certainty. That illusion helps the writer temporarily to avoid self-doubt and anxiety in order to enter the creative arena Ultimately, however, the protected writing space must expand if the writer is to address and integrate the world of psychoanalytic ideas within the writer's own creative process. This process is illustrated by a description of the evolution of my own experience as a psychoanalytic writer.

responseSet.title: Bilaterality: Hemispheric Specialisation and Integration
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Bilaterality: Hemispheric Specialisation and Integration

Regina Pally

Regina Pally

11980 San Vicente Blvd., Suite 810
Los Angeles
CA 90049

Regina Pally  

Introduction

The two cerebral hemispheres are identical in appearance. However, ever since Broca discovered in 1861 that left hemisphere damage results in ‘expressive’ aphasia, neuroscientists have realised that the brain is functionally asymmetrical (Ornstein, 1997). A few years later, Wernicke identified that left hemisphere damage leads to ‘receptive’ aphasia. As a result, neuroscientists held the belief that the left hemisphere was superior to the right because it was the language centre, and thus the seat of reason and intellect. Eventually, however, Hughlings-Jackson, in the 1870s, realised that the right hemisphere plays a central role in comprehension of the world together with the left. During this same period, many neuroscientists were influenced by popular notions regarding the essential nature of man, such as those depicted in Robert Louis Stevenson's 1886 story of Dr Jekyll and Mr Hyde. For these neuroscientists, it was as if humans had two separate minds, even two ‘consciousnesses’, a ‘cultivated’ left one and a ‘primitive’ right one.

In this century, in the 1930s, Lange revitalised interest in the central importance of the right hemisphere. He recognised that the right hemisphere provides a general contextual background to a person's world view. During the 1960s the initial work of Sperry and Gazzaniga (Gazzaniga et al., 1962) at first seemed to corroborate the ‘two minds, two consciousnesses’ view held by some late nineteenth-century scientists. Subsequent work, however, resulted in the now generally accepted ‘asymmetric but integrated’ perspective. The right and left cerebral cortex are each lateralised for specialised functions, but in the healthy brain the two hemispheres share their information and operate collaboratively (Joseph, 1996; Deacon, 1997; Gazzaniga, 1995; Ornstein, 1997).

Research regarding lateralisation of specialised functions pertains mostly to cortical functions. However it must be remembered that specialised functions of each cortex are influenced by input from subcortical structures, as well as from other cortical sites in the same or opposite hemisphere. According to Deacon, functional asymmetry evolved because in certain circumstances organisms must prioritise, and have only one goal, express only one action, or speak only one thought. For these particular functions only one hemisphere must have the advantage.

responseSet.title: Should We or Shouldn't We? Some Aspects of the Confidentiality of Clinical Reporting and Dossier Access
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Should We or Shouldn't We? Some Aspects of the Confidentiality of Clinical Reporting and Dossier Access

Allannah Furlong

Allannah Furlong

5515 Queen Mary 302
Montreal
Quebec H3X IV4

Allannah Furlong  

In this paper, reservations are expressed about two deviations from analytic neutrality: when the analyst seeks the patient's permission for publication or presentation of clinical material and when the analyst allows the patient access to the dossier under access-of-information legislation. In the first case, concern centres mainly on the entanglement of the patient in the therapist's sanctioned version of their work, an entanglement that might inhibit future revisions of the patient's self-understanding. In the second case, the analytic mental space, symbolised by the dossier, is viewed as neither uniquely the analyst's nor the patient's, a complex dialectical chamber the privacy of which must be respected, even by the patient whose discourse contributes to it, in order for it to function effectively. Transparency and accountability in the analytic context reveal a paradox that is not exclusive to it: the possibility of full disclosure runs counter to the expression of subjective truth. In a clinical example, curiosity about the dossier is seen to have been a new version of an earlier thwarted questioning about origins and identity. A specific deficiency in the therapist's understanding may have contributed to the patient's enactment.

responseSet.title: Unconscious Logic: An Introduction to Matte Blanco's Bi-logic and Its Uses.  By Eric Rayner.: London: Routledge.  1995.  Pp. 180.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Unconscious Logic: An Introduction to Matte Blanco's Bi-logic and Its Uses. By Eric Rayner.

London: Routledge. 1995. Pp. 180.

John Keene

John Keene

134 Marshalswick Lane
St. Albans, Hertfordshire AL1 4XB

John Keene  

Many readers who have been intrigued over the years by the titles of Matte Blanco's major works The Unconscious as Infinite Sets: An Essay in BiLogic (1975, London: Duckworth) and Thinking, Feeling and Being (1988, London and New York: Routledge) may have been put off by Matte Blanco's concern to write books that would be equally sophisticated and rigorous for the mathematician and logician as for the psychoanalyst. The problem was recognised in the preparation of the second book, in which Eric Rayner contributed to a helpful introduction to the main arguments of The Unconscious as Infinite Sets. New readers' need for a helping hand is part of the inspiration for this book, though by no means the sum of its ambitions. The key underlying aim is for readers to appreciate Rayner's thesis that Matte Blanco's formalising of the logical structures underlying the Freudian unconscious offers psychoanalytic theory the best chance of reintegrating itself with mainstream science, as well as of giving psychoanalysis some new and clinically useful hypotheses. However, Rayner identifies a paradox in his opening chapter—‘a few varied and more or less sane intellectuals, psychoanalysts and others find bi-logic illuminating and useful; but many more, even if they have heard of it, are baffled and probably bored’ (p. 2). This book aims to find a bridge between the two positions and to discuss the logical aspects as far as possible in ordinary language.

responseSet.title: Sexuality in the age of AIDS
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Sexuality in the age of AIDS

Mark Blechner

Mark Blechner

145 Central Park West
New York
NY 10023

Mark Blechner  

Dear Sir,

It was painful to read the account of the panel on ‘Sexuality in the age of AIDS’ (Otero & Escardó, 1998). The panel was evidence of multiple scotomata among the participating psychoanalysts. There was ignorance of the psychoanalytic literature on AIDS and HIV (e.g. Isay, 1989; Aronson, 1996; Blechner, 1993a, 1997), none of which was mentioned. The panel participants tended to ascribe the illness to the other, the ‘not-me’ and to describe the purported psychopathology of those with the illness. When AIDS first appeared, when there was a lack of knowledge and much inchoate fear, this pattern of disavowal was common, but it is not acceptable among professionals fifteen years into the epidemic. As further evidence of disavowal and denial, the panel made no mention of psychoanalysts who have died of AIDS and of the many relatives and friends of psychoanalysts who have been lost to the illness. In addition, the obsessive focus on homosexuals at this panel was misguided. Internationally, more cases of HIV infection are spread through heterosexual than homosexual contact, and in the United States, heterosexual women are the group with the fastest rise in the rate of HIV infection (HIV Center, 1991).

While the problem of poor relations between gay men and lesbians with psychoanalysis was mentioned, there seemed to be little self-awareness of the homophobia and anti-homosexual hatred among the participants in the panel. Rather than focus on the purported psychotic aspects of homosexuals, as did some of the panel members, the next Congress of the International Psychoanalytical Association ought to have a panel that addresses the problem of bigotry among psychoanalysts—the destructive and often delusional fear and hatred of gay men and lesbians. There is now a substantial amount of psychoanalytic literature on this problem (e.g. Schafer, 1995; Schaffner, 1995; Magee & Miller, 1997; Frommer, 1994; Blechner, 1993b; Moss, 1992; Mitchell, 1981) although none of it was cited by this panel.

responseSet.title: Perversion: Chaired by Friedemann Pfäfflin, Ulm
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Perversion

Chaired by Friedemann Pfäfflin, Ulm

Rosalind Leigh

Rosalind Leigh

Psychotherapy Services, A14 Archway Wing
Whittington Hospital
London N19 5NF

Rosalind Leigh  

The panel was introduced by the chairman, Dr Friedemann Pfäfflin. He noted that psychoanalysts spend a large proportion of their time dealing with material in their patients' dreams and phantasies that can be thought of as perverse. However, once phantasies spill over into overt deviant behaviour the patient may be more likely to end up in prison rather than on the analyst's couch. Although the analytic theory of psychosexual development was in part founded on the exploration of perverse behaviour, very few analysts have patients with manifest perversions in treatment.

Pfäfflin spoke about the way in which the term ‘perversion’ has been dropped from the psychiatric classification of disorders as it was felt to be too moralistic. However, he says, the term ‘paraphilia’, which has been substituted, still refers to deviation from norms. Colloquial language retains the stigmatising and disapproving implication of the term ‘perversion’. Freud, however, took the word and attempted to transform it into a psychoanalytic technical term. The polymorphously perverse innate predisposition (Anlage) is the foundation of all normal or abnormal psychosexual development.

He went on briefly to summarise different theoretical conceptualisations of perversion. Initially it was seen as a leftover of untransformed partial drives. Later it was thought of as a defence against castration anxiety, then as a defence against pre-oedipal anxieties and affects. He pointed out that perversions are to be found on all structural levels, and are not determined simply by specific behaviour but rather by the inner object relationship. He queried whether we should distinguish between perversion demonstrated in manifest sexual behaviour and perverse object relations. He also mentioned the perennial unresolved question of what are the factors that give rise to enacted perverse behaviour rather than to a particular neurotic symptom. Should perversion be thought of as the result of a structural, for example superego, deficit or is it an attempt to fill in a deficit or a gap in the self? Pfäfflin then proceeded to introduce the panellists.

responseSet.title: ‘Killing the Angel in the House’: Creativity, Femininity and Aggression
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

‘Killing the Angel in the House’: Creativity, Femininity and Aggression

Rozsika Parker

Rozsika Parker

17 Archibald Road
London N7 0AN

Rozsika Parker  

The author brings to bear an art historical perspective on the psychoanalytic understanding of creativity as an object relationship, proposing that the creative endeavour is determined by a wider, more complex network of internal and external object relationships than is usually assumed. The workings of tradition, language, contemporary practices, methods and materials are explored. Creative block is considered in the context of the determining relationships, with particular reference to the role of aggression. The position of the latter within psychoanalytic theories of creativity is surveyed and it is proposed that aggression has a pivotal place not primarily in instituting sublimation, reparation or reaction formation but simply because the processes of creativity demand it. Virginia Woolf's image of ‘Killing the Angel in the House’ is analysed and used to track the implications of gender, focusing on the concept of the muse. It is pointed out that traditionally, the fear, guilt and anxiety associated with aggressive creativity has been mediated by the muse, which is compared to the internal good object. Drawing on art history, artists' statements, and clinical material, the author illustrates the disparate means by which the presence of ‘muse’ can be internalised to infuse the relationships that constitute creativity.
