responseSet.title: ‘The Analyst's Subjectivity and the Analyst's Objectivity’ by Owen Renik (Including the Response by Marcia Cavell)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

‘The Analyst's Subjectivity and the Analyst's Objectivity’ by Owen Renik (Including the Response by Marcia Cavell)

Paul Williams

Paul Williams

15 Arden Road
London N3 3AB

Paul Williams  

This paper and discussion were put on the web site on 8 June 1998 and were replaced by the paper by Esman on 20 July. Marcia Cavell's written response to Renik is summarised below (albeit in compressed form) as it influenced subsequent discussants. Her discussion is published in full in this edition of the International Journal. First, a brief summary of Renik's paper. Renik argues that the recent emphasis on intersubjectivity has sometimes been misunderstood to involve the impossibility of achieving objectivity in clinical psychoanalysis. This is not so, he argues, and to support this he offers a pragmatic definition of analytic objectivity alongside the analyst's ‘irreducible subjectivity’. It is only when the analyst takes into account the absoluteness of subjectivity that any objectivity is possible.

He moves straight to a clinical case, that of Ethan, a 31-year-old physician, who has been a patient of Renik's for one year. Ethan idealises Renik whom he sees as a tolerant father. Renik is aware of the idealisation and the eventual likelihood of Ethan's discovery of Renik's clay feet. He discusses in detail a session in which Ethan describes how he had managed to save a young woman brought into the accident and emergency department of Ethan's hospital. Renik is impressed by his patient's conscientiousness and notes that Ethan does not seem proud of his efforts. He plans to bring this up. Ethan surprises Renik by saying that he thinks Renik's attention has strayed, but then carries on. Renik eventually has the opportunity to interpret Ethan's anxiety about Renik's reaction to him voicing his success. The session proceeds and this anxiety about success is connected by Ethan to his somewhat authoritarian father and the possibility that Renik might be competitive, even if he didn't show it. Renik reflects on the exchanges and at a certain point recalls that he had earlier turned his head to check his answering machine, as he had been expecting a call from a friend. His attention had been elsewhere, for a moment, and Ethan had probably noticed that Renik's voice had momentarily come from a different direction. Renik then reports his own, extensive associations to Ethan's material regarding the emergency room incident, which had contributed to the distraction. These associations concerned his reactions to his sick and dying mother. At an appropriate time in the session, he confides in Ethan that he had been distracted by the answering machine. Ethan's complex, positive reaction is described.

responseSet.title: Some Jungian and Freudian Perspectives on the Oedipus Myth and Beyond
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Some Jungian and Freudian Perspectives on the Oedipus Myth and Beyond

James Astor

James Astor

6 Ladbroke Square
London W11 3LX

James Astor  

The author traces some of the differences and similarities between those psychoanalysts and analytical psychologists who met in London to discuss contemporary attitudes to the oedipal situation. The conference provided an opportunity to consider briefly whether the characterisation of Jung as an apostate was correct or whether this was a consequence of the political need, following the separation of Jung and Freud, to protect psychoanalysis from professional criticism until it had become sufficiently well-established to be able to tolerate dissenting opinion. The author considers the misjudgements of history, why psychoanalysts do not read Jung, and some of the differences between the conceptual foundations of psychoanalysis and those of analytical psychology. After setting out some of the directions in which Jungian thought has been moving, particularly in respect of the work of Fordham, Klein and Bion, the author compares current clinical approaches as exemplified by established practitioners and includes parts of the discussion between members of the two associations. A brief indication of what the papers contained is also included.

responseSet.title: Sexuality in the Age of Aids: Chaired by Rosine Debray, Paris
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Sexuality in the Age of Aids

Chaired by Rosine Debray, Paris

Francisco Otero and Adela Escardo

Adela Escardo

Los Andes 145
Lima 27

Adela Escardo  

The Chairman opened the panel by questioning whether there is significant progress today in the way psychoanalysts deal with hiv-aids patients, and whether there are changes in the way we understand this illness and its evolution from a theoretical point of view.

She noted that psychoanalytic knowledge of hiv-aids patients has increased since the epidemic was discovered fifteen years ago. Experience at the Psychosomatic Hospital in Paris, the Hospital Pierre Marty, shows that if major differences do exist in hiv-aids patients, they are linked to the varying characteristics of their psychosomatic structure and mainly to the characteristics of their psychic organisation. Homosexual patients appear to be very different from each another based on these distinctions. The disease still poses a shock both for patient and analyst, but the fear of imminent death is no longer so present as medical treatment in western countries has led to an increased hope of life expectancy.

The second point Debray made was a problem that concerned the National aids Association for Research (ANRS) in France over non-compliance in the medical treatment of hiv-aids patients who are under tri-therapies. In her view this association has until now worked very much against a psychological or psychoanalytical approach. They feared possible revelations about homosexual characteristics that could lead to conflicts with the homosexual community. By saying this she was really demonstrating the very strong and at times hostile feelings that characterise the relationship between the homosexual and the psychoanalytic community. After these opening words Debray called on Peter Hildebrand to address the audience.

In his presentation, entitled ‘Sexuality in the age of aids', Hildebrand started by offering the general framework of freedom of expression regarding sexuality in which aids had sprung in the West. He emphasised the decriminalisation of homosexuality and the opportunity to declare and to act out one's sexual desires and fantasies within their own context. The simultaneous emergence of homosexual groups as a political entity proclaiming that their sexual preferences were normative, and the hiv virus, created the conditions for conflict and the stigmatisation of the ‘gay’ individual together with a resentful response. He observed that much of the responsibility was ascribed to psychoanalysis since homosexual groups regard psychoanalysts, ‘with some justice’, as bigoted and homophobic—a far cry from Freud's letter to the mother of a homosexual son, reassuring her that he did not regard homosexuality as pathological (Jones, 1980, pp. 208-9).
